amsterdam:en: Amsterdam plays its hand on spring light and summer. June-July are my favourite months (8.0/10): 13-21°C, 41-47% rain in showers, 16h days, terraces by the canals until 23h. From April to May, it's tulip season: Keukenhof opens for 8 weeks (mid-March to mid-May), Bloemencorso on the 3rd Saturday of April, massive crowds. From December to February, expect 2-8°C and 39-50% rain: often grey skies, light fading at 17h. Christmas markets season and freezing canals (rare but photogenic). Amsterdammers prefer these months for museums (Rijksmuseum, Van Gogh Museum, Anne Frank House) with shorter waits. Anne Frank House booking required up to 6 weeks ahead. May-June and September stay the best crowd/weather compromises. Bikes everywhere: 800,000 bikes for 850,000 inhabitants. The cannabis culture in coffeeshops remains legally hazy but tolerated.
berlin:en: Berlin lives its best moments when the weather's good: the city is built for the outdoors (parks, terraces, nearby lakes like Wannsee). June-August are my favourite months (8.5-8.6/10): 14-24°C, 27-37% rain, days stretching to 22h, biergartens packed, techno club nights (Berghain, Watergate). From December to February, expect -1/6°C and 39-43% rain: often oppressively grey skies, days down to 8h of light. Christmas markets (Gendarmenmarkt, Weihnachtsmarkt am Roten Rathaus) partially redeem this. Winter = museum season: Museum Island (Pergamon under renovation until 2027), Holocaust Memorial, former Tempelhof, Stasi Museum. Reichstag booking required via the Bundestag (free). Berlin remains one of Europe's cheapest capitals: hostels at 25€, Turkish/Asian restaurants at 8-12€. May and September are excellent crowd/weather compromises.
prague:en: Prague keeps its medieval charm intact: Charles Bridge, Castle, UNESCO old town, but overtourism has settled in. June-August are my favourite months (8.5-8.6/10): 14-26°C, 34-38% rain, terraces packed at Letná, festivals (Bohemia Jazz Fest, Signal Festival of light art in October). From December to February, expect -1/7°C and 33-37% rain: dry cold, Christmas markets at Old Town Square and Wenceslas Square. Charles Bridge becomes walkable again (in midsummer it overflows). Letná district for the panoramic view and local biergartens, Vinohrady for trendy restaurants, Žižkov for alternative nightlife. Beer at 1.50-2€ everywhere, traditional meals (svíčková, guláš) at 8-12€. May and September-October are the best crowd/weather compromises. Direct flight from Paris in 1h45.
vienne:en: Vienna is the imperial capital par excellence: Habsburgs, opera, Viennese coffee, waltz. July-August are my favourite months (8.8/10): 17-27°C, 30-34% rain, music festival at the Theater an der Wien, opera closed in July-August (season resumes in September). From December to February, expect -2/8°C and 29-33% rain: dry cold, major Christmas markets (Rathausplatz, Schönbrunn, Belvedere), Opera Ball season opening late February. Schönbrunn and Belvedere (Klimt, The Kiss) remain the must-sees, online tickets recommended (queues reduced). Try a Sachertorte at Demel or Sacher to compare the historic rivalry. The Naschmarkt Saturday morning for Ottoman cuisine and antiques. Trams 1 and 2 loop around the Ring (imperial boulevard) for 2.40€. May-June and September-October are the real sweet spots tourism/prices.
lisbonne:en: Lisbon has exploded touristically since 2015: Brexit, expats, digital nomads, cruises. The city keeps its laid-back feel despite soaring rents. July-August are my favourite months (9.7/10): 18-28°C, 2-3% rain, terraces in the evening until midnight. From December to March, expect 9-17°C and 25-36% rain: Atlantic, damp wind, but often clear skies between showers. This is when the real fado houses come alive in Alfama (the commercial troupes run all year). Tram 28 stays the most famous but packed in season: prefer the 15 (Belém) or the 18 (less touristy). Cascais and Sintra 30 minutes by train. Sintra-Pena has become a classic: go on weekdays and early (booking required). The pastéis de nata scene goes beyond Belém: Manteigaria (Chiado) stays my favourite. May-June and September-October are the best crowd/weather compromises.
budapest:en: Budapest is Buda and Pest separated by the Danube: 2 cities in 1, baroque/Art Nouveau, thermal baths. June-August are my favourite months (8.9-9.1/10): 17-29°C, 26-39% rain, swimming at the Széchenyi Baths (outdoor pools open year-round at 38°C), festivals (Sziget early August, one of Europe's biggest). From December to February, expect -2/8°C and 26-32% rain: dry cold, Christmas markets at Vörösmarty Square, and especially the Gellért and Széchenyi baths under the snow (steam in the cold, a striking experience). The Chain Bridge reopened in 2023 after 4 years of renovation. Goulash at Centrál Kávéház, Tokaji in a borbár, Ruin pubs in the Jewish quarter (Szimpla Kert the most famous). Budapest remains one of Europe's cheapest capitals: traditional meals 8-15€, 4-star hotels 60-100€/night.
tokyo:en: Tokyo is the ultimate megalopolis: 37 million inhabitants in the urban area, but surprisingly orderly and clean. May is my favourite month (8.0/10): 15-23°C, 39% rain, end of azalea blooms (Nezu Shrine), before the monsoon. From June to mid-July, it's the tsuyu (rainy season): 49-51% rainfall, hydrangeas in bloom (Meigetsu-in in Kamakura). July-August are scorching (25-32°C, oppressive humidity), matsuri (festival) season. September-October are the best months: temperatures dropping back, clear skies, momiji (autumn colours) starting in November in the Kanto region. Mid-March to early April = cherry blossom (sakura), absolute tourist peak, hotel prices x3-x5. Districts to explore: Shibuya (Crossing), Harajuku (Takeshita Street), Shinjuku (Golden Gai for micro-bars), Asakusa (Senso-ji), Akihabara (otaku), Tsukiji-Toyosu for the fish market. Try a ramen at Ichiran or Afuri, conveyor belt sushi, Robot Restaurant (unapologetic kitsch). JR Pass less and less worthwhile since 2023.
new-york:en: New York is the ultimate urban experience: 8.5 million inhabitants across Manhattan-Bronx-Brooklyn-Queens-Staten Island. June stays my favourite month (8.6/10): 17-26°C, 36% rain, parks in full swing (Central Park, Prospect Park), terraces, festivals (SummerStage, Shakespeare in the Park). July-August are hot and humid (20-30°C, occasional heatwaves at 35°C+), New Yorkers fleeing to the Hamptons on weekends. From December to February, expect -3/6°C and 31-35% rain/snow: biting cold during blizzards, but luminous Christmas (Rockefeller Center tree, Saks and Bergdorf window displays). September-October are my real sweet spots: 11-25°C, clear skies, indian summer, Central Park foliage late October. Highlights: Times Square, Empire State, Top of the Rock, Brooklyn Bridge on foot, MoMA, Met, Statue of Liberty + Ellis Island, 9/11 Memorial. Districts: SoHo, West Village, Williamsburg (Brooklyn), Lower East Side. Try bagel at Russ & Daughters, pizza at Joe's or Roberta's, deli at Katz's. Subway 24/7. Avoid taxis except from airports (Uber/Lyft cheaper).
los-angeles:en: Los Angeles is Mediterranean stability over 12 months: no truly tough season, but important nuances. June is my favourite month (9.7/10): 15-28°C, 2% rain, and the "June Gloom" fog finally lifts. From July to September, expect 17-32°C with autumn Santa Ana winds fanning fires in the hills (fire season July to October). Smog can be uncomfortable when thermal inversion traps the air. From December to March, it's the Californian "rainy season": 17-27% rain, but still 8-20°C, mild winters. The Pacific Coast Highway towards Malibu stays viable, landslides may close Mulholland after major events. April-May and October-November are excellent transitions: fewer people on the beaches, pleasant terraces, often clear skies after the dying rains.
dubai:en: Dubai is urban extravagance in the heart of the desert: 3.5 million inhabitants (90% expats), Burj Khalifa (828m, the world's tallest building), Palm Jumeirah, air-conditioned malls. March is my favourite month (9.1/10): 19-28°C, 7% rain, sea at 24°C, ideal temperatures before the heatwave. From June to September drop to 2.2-3.5/10 due to extreme heat (26-43°C, sometimes 50°C, max humidity along the Gulf): everything happens in air-conditioned interiors, beaches deserted in the day, hotel prices reduced by 50%. From November to April, it's the high season: 17-32°C, prices x3, 5-star+ hotels fully booked. Highlights: Burj Khalifa (At The Top 124th/148th floor, or 152nd/154th more expensive, book 2 weeks ahead), Dubai Mall (1200 shops, aquarium, musical fountains), Old Dubai (Bastakia historic quarter, abra crossing the Creek for 1 dirham), Palm Jumeirah (Atlantis, monorail), Burj Al Arab (the only "7-star" hotel), Gold Souk and Spice Souk in Deira. Desert excursions: Dune bashing, Bedouin dinner, sandboarding (200-400 AED). Try shawarma, machbous, Medjool dates, Arabic coffee with cardamom. Direct flight Paris in 7h.
singapour:en: Singapore is the ultra-modern city-state par excellence: 6 million inhabitants, 100% urban, but 47% green space. Equatorial climate = it's hot and humid ALL year (constant 24-30°C). February is my favourite month (6.9/10): 24-30°C, 68% rain, start of the north-east dry monsoon. From April to May and from November to January, it's monsoon season: 80-93% rainfall in brief but intense showers (often late afternoon). Singapore never tops 6.9/10: too humid a climate to reach excellent. Highlights: Gardens by the Bay (Supertrees, air-conditioned domes), Marina Bay Sands (pool 200m up, paid entry at the top), Sentosa (island park), Universal Studios. Hawker centres for cuisine (Maxwell, Lau Pa Sat, Newton): chicken rice, chili crab, laksa, char kway teow at 4-7$. Districts: Chinatown, Little India, Kampong Glam (Arab). Very expensive outside hawkers: restaurant meal 30-60$, 4-star hotel 200-400$. Direct flight from Paris in 13h.
reykjavik:en: Reykjavik lives two opposing worlds. July and August are the only truly viable months: 9-13°C, midnight sun that doesn't really set, southern fjords accessible, hikes until midnight. July remains my pick for landscapes: the F roads (highlands) open around the 15th, giving access to Landmannalaugar and Þórsmörk. Tourist peak at the same time: Golden Circle tours overflowing, 4×4 rentals climbing to 250€/day. From October to March, conditions become severe: days closing at 16h, 80 km/h winds, northern roads cut by snow. This is northern lights season: December-February, with their 18h nights, are the best months for aurora hunters, provided you accept near-total darkness. May-June offer a decent transition: increasing sunlight, prices still reasonable, but capricious weather.
kyoto:en: Kyoto is Japan's historic soul: 17 UNESCO sites, 1600 temples, geishas in Gion. May is my favourite month (8.0/10): 13-24°C, 39% rain, zen gardens at peak freshness, end of cherry blossoms at altitude (Kuramadera). From October to November, it's momiji (autumn colours): Tofuku-ji, Eikando, Kiyomizudera illuminated in the evening, peak mid-November. From June to mid-July, it's tsuyu (rainy season). July-August drop to 6.9/10 due to humid heatwave (23-32°C). March-April = sakura (cherry blossoms), absolute tourist peak, hotels x3-x5. Highlights: Kinkaku-ji (Golden Pavilion), Ryōan-ji (zen garden), Fushimi Inari (10,000 orange torii), Arashiyama (bamboo forest, but packed), Gion (geishas in the evening, do not harass them). Tofuku-ji and Tenryu-ji less crowded. Try a kaiseki ryori (multi-course traditional cuisine), a matcha at Tsujiri, a yuba at Nanzen-ji. Cycling easy (rental 1000¥/day). May and October are the real sweet spots tourism/weather.
madagascar:en: Madagascar, the world's 4th largest island: 28 million inhabitants on 587,000 km² off the coast in the Indian Ocean, separated from Africa for 165 million years = record endemism (90% of fauna and flora unique worldwide, 100+ lemur species). Capital Antananarivo at 1300m altitude. September is my favourite month (9.0/10): 12-24°C, 6% rain, end of dry season, ideal temperatures on the highlands. From April to November, it's the ideal dry season for most of the country (except permanently wetter east coast): 10-27°C, 5-36% rainfall depending on region. From December to March, it's the rainy season and cyclones: 45-75% rainfall, real cyclone risk on the east and north coasts (Cyclone Batsirai 2022, Freddy 2023 caused 100+ deaths). Regions to know: Antananarivo ("Tana", capital, see dedicated review), Avenue of the Baobabs near Morondava (west coast, 6 Adansonia grandidieri 30m baobabs, Madagascar's signature, sunset signature photo), Andasibe-Mantadia National Park (rainforest 3h from Tana, observation of Indri Indri = the largest lemur 9.5 kg, morning call audible at 3 km), Ranomafana National Park (UNESCO 2007, another major lemur park with the "golden lemur"), Tsingy de Bemaraha (UNESCO, extremely sharp limestone formations in the heart of a national park, vertiginous hiking), Île Sainte-Marie (Nosy Boraha, east coast, former 17th-18th century pirate island, humpback whales July-September), Nosy Be in the north-west (main beach tourist island — see dedicated review), Nosy Iranja, Nosy Komba (paradise islands offshore), Antsirabe ("Madagascar's Vichy", thermal springs). Safety: Madagascar is broadly safe for tourism, vigilance in Tana and Toamasina (theft), red zone in the arid south-west "Triangle of Death" and some southern rural areas after dahalo (rural banditry). Try romazava (beef stew), ravitoto, mofo gasy (sweet morning fritter), THB (Three Horses Beer, local pride), Malagasy wine (Lazan'i Betsileo from the Ambalavao region), arranged rum (vanilla + fruits + clove).
machu-picchu:en: Machu Picchu, at 2400m altitude, is the world's most famous Inca archaeological site: 6000 visitors/day capped via timed tickets. July is my favourite month (7.9/10): 11-21°C, 34% rain, Peruvian dry season, generally clear skies. From June to August, it's the tourist peak: tickets to book 3-4 months ahead, prices around 50-200$ depending on the route (3 different routes since 2024). From December to March, it's the rainy season: 83-98% rainfall, frequent mist (can block the view, but also makes the site mysterious), Inca Trail closed all February. April-May and September-October are the real sweet spots: reduced crowds, still acceptable weather, prices slightly off-peak. Logistics: Lima-Cusco flight, Cusco-Aguas Calientes train (3h30 by PeruRail Vistadome or Inca Rail Premium, 60-200$ one way), Aguas Calientes-Machu Picchu bus (12$ return, 25 min). Plan a minimum 1 night in Aguas Calientes (Machupicchu Pueblo) for 6am entry. Huayna Picchu or Montaña Machu Picchu = additional climb (separate booking, limited capacity). Local guide mandatory since 2017 (50-150$/group).
rio-de-janeiro:en: Rio is one of the world's most spectacular cities: 6 million inhabitants between mountains (Pão de Açúcar, Corcovado) and sea (Copacabana, Ipanema, Leblon). September is my favourite month (8.6/10): 20-26°C, 28% rain, end of austral winter, ideal temperatures without stifling heat. From December to March, expect 23-30°C and 42-48% rain: austral summer, tourist peak, sea at 25°C, but also Carnival (4 days before Mardi Gras, variable dates February-March), prices x4-x5, hotels fully booked 6 months ahead. From May to August (austral winter), expect 18-25°C: cool morning temperatures, ideal for hikes (Pedra Bonita, Dois Irmãos). Highlights: Christ the Redeemer at Corcovado (cog train from Cosme Velho), Pão de Açúcar (2-section cable car), Escadaria Selarón (Lapa), Jardim Botânico, Niterói and its MAC (Niemeyer) by ferry. Districts: Copacabana (touristy), Ipanema (chic, more pleasant for swimming), Leblon (upmarket), Santa Teresa (artistic on the heights), Lapa (nightlife). Safety: favelas need a local guide, beaches OK during the day, vigilance at night. Try feijoada (Saturday tradition), churrasco, açaí bowl, caipirinha (born here).
val-d-isere:en: Val d'Isère, premium French ski resort: 1850m, 1600 permanent residents, founded in 1934 by ski pioneers Émile Allais and Charles Diebold. Linked to Tignes via l'Espace Killy (300 km of pistes, named after Jean-Claude Killy born here, triple Olympic champion 1968). August is my favourite month (7.7/10): 8-19°C, 42% rain in storms, alpine hiking, summer skiing on the Pisaillas glacier (mid-June to mid-July). From December to April, expect -10/6°C and 41-58% precipitation often as snow: prime ski season, one of the snowiest in the Alps (8m/year), Critérium de la Première Neige (Alpine World Cup) early December. Espace Killy pistes: 78 runs (300 km), 1900m vertical drop, 88 lifts, Grande Motte glacier 3450m on the Tignes side. Legendary pistes: Face de Bellevarde (1992 Albertville Olympics men's downhill, one of the world's hardest), OK Piste (giant slalom). Off-piste: Tour du Charvet, Vallon des Aiguilles, accessible with a guide. Crowd: technical skiers, plenty of Brits, more rustic than Courchevel but equally pricey. Architecture: village centre respects Savoyard style (stone, wood, slate chalets) after the concrete misstep of the 70s. Try tartiflette, fondue, croziflette, Savoie wines. Logistics: Val d'Isère 3h from Lyon by car (winding road), Paris-Bourg-Saint-Maurice train + 1h30 bus. Hotels 200-1500€/night in peak season.
megeve:en: Megève, in Haute-Savoie: 3000 inhabitants at 1100m, founded in 1916 by Noémie de Rothschild as the French alternative to Saint-Moritz, village-resort with preserved charm (concrete tower blocks banned, traditional Savoyard chalet architecture strictly enforced). August is my favourite month (8.1/10): 11-23°C, 45% rain in mountain storms, alpine hiking, 18-hole golf, opera and baroque music festival. From June to September, expect 4-23°C: active summer season. From December to March, expect -5/8°C and 42-46% precipitation often as snow: prime ski season. Pistes: 445 km Évasion Mont-Blanc (Megève + Saint-Gervais + Combloux + La Giettaz + Praz-sur-Arly), modest altitude for Haute-Savoie (1100-2350m, so snow cover sometimes patchy before Christmas and after Easter). Mont d'Arbois, Côte 2000, Rochebrune (3 separate sectors, free shuttles). Megève is less technical than Val d'Isère or Tignes, gentler and more family-oriented, upmarket class. Crowd: French and Belgian aristocracy, well-off families, more discreet than Courchevel. Hotels: Les Fermes de Marie (Sibuet, 5* relais & châteaux), Four Seasons Megève (5*+ opened 2017), Les Loges Blanches, Mont-Blanc (historic palace), 250-2500€/night in peak season. Try tartiflette, Savoyard fondue, raclette, diots, génépi, Savoie wines. Logistics: Megève 1h15 from Geneva by car (Geneva airport is the fastest access for Haute-Savoie from Paris).
morzine:en: Morzine, in the Portes du Soleil: 3000 inhabitants at 1000m, one of France's largest village resorts (wooden chalet architecture, no concrete blocks), linked to Avoriaz by cable car. August is my favourite month (8.2/10): 13-23°C, 40% rain, hiking and MTB (Morzine = France's downhill MTB capital with Crankworx). From June to September, expect 5-23°C: very active summer season (one of the most dynamic Alpine resorts in summer), Pass'Portes du Soleil MTB in June with 600 km of trails. From December to March, expect -4/6°C and 42-47% precipitation often as snow: prime ski season. Pistes: 600 km Portes du Soleil (12 linked resorts including Morzine + Avoriaz + Châtel on the French side, Champéry + Champoussin + Les Crosets on the Swiss side), single ski pass, one of the world's largest ski areas. Pleney and Nyon-Pointe de Nyon = 2 main slopes at Morzine, accessible by cable car from the village centre. Avoriaz 15 min by cable car (village-resort built in wood in 1966 by Jacques Labro, car-free, horse-drawn shuttles). Crowd: very many Brits (40% of winter clientele), families, snowboarders. More accessible than Megève price-wise. Hotels: Hôtel Le Tremplin, La Bergerie, Mountain Mavericks (British chalets) — 100-500€/night. Try tartiflette, raclette, fondue, diots aux pommes. Logistics: Morzine 1h15 from Geneva by car/taxi (tourist transfers common), 80 km from the Swiss border.
courchevel:en: Courchevel, premium French ski resort: 4 villages at altitude (Courchevel 1850 the chicest, 1650 Moriond, 1550 Village, 1300 Le Praz), part of the Trois Vallées (the world's largest ski area with 600 km of linked pistes: Courchevel + Méribel + Val Thorens). July is my favourite month (7.6/10): 8-19°C, 54% rain in mountain storms, hiking and MTB. From June to September, expect 4-19°C: summer season (but Courchevel is winter-first, many places shut). From December to April, expect -9/6°C and 40-67% precipitation often as snow: prime ski season. Courchevel 1850 = world capital of luxury skiing: 8 5*+ Palace hotels (Cheval Blanc, Les Airelles, K2 Palace), 8 Michelin-starred restaurants (a world record for a ski resort), Hermès, Louis Vuitton, Cartier boutiques, helicopters to Geneva. Russian and Saudi clientele dominant since 2000. Pistes: 150 km Courchevel alone, 600 km Trois Vallées, 1900m vertical drop, 102 lifts. Saulire (2700m, Mont Blanc panorama), descent to Méribel via the Dou des Lanches. Courchevel altiport = one of the world's most dangerous landing strips (525m, sloped), reserved for private jets. Try tartiflette, génépi, Savoyard cheeses (Beaufort, Reblochon). Logistics: Courchevel 2h from Lyon by car, Paris-Moûtiers train + 30 min bus or taxi. Very expensive: ski pass 75€/day, hotels 300-3000€/night in peak season.
meribel:en: Méribel, French ski resort at the heart of the Trois Vallées: 1700m, 1900 permanent residents, founded in 1938 by Briton Peter Lindsay (traditional Savoyard chalet architecture strictly enforced). Considered "the most traditional resort in the 3 Vallées". July is my favourite month (8.0/10): 10-21°C, 47% rain in storms, alpine hiking, MTB, golf (18 holes, one of Europe's finest at 1700m). From June to September, expect 6-21°C: quiet summer season (few tourists outside ski season). From December to April, expect -8/5°C and 39-66% precipitation often as snow: prime ski season. Trois Vallées pistes: 600 km in total, Méribel = 150 km of its own, ideal central position to swing over to Courchevel or Val Thorens in the same day. Saulire (2700m, natural border with Courchevel). Plattières snowpark (one of the best in the Alps). 1992 Albertville Olympics: Méribel hosted the women's hockey events (Olympic Ice Rink still in use). Crowd: well-off French and British families (Méribel has the largest British community in the French Alps with Val d'Isère). More upmarket than Val Thorens, less ostentatious than Courchevel. Hotels: Le Coucou, Le Mont Vallon, Allodis (4-5*, 200-1500€/night). Try diots aux pommes, beaufort, génépi. Logistics: Méribel 2h from Lyon, Paris-Moûtiers train + 35 min bus.
st-anton:en: St. Anton am Arlberg, Austrian ski resort: 2500 inhabitants at 1300m, considered the cradle of modern alpine skiing (Hannes Schneider founded the first modern ski school here in 1907, the "Arlberg" technique that revolutionised teaching). Linked to Lech-Zürs-Stuben via Ski Arlberg (305 km of linked pistes, the largest area in Austria). August is my favourite month (7.5/10): 12-21°C, 66% rain in mountain storms. From December to April, expect -6/7°C and 44-69% precipitation often as snow: prime ski season, one of the snowiest in Austria (8m/year), legendary après-ski (Krazy Kanguruh, MooserWirt = world-renowned bars, atmosphere until 22h after the slopes). Legendary pistes: Stuben Albona (north-facing shaded slope, well-preserved snow), Valluga (2811m, accessible by cable car for skiers with a guide only, legendary freeride). Off-piste: Backside, Maroitäli, Schindlerkar (guide mandatory). St. Christoph above St. Anton = near-deserted hamlet with Hospiz Hotel (historic 5*). Crowd: technical and athletic skiers (the saying goes "a red piste at St. Anton = a black anywhere else"), Brits, Germans. Less chic than Lech or Verbier, more party-oriented. Logistics: St. Anton 1h30 from Innsbruck by train, 2h from Zürich. Hotels 150-500€/night, ski pass 70€/day. Try Tiroler Gröstl, Käsespätzle, Kaiserschmarrn, schnaps.
kitzbuhel:en: Kitzbühel, Austrian ski resort: 8500 inhabitants at 760m (low altitude), still-intact colourful medieval village ("the pearl of the Alps"). World-renowned for the Hahnenkamm (the world's most dangerous World Cup downhill, mid-January). August is my favourite month (7.8/10): 13-24°C, 57% rain in storms, hiking, MTB, golf (4 courses including the 18-hole Eichenheim). From December to April, expect -6/9°C and 41-50% precipitation often as snow: prime ski season, but the low altitude means snow cover can be unreliable (snow cannons needed early season, glaciers nearby = Kitzsteinhorn 2-3 valleys away). Pistes: 233 km KitzSki (Kitzbühel-Kirchberg-Jochberg-Pass Thurn), 1500m vertical drop. Hahnenkamm (1665m, "Streif" downhill 3312m, 860m vertical, 145 km/h on the Mausefalle, ravages even the world's best downhillers, mid-January weekend). Legendary pistes: Streif (the famous one, classed black but open to skiers outside the event), Ganslern. Off-piste with a guide: Bichlalm, Kitzbüheler Horn. Medieval old town 16th century (one of the best preserved in the Alps), luxury boutiques, starred dining. Crowd: Austrian and German aristocracy, Brits. Try Wiener Schnitzel, Kaiserschmarrn, Apfelstrudel, glühwein. Logistics: Kitzbühel 1h30 from Innsbruck, 1h30 from Salzburg, direct station from Vienna. Hotels 150-1000€/night (Hotel zur Tenne, Gasthof Pension Eckart).
vail:en: Vail is the largest single-mountain ski resort in the United States: 2400 hectares, 7 back bowls (world-class off-piste valleys). Faux-Tyrolean village built in the 60s. July is my favourite month (8.5/10): 7-24°C, 41% rain in storms, good for hiking to Booth Falls or Vail Pass. From December to March, expect -12/4°C: cult ski season. Vail Pass can close during storms (I-70 highway to Denver). Prices among the highest in Colorado: walk-up day pass 200$+, hotels 500-1500$/night. The Epic pass from Vail Resorts gives access to 70+ resorts worldwide. From April to May and October to November, it's the off-season: resort half-shut. The village keeps cultural life going (Vail Dance Festival, Bravo!) in summer. Denver 2h30 by road via I-70 (often jammed Sunday afternoons in winter).
aspen:en: Aspen is the chicest ski resort in the United States: Colorado, jet-set, eye-watering prices (200-2000$/night), 4 mountains (Aspen Mountain, Snowmass, Buttermilk, Highlands). July is my favourite month (9.2/10): 8-26°C, 25% rain, the right moment for the Aspen Music Festival (2 months of opera and classical) and hiking to the Maroon Bells (Colorado's most photographed peaks). From December to March, expect -13/2°C: cult ski season. Winter X Games at Buttermilk late January (free entry, but huge crowds). Aspen Highlands hosts Highland Bowl, one of North America's toughest hike-to-ski bowls. From April to May and November, it's the off-season: everything shuts, ghost town. The culinary scene rivals NY/SF (Element 47, Matsuhisa). John Denver lived here. Direct flight to Aspen-Pitkin Airport but turbulent in winter. Alternative Denver (4h by road via Independence Pass, closed November-May).
jackson-hole:en: Jackson Hole, Wyoming, is the extreme ski resort par excellence: Corbet's Couloir (entry by 3-6m drops), 50% of the runs black or double-black diamond. Glacial valley dominated by the Tetons. July is my favourite month (9.8/10): 10-27°C, 11% rain, peak access to Grand Teton National Park and Yellowstone Park 1h30 away (geysers, Old Faithful, wildlife). From December to March, expect -12/0°C: ski season. The Tram (cable car) climbing to Rendezvous Peak (3185m) is the signature experience. Snow King in the heart of the village for beginners. From April to May and November, it's the off-season: Yellowstone closed, resort shut. In-town hotels (Wort Hotel, Snow King Resort) more affordable than the private lodges at Teton Village (1000-3000$/night in peak season). Direct flight to Jackson Hole Airport or Salt Lake City 5h by road.
koh-phi-phi:en: Koh Phi Phi, archipelago of 6 islands in the Andaman Sea in southern Thailand, halfway between Phuket and Krabi. The 2 inhabited islands: Phi Phi Don (4000 inhabitants, hotels and restaurants) and Phi Phi Leh (uninhabited, this is the Maya Bay from The Beach with Leonardo DiCaprio in 2000). Vertical limestone cliffs erupting from the turquoise sea = signature landscape of southern Thailand. From January to March are my favourite months (6.9/10): 25-29°C, 23-31% rain, the ideal dry season. Koh Phi Phi never tops 6.9/10. From November to April, it's the tourist high season: 25-29°C, sea at 28°C, extreme crowds at Maya Bay and on Phi Phi Don, hotel prices x3-x4. From May to October, it's the monsoon: 65-88% rainfall, ferries often cancelled in rough seas, many hotels close. Highlights: Maya Bay (Phi Phi Leh, signature bay from The Beach, closed 2018-2022 to allow regeneration of coral destroyed by 5000 visitors/day, reopened under strict quotas since January 2022 = max 4000 visitors/day, swimming forbidden, 1h access limit, stay duration capped), Phi Phi Don Viewpoint (3 levels, 30 min stair climb from Tonsai Bay, signature panorama over the hourglass-shaped Tonsai-Loh Dalum bays), Bamboo Island (Koh Mai Phai, white sand beach), Mosquito Island, Pileh Lagoon (emerald-green lagoon between cliffs), Loh Samah Bay (facing Maya Bay), Monkey Beach (on Phi Phi Don, sometimes aggressive wild macaques), Long Beach (quiet, view of Phi Phi Leh). Diving: cult sites around Phi Phi (Anemone Reef, Shark Point, King Cruiser Wreck, Hin Muang/Hin Daeng to the south), PADI training among the cheapest in the world. Tonsai Village = pedestrian centre with restaurants/bars/clubs, no roads or cars on the island (only carts). Nightlife lively (Slinky Bar, Reggae Bar, fire shows). Try Thai green curry, fresh seafood BBQ, banana-Nutella pancakes from the market, fruit shakes. Logistics: ferries from Phuket (2h) or Krabi (1h30). No airport on the island.
koh-tao:en: Koh Tao, small island on Thailand's east coast in the Gulf of Thailand: 21 km², 2000 inhabitants, nicknamed "Turtle Island" (its shape recalls a turtle + sea turtles historically present). One of the world's destinations for learning to dive (PADI Open Water training 9000 baht ≈ 250€ all-inclusive, among the cheapest in the world). January-February are my favourite months (8.6/10): 25-27°C, 28-30% rain. From January to August, it's the relative dry season in the Gulf (different climate from the Andaman west coast): 25-29°C, sea at 28°C. From May to August, it's the tourist high season. From September to December, it's the north-east monsoon: 44-84% rainfall, rough sea, many dive centres close or scale back. Highlights: Sairee Beach (the longest at 1.8 km, nightlife, restaurants), Mae Haad (port, ferries), Chalok Baan Kao (south, quieter), Shark Bay (snorkelling with blacktip reef sharks and turtles), Tanote Bay (east coast, 4×4 access tricky, snorkelling, deep water solo climbing), John-Suwan Viewpoint (south panorama over 3 clover-leaf bays, 30 min climb), Koh Nang Yuan (3-pronged islet linked by sandbars, one of Thailand's best-known postcard images, 1500 baht private entry). Major dive sites: Chumphon Pinnacle (the top, seasonal bull sharks), Sail Rock (between Koh Tao and Koh Phangan, one of the Gulf's best sites, whale shark possible March-May), Japanese Gardens, Twin Peaks, White Rock. PADI centres: Koh Tao has the world's highest density of dive centres (50+), Big Blue Diving and Crystal Dive among the best known. Crowd: backpackers mostly, 20-35 year olds, international diver training. Excursions: Koh Phangan 1h30 by ferry (Full Moon Party at Haad Rin), Koh Samui 2h (more upmarket island). Try Massaman curry, BBQ seafood, tropical smoothies. Logistics: ferries from Chumphon (2h, the most practical), Surat Thani (4h), Koh Samui (2h).
lombok:en: Lombok, Bali's neighbouring island (35 km to the east): 4700 km², 4 million inhabitants, quieter and less touristy than Bali, beaches among the wildest in Indonesia, dominated by the Rinjani volcano (3726m, Indonesia's 2nd highest). July-August are my favourite months (8.3/10): 21-29°C, 40-44% rain, this is the dry season. From May to September, it's the dry season: 40-67% rainfall. From November to March, it's the rainy season: 84-91% rainfall in violent showers. From April to June, expect 22-30°C with 51-71% rain: shoulder season. Highlights: Mount Rinjani (2-4 day climb, camping at the Segara Anak crater lake 2000m, sunrise at the summit, accessible only with certified guide, closed in the rainy season December-March), Gili Islands 25 min by boat from Bangsal Harbour (3 paradise islets without cars: Gili Trawangan party-oriented, Gili Air family-friendly, Gili Meno romantic), southern beaches (Kuta Lombok surf village, Tanjung Aan turquoise crescent beach, Mawun, Selong Belanak), Sembalun Valley (terraced rice paddies at the foot of Rinjani), Senaru and Tiu Kelep Waterfalls. Sasak culture = majority indigenous people of Lombok (Muslim, distinct from Hindu Balinese), traditional Sade and Ende villages. Try ayam taliwang (spicy chicken), plecing kangkung, sate rembiga. Safety: major earthquake in August 2018 (500 deaths) — rebuilding since. Direct flights from Singapore, Bali (25 min), Jakarta.
java:en: Java, main island of Indonesia in South-East Asia: 145 million inhabitants on 132,000 km² = the world's most densely populated island (1100 inhabitants/km², denser than Bangladesh). 60% of Indonesia's total population, 22 active volcanoes, capital Jakarta + Yogyakarta Javanese cultural capital + Borobudur + Prambanan major UNESCO sites. June-August are my favourite months (6.8-6.9/10): 19-30°C, 11-15% rain, the ideal dry season. Java never tops 6.9/10 (humid equatorial climate with marked monsoon). From May to September, it's the dry season: 20-31°C, the tourist high season. From October to April, it's the wet season: 47-72% rainfall, stable temperatures 22-31°C, the greenest landscapes but roads sometimes flooded. Highlights: Borobudur (UNESCO 1991, the world's largest Buddhist temple 8th century in central Javanese jungle, 504 Buddha statues across 9 levels symbolising the path to nirvana, signature visual at sunrise with Merapi-Merbabu volcanoes in the background = signature Java experience), Prambanan 50 km away (UNESCO 1991, Indonesia's largest Hindu temple 9th century, complex of 240 temples at the foot of Merapi volcano, contemporary rival to Borobudur), Yogyakarta ("Jogja", Javanese cultural capital, former sultanate still active with Kraton palace residence of Sultan Hamengkubuwono X, traditional Javanese batik workshops Indonesia's signature craft, gamelan Javanese orchestral music), Mount Bromo (Bromo Tengger Semeru National Park, active volcano 2329m in a "sea of sand" caldera with lunar appearance, signature panoramic sunrise at King Kong Hill viewpoint, hike to crater possible, one of Indonesia's postcard images), Mount Ijen (acid blue nocturnal volcano 2769m, blue flames = phenomenon unique in the world, sulphurous gas combustion visible at night before dawn, 3h round-trip hike with mandatory mask, local miners still extract sulphur by hand = signature documentary photography images), Jakarta capital (10 million inhabitants, chaotic-cosmopolitan-polluted megalopolis, already covered by BDW), Bandung (cooler mountain city, Art Deco colonial heritage), Karimunjawa Islands (paradise archipelago north of Java, preserved alternative to Bali), Dieng Plateau (7th century Hindu temples in lunar-like high-altitude landscapes). Javanese cuisine = one of the most refined in South-East Asia: nasi gudeg (rice with jackfruit, signature of Yogyakarta), sate ayam (chicken skewers), gado-gado (vegetable salad with peanut sauce), bakso (meatballs), tempe goreng (fried tempeh, Java = origin of tempe), local Bintang beer, kopi luwak ("civet coffee" originating in Java but ethically controversial). Logistics: airports Jakarta Soekarno-Hatta (CGK) + Yogyakarta International (YIA) opened 2019.
krabi:en: Krabi is the south-west Thai beach province, a more natural alternative to Phuket: Ao Nang (main resort), Railay (limestone peninsula accessible only by boat), Koh Lanta. From November to February (6.9/10): 22-31°C, 31-46% rain, sea at 28°C, the dry high season. Krabi never tops 7/10 (constant hot-humid tropical climate). From May to October, it's the south-west monsoon: 75-92% rainfall, rough sea, many boats halted. From March to April, expect 23-34°C with 42-75% rain: stifling heat. Highlights: Railay Beach (peninsula accessible only by boat from Ao Nang 15 min, 4 beaches: West, East, Phra Nang, Tonsai — climbing mecca on limestone cliffs, more than 700 routes), Phra Nang Cave Beach (one of the world's most beautiful beaches according to many rankings), Ao Nang (main resort, hotels and restaurants), 4 Islands tour (Phra Nang Cave, Koh Tup, Chicken Island, Koh Poda by longtail), Hong Islands tour (spectacular interior lagoon), Emerald Pool and Hot Springs at Khao Phra Bang Khram. Koh Phi Phi 2h by ferry from Krabi. Koh Lanta to the south (quieter island, long beach, diving at Hin Daeng/Hin Muang). Tiger Cave Temple (Wat Tham Sua, 1237 steps for the view). Try tom yum, massaman curry, local seafood. Direct flight Bangkok-Krabi in 1h15.
punta-cana:en: Punta Cana is the all-inclusive Caribbean beach resort par excellence: 60 km of coconut beaches in the east of the Dominican Republic. March is my favourite month (8.2/10): 23-27°C, 52% rain, sea at 26°C. From June to November, it's hurricane season: 64-78% rainfall, hurricane risk (main Caribbean route), but off the main path in Punta Cana (Atlantic east side). From December to April, expect 23-29°C: North-American and Canadian high season, hotel prices x2-x3. Highlights: Beaches Bávaro, Cabeza de Toro, El Cortecito (the liveliest), Macao (wilder). Hoyo Azul (turquoise cenote at Scape Park), Saona and Catalina (catamaran excursions to postcard islets), Isla Saona (tourist peak, departures from Bayahibe). ATV/buggy excursions in the countryside, diving on the east coast. Bavaro and Higüey are the tourist zones. Las Terrenas (Samaná Peninsula) 2h away offers a more European, less all-inclusive atmosphere (substantial French community). Santo Domingo 2h (UNESCO, the first European city in the Americas 1496). Try mangú (plantain), pollo guisado, sancocho, mama juana (rum-wine-spices). All-inclusive culture very dominant: little incentive to leave the resorts. Direct flight from Paris in 9h.
jamaique:en: Jamaica, land of reggae (Bob Marley), rum, Blue Mountain Coffee. January is my favourite month (8.6/10): 20-29°C, 30% rain, end of the American high season. From June to October, it's hurricane season: 67-81% rainfall, real hurricane risk. From December to April, expect 20-30°C: North-American tourist high season, prices x2-x3. Highlights: Negril (Seven Mile Beach, Rick's Café cliffs at sunset), Montego Bay (touristy, all-inclusive, Doctor's Cave Beach), Ocho Rios (Dunn's River Falls 180m of waterfalls to climb, Mystic Mountain bobsleigh), Port Antonio (more local east coast, Blue Lagoon, Reach Falls), Kingston (capital, Bob Marley Museum on Hope Road, Trench Town for fans), Blue Mountains (world-renowned coffee, Blue Mountain Peak 2256m, 4h ascent for sunrise). Safety: Kingston demands vigilance (some risky neighbourhoods), all-inclusive resorts generally secured, private transport recommended. Try jerk chicken/pork (traditional jerk spice), ackee and saltfish (national dish), patties, rum punch. Rasta culture: visible everywhere, marijuana decriminalised (2 ounces) but not legal. Direct flight Paris-Montego Bay (charter airlines in season).
barbade:en: Barbados is the British Caribbean: left-hand driving, national cricket, rum (Mount Gay, founded in 1703, the world's oldest distillery). April stays my favourite month (8.6/10): 23-28°C, 41% rain, sea at 27°C. The outer coral reef (west coast) protects from heavy swells: Carlisle Bay, sheltered beach good for swimming. The east coast (Bathsheba, Soup Bowl) keeps the wild Atlantic: world-class surf, dangerous swimming. From August to October, it's hurricane season with 70-77% rainfall: tropical storm risk, but off the main path (Barbados is protected by its position east of the Caribbean arc). Bridgetown is UNESCO for its colonial historic centre (Nidhe Israel synagogue from 1654, one of the oldest in the Americas). From December to March stays the American and British high season: prices x2-x3. May-June and November are excellent compromises crowd/weather/price.
guadeloupe:en: Guadeloupe, French overseas department in the Lesser Antilles: 1700 km², 380,000 inhabitants, butterfly-shaped archipelago (mountainous Basse-Terre to the west + flatter Grande-Terre to the east). January is my favourite month (8.3/10): 23-28°C, 50% rain, sea at 27°C, this is the carême (dry season). From December to April, it's the tourist high season: 23-29°C, 50-59% rainfall in brief showers, hotel prices x2-x3 (flight from Paris x3 in peak season). From July to October, it's the active hurricane season ("hivernage"): 65-83% rainfall, real hurricane risk (Maria 2017 caused major damage), but stable temperatures (24-31°C). May-June and November are the best shoulder seasons: reduced prices, moderate crowds. Basse-Terre highlights (volcano, jungle, hiking): La Soufrière (1467m, 1h30 hike to summit, active sulphur, fumaroles), Carbet Falls, Grande Anse Beach in Deshaies, Plage de la Perle, Cousteau Reserve (diving and snorkelling). Grande-Terre highlights (beaches, more touristy): Pointe-à-Pitre (capital, St-Antoine market), Sainte-Anne (Caravelle beach), Saint-François (marina, golf), Pointe des Châteaux (far east, limestone cliffs). Neighbouring islands: Marie-Galante ("the island of 100 mills"), Les Saintes (Terre-de-Haut, world's most beautiful bay according to UNESCO), La Désirade. Try bokit, accras, colombo, ti-punch, coconut sorbet, Damoiseau and Bologne rums. Direct flight from Paris in 8h.
martinique:en: Martinique, French overseas department in the Lesser Antilles: 1100 km², 360,000 inhabitants, the "island of flowers" (in Creole "Madinina"). January is my favourite month (8.1/10): 23-28°C, 58% rain, this is the carême (dry season), sea at 27°C. From December to May, it's the tourist high season: 22-30°C, hotel prices x2-x3, flight from Paris x3 in peak season. From July to October, it's the active hurricane season ("hivernage"): 70-81% rainfall, real hurricane risk (Maria 2017 hit). From June and November are the best shoulder seasons. Highlights: Fort-de-France (capital, spice market), Saint-Pierre (former "Little Paris of the Antilles", destroyed by the eruption of Mount Pelée on 8 May 1902 = 30,000 deaths in 1 minute, only 1 survivor Cyparis in his cell, ruins visitable), Mount Pelée (1397m, 6h round-trip hike, still active volcano), Trois-Îlets (birthplace of Empress Josephine), Diamond Rock (Le Diamant, signature rock to the south), Pointe du Bout (hotel zone), Anses-d'Arlet (signature beachside church), Plage des Salines (the most famous, south), Tartane (Caravelle, wild coast), Habitations Clément (rum, former plantation), Saint-James (rum). Activities: diving (Diamond Rock, Saint-Pierre), Canal des Esclaves hike, Grenadines cruises south (Saint Lucia 30 min by helicopter). Try cod accras, chicken colombo, Creole boudin, ti-punch, planteur, AOC Martinique rum (the world's only AOC for rum). Direct flight from Paris in 8h.
majorque:en: Mallorca, the largest of the Balearics: 3640 km², 950,000 inhabitants, dominated by the Serra de Tramuntana mountains (UNESCO). Internationally diverse (Germans in the majority, Brits, Scandinavians, French). June is my favourite month (9.6/10): 20-28°C, 15% rain, sea at 22°C, crowds still manageable. July-August drop to 6.9/10 due to heat (23-32°C) and especially the extreme summer crowds: 18 million visitors/year, established overtourism (packed beaches, traffic jams, prices x4-x5). From December to February, expect 8-16°C and 19-24% rain: mild for Spain, many hotels and restaurants close, golf in full activity (Northern Europeans wintering). Regions: Palma (capital, Gothic Cathedral, Old Town, Es Baluard modern art museum), Serra de Tramuntana (Sóller on the historic wooden train, Valldemossa where Chopin and George Sand spent the winter of 1838-39, Deià village of artists), Cap Formentor in the north (winding road to the lighthouse, spectacular viewpoint), Alcudia (Roman old town, 14 km beach), Cabrera Archipelago (national park to the south, accessible by boat), Coves del Drac (caves with underground lake and classical music concert), Magaluf (poor reputation, young Brits, avoid if you want quiet). Beaches: Es Trenc (wild, south), Cala Mondrago (nature park), Cala Macarella (actually on Menorca), Cala Deia. Try ensaimada, tumbet, sobrasada, Binissalem wines. May and September-October are the best tourism/weather compromises.
minorque:en: Menorca, the quietest of the Balearics: 700 km², 95,000 inhabitants, designated UNESCO Biosphere Reserve in 1993 (35% of the territory protected). Beaches wilder than on Mallorca or Ibiza. June is my favourite month (9.5/10): 20-27°C, 11% rain, sea at 22°C, crowds still manageable. July-August drop to 6.9/10 due to heat (22-30°C) and the summer crowds: mostly Italians and Catalans, hotel prices x3-x4, car hire difficult (book 3 months ahead in July-August). From December to February, expect 9-16°C and 24-25% rain: mild, many hotels close, Barcelona ferries reduced. Highlights: Mahón (Maó, capital, natural harbour, one of the world's largest), Ciutadella (charming old town), Cami de Cavalls (185 km trail around the island, legacy of medieval horsemen). Calas (coves): Cala Macarella, Cala Macarelleta, Cala Mitjana, Cala Pregonda (the wildest), Cala Tortuga (tricky access). Talayotic sites (UNESCO 2023): Talatí de Dalt, Trepucó (megalithic constructions 2000-1000 BC). Try caldereta de langosta (born here), sobrasada, queso Mahón, pomada (Xoriguer gin + lemonade). Festes de Sant Joan in Ciutadella mid-June (horses rearing in the crowd). May and September-October are the real sweet spots tourism/weather.
corse:en: Corsica, the "island of beauty": 8000 km², 350,000 inhabitants, mountains (Monte Cinto 2706m) plunging into the Mediterranean. July is my favourite month (8.9/10): 15-24°C, 23% rain, sea at 24°C at the Palombaggia, Rondinara, Saleccia beaches. August excellent (8.7/10) but massive crowds, prices x3, ferries fully booked, sea routes congested. From December to March, expect -1/8°C in the mountains and 6-15°C on the plain: skiing possible at Capanelle or Vergio (but unreliable), winter hiking impossible at altitude. Highlights: Bonifacio (citadel perched on limestone cliffs), Calvi (Genoese citadel), Bastia (Place Saint-Nicolas), Ajaccio (Napoleon's house), Calanques de Piana (UNESCO), Désert des Agriates, GR20 (one of Europe's hardest treks, 16 stages, June-October). Beaches: Palombaggia, Rondinara, Saleccia, Lotu (accessible only by boat or 4×4). Try Corsican charcuterie (lonzu, coppa, prisuttu), brocciu, fiadone, Patrimonio or Ajaccio wine. May-June and September-October are the real sweet spots crowd/weather.
sardaigne:en: Sardinia, the Mediterranean's second largest island: 24,000 km², 1.6 million inhabitants. Beaches among Europe's most beautiful, wild and preserved interior. June is my favourite month (9.3/10): 16-30°C, 17% rain, sea at 22°C, start of the high season. July drops to 6.9/10 due to heat (20-34°C, sometimes 42°C). August stays very good (8.6/10) but massive crowds, especially on the Costa Smeralda. From December to February, expect 4-14°C and 27-32% rain: mild but wet, many hotels close. Highlights: Costa Smeralda (created by the Aga Khan in 1962, jet-set, Porto Cervo), La Maddalena archipelago (Caprera), Cagliari (capital, Castello quarter), Alghero (Catalan-Italian, Capo Caccia cliffs and Neptune's Caves), Bosa (colourful village), Costa Verde (wild), Barbagia (interior, pastoral traditions). Beaches: Cala Goloritzé, Spiaggia di La Pelosa, Spiaggia di Tuerredda. Selvaggio Blu trail (one of Europe's hardest, 7 days). Nuraghi (megalithic constructions 1500 BC, UNESCO Su Nuraxi). Try porceddu (suckling pig), pane carasau, pecorino sardo, mirto. May and September-October are the best tourism/weather compromises.
crete:en: Crete is the largest Greek island (8300 km², 600,000 inhabitants): mountains, gorges, beaches, 4000 years of Minoan heritage. June-July are my favourite months (9.4/10): 20-31°C, 5-14% rain, sea at 24°C. August drops to 6.9/10 due to heatwave (22-31°C), summer crowds (Italians, Germans), prices x3-x4. From December to February, expect 7-16°C and 37-43% rain: mild for France but many small hotels close, snow-covered peaks (the Lefka Ori 2452m even offer skiing at the Kallergi refuge). Regions: Heraklion (capital, base for Knossos), Chania (Venetian old town, picturesque harbour, considered the most beautiful Cretan city), Rethymno (fortress, beach), Agios Nikolaos / Elounda (luxury zone in the east, many 5*+ Domes Resorts), Sitia (far east, Vai palm grove). Highlights: Knossos (Minoan palace reconstructed by Sir Arthur Evans, controversy over the "fantasy reconstruction", complete with the Heraklion Archaeological Museum), Samaria Gorge (16 km, 5-7h, open May-October, one of Europe's longest, hiked downhill towards Agia Roumeli), Beaches: Elafonissi (pink sand, turquoise lagoon, over-visited), Balos (white sand, boat or hike access), Vai (palm grove, Europe's largest), Falasarna (sunsets). Try dakos (salad), moussaka, kalitsounia, Cretan raki (eau-de-vie offered with pride). Spinalonga (former leper colony island, subject of Victoria Hislop's novel The Island). May and September-October are the best tourism/weather compromises.
corfou:en: Corfu, Greek island in the Ionian Sea off Albania: 100,000 inhabitants on 600 km², the greenest of the Greek islands (wetter climate than the Cyclades, massive olive groves, 4 million olive trees planted under Venetian rule). Mixed Venetian (UNESCO old town 2007), British (1815-1864) and Greek heritage, atmosphere unique in Greece. June is my favourite month (9.6/10): 21-28°C, 12% rain, sea at 22°C. July-August drop to 6.9/10 due to heat (24-32°C) and crowds (Italians, Brits and Germans in the majority, hotel prices x3-x4, packed beaches). September stays excellent (8.6/10). From November to March, expect 9-15°C and 36-53% rain: mild but wet, many hotels close. Highlights: Corfu Old Town (UNESCO, maze of "kantounia" alleys, 16th-18th century Venetian architecture, Liston arcaded esplanade in Rue de Rivoli style built by the French in 1807, Spianada = the largest square in the Balkans), Venetian Fortress (Palaio Frourio 10th-16th century), New Fortress (16th century), Achilleion (Empress Sissi's palace 1890, statue of Achilles), Paleokastritsa (west coast, turquoise coves, perched 18th-century monastery), Cape Drastis (wild northern cliffs), Porto Timoni Beach (heart-shaped double beach, accessible on foot or by boat), Pontikonisi ("mouse island", the islet photographed in front of the Vlacherna monastery on plane arrival). Excursions: Antipaxos (Caribbean-turquoise waters), Albania (Saranda 30 min by ferry from Corfu). Try sofrito (slow-cooked beef), pastitsada (rooster pasta), bourdetto (spicy fish), kumquat liqueur (Corfu fruit), tsitsibira (gingerbeer British heritage). Corfu-Kapodistrias airport (CFU).
rhodes:en: Rhodes, largest island of the Dodecanese off Turkey: 115,000 inhabitants on 1400 km², one of Greece's most historic islands (Colossus of Rhodes = one of the 7 Wonders of the ancient world, destroyed by earthquake in 226 BC). Heritage of the medieval Knights Hospitaller (1309-1522, UNESCO old town 1988) then Ottoman then Italian (1912-1947). June is my favourite month (9.7/10): 22-29°C, 4% rain, sea at 24°C. July-August drop to 6.9/10 due to heat (25-33°C, sometimes strong meltemi wind) and crowds (one of the most visited Greek islands, European charters, hotel prices x3-x4). October stays excellent (8.8/10). From November to March, expect 11-21°C and 13-41% rain: mild for the Mediterranean, many hotels close but Rhodes town stays open year-round. Highlights: Rhodes Old Town (UNESCO, the largest inhabited medieval city in Europe, 4 km of ramparts, Palace of the Grand Master 14th century restored by Mussolini in 1937, Street of the Knights = one of the best-preserved medieval streets in Europe, 7 "inns" of the languages of the Knights), Acropolis of Lindos (45 km south, 4th century BC Temple of Athena Lindia on a 116m promontory, spectacular view over Saint-Paul Bay), Valley of the Butterflies (Petaloudes, June-September = migration of Panaxia quadripunctaria butterflies), Anthony Quinn Bay (turquoise coves, the actor invested here in the 1960s), Tsambika and Prasonisi (renowned kitesurf beaches), Filerimos (ancient acropolis with calvary). Excursions: Symi (ranked the most beautiful Dodecanese island, colourful neoclassical houses, 1h by ferry). Try pitaroudia (chickpea fritters), melekouni (sesame-honey cake), Santorini fava, ouzo. Rhodes airport (RHO).
capri:en: Capri, the chic island of the Bay of Naples: 10 km², 13,000 permanent inhabitants, 2.5 million visitors/year. July is my favourite month (9.6/10): 24-26°C, 5% rain, sea at 24°C, but also absolute crowd peak: ferries from Naples or Sorrento packed, hotel prices x4-x5 (palace hotels 1500-3000€/night). From June to September, the island sees the international jet-set parade, paparazzi at Marina Grande as the stars disembark. From November to March, expect 11-18°C and 32-52% rain: 70% of restaurants and hotels close, ferries reduced, local village atmosphere. Highlights: Grotta Azzurra (entry by rowing boat, electric light reflected by the white limestone), Faraglioni (3 signature rocks of the island), Villa San Michele (of the Swede Axel Munthe at Anacapri), Monte Solaro (589m, chairlift from Anacapri), Villa Jovis (Tiberius spent his last 10 years here). Piazzetta (Capri town, jet-set at aperitivo). Try insalata caprese (born here), ravioli capresi, torta caprese (chocolate-almond cake). Anacapri is quieter than Capri town. May and September-October are the best crowd/weather compromises.
formentera:en: Formentera is Ibiza's quiet sister: no airport, access only by ferry from Ibiza (30 minutes). Flat island (19 km long), ideal by bicycle. Caribbean-style turquoise beaches. July is my favourite month (9.7/10): 24-26°C, 1% rain, sea at 25°C. From July to August, the invasion is total: luxury Italians and French, prices x3, restaurants overflowing. Famous beaches: Ses Illetes (regularly ranked among Europe's most beautiful), Cala Saona, Migjorn. From November to March, expect 13-19°C and 17-28% rain: the island almost completely hibernates, 80% of restaurants and hotels close. May-June and September-October are the real sweet spots: still summer weather, pleasant cycling, and the chance to enjoy the beaches almost alone. No nightlife (unlike Ibiza): this is a contemplation destination, not a party one. Faro de la Mola for sunset.
sintra:en: Sintra, UNESCO listed, was the holiday retreat of the Portuguese kings: 30 min by train from Lisbon, humid microclimate that creates lush vegetation (almost tropical in places). August is my favourite month (9.3/10): 17-24°C, 4% rain, ideal temperatures when Lisbon is hitting 30°C+. July excellent (9.2/10). From November to March, expect 8-17°C and 33-45% rain: very misty (the castles in the mist are photogenic but the panoramic views vanish), many hotels and restaurants close. Highlights: Palacio Nacional da Pena (19th-century romantic palace in garish colours, the most famous, packed in peak season, booking required), Quinta da Regaleira (initiation well, esoteric gardens), Castelo dos Mouros (Moorish ruins at the summit), Palacio Nacional (town centre), Cabo da Roca (the westernmost point of continental Europe, 30 min by bus). Logistics: take the train from Lisbon Rossio (40 min, 2.30€), then bus 434 between sites (loop, 12€/day). Go on weekdays and early absolutely (town packed 11h-17h). Try travesseiros and queijadas (local pastries) at Piriquita. Cascais and Estoril 30 min by train for the sea.
madrid:en: Madrid has no sea but compensates with everything else: 3000h of sun/year, Europe's highest capital (650m). June is my favourite month (9.4/10): 17-30°C, 15% rain, evenings stretching until 2am on the terraces. July-August drop to 6.9/10 due to dry heatwave (20-34°C, sometimes 40°C): Madrileños flee to the sea, many small shops close in August, the city strangely quiet. From December to February, expect 1-14°C and 19-23% rain: dry cold but blue sky, ideal for the museum triangle (Prado, Reina Sofía, Thyssen). September-October are my real sweet spots: 12-27°C, golden light, fiestas del Pilar, harvests in La Mancha. Try aperitivo in the Barrio de las Letras, the San Miguel market for tapeo, churros at San Ginés at 3am. Toledo, Segovia and El Escorial are 1h by train for day visits.
seville:en: Seville is the quintessence of Andalusia: Alcazar, cathedral, flamenco, tapas. May is my favourite month (9.5/10): 15-28°C, 15% rain, jacarandas in bloom everywhere, Feria de Abril (2 weeks after Easter) for the flamenco festival par excellence. July-August drop to 6.9/10 due to dry heatwave (22-37°C, sometimes 45°C): Sevillanos living in air conditioning, going out after 22h, the city near-dead during the day. From December to February, expect 6-19°C and 18-21% rain: mild, ideal for visiting without crowds, oranges falling in the streets. Holy Week (Semana Santa) in April is the absolute tourist peak: prices x4-x5, processions every night. Alcazar (booking required), Cathedral and Giralda, Plaza de España (María Luisa Park), Triana on the other side of the Guadalquivir for authentic flamenco. Try a Cruzcampo with serranitos. March-April and October-November are the best tourism/weather compromises.
florence:en: Florence concentrates the Italian Renaissance into 5 km²: Uffizi, Duomo, Ponte Vecchio. June is my favourite month (9.2/10): 16-29°C, 25% rain, Tuscan light on the Piazzale Michelangelo terraces (city view), aperitivo at Santo Spirito. July-August drop to 6.9/10 due to stifling heat (19-32°C, feels like 38°C+): Florentines fleeing to Tuscany, Uffizi suffocating even with air conditioning. From December to February, expect 2-13°C and 36-42% rain: damp cold, but this is the best museum period (Uffizi attendance down 50%, short waits). The Uffizi and Galleria dell'Accademia (David) require booking in peak season. Oltrarno district for the local side (artisans, popular restaurants), San Lorenzo for the market and popular cuisine, Fiesole 30 min by bus for the Etruscans and the view. May and September-October are the real sweet spots tourism/weather.
venise:en: Venice has reached overtourism extremes: 30 million visitors/year for 50,000 inhabitants. The city council introduced a 5€/day entry ticket for day-trippers in 2024. June-August are my favourite months (9.0-9.2/10): 19-28°C, 25-27% rain, 15h days, but also absolute crowd peak. From November to February, expect 0-13°C and 22-33% rain: Venice in its most magnificent face for connoisseurs (fog, empty calli, Burano without tour operators), Carnival late February for costumes, but also acqua alta (high tide rising to San Marco 5-15 days/winter, MOSE active since 2020 reduces the risk). Off the beaten track: Cannaregio (the Jewish ghetto), Castello (real local life), Burano and Torcello (1h vaporetto). Booking required for St Mark's and the Doge's Palace. May and September-October are the best crowd/weather compromises, far from deserted though.
milan:en: Milan is Italy's business, fashion and design capital: less charming than Florence or Venice, but more modern. June is my favourite month (8.9/10): 18-28°C, 35% rain in storms, Navigli terraces packed, aperitivo from 6-8pm (concept born here, 8-12€ with buffet). July-August are hot and heavy (20-30°C, oppressive humidity), Milanese fleeing to the sea in August, the city strangely quiet. From December to February, expect -1/8°C and 23-30% rain: frequent fog over the Po Plain, ideal for museums (Pinacoteca di Brera, Leonardo's Cenacolo with booking 3 months ahead). Duomo Cathedral with rooftop access, Galleria Vittorio Emanuele II, Brera district (artists), Navigli (canals, nightlife). La Scala opens its season on 7 December (Sant'Ambrogio). Lake Como 1h by train, Bergamo 50 min. May and September-October are the best tourism/weather compromises.
munich:en: Munich (München), capital of Bavaria: 1.5 million inhabitants, Germany's most prosperous city (BMW, Siemens), "Northern Italy" atmosphere (biergartens, baroque art). July-August are my favourite months (8.1/10): 14-24°C, 46-48% rain in afternoon storms, biergartens packed, festivals. Oktoberfest (mid-September to early October, 16 days, 6 million visitors, 7 million litres of beer) = absolute tourist peak, prices x5, hotels fully booked 6 months ahead, unique atmosphere. From November to March, expect -2/8°C and 38-45% precipitation often as snow: dry cold, Christkindlmarkt Christmas market (Marienplatz, more than 30 Christmas markets in Munich) from 25 November to 24 December, skiing possible 1h30 by bus (Zugspitze 2962m, Garmisch-Partenkirchen). Highlights: Marienplatz (main square, Glockenspiel at 11h and 12h every day — 32 animated figures), Frauenkirche (signature gothic cathedral with its 2 green domes), Residenz (Wittelsbach palace for 700 years), Hofbräuhaus (the world's most famous brewery since 1589), Englischer Garten (Europe's largest urban park, larger than Central Park, permanent surf wave on the Eisbach, biergartens, Chinesischer Turm), Viktualienmarkt (permanent central market), BMW Welt and Museum. Excursions: Neuschwanstein Castle 2h away (Ludwig II, the castle that inspired Disney, book ahead), Dachau 30 min away (first Nazi concentration camp, memorial, free), Salzburg 1h30, Innsbruck 2h. Try Wiener Schnitzel, Weisswurst (white sausage, traditionally eaten before noon), Brezen (pretzel), Schweinshaxe (pork knuckle), beer (Augustiner, Hacker-Pschorr, Spaten).
copenhague:en: Copenhagen lives its best moments in summer: bicycle city, urban beaches (Amager Strandpark), harbour baths (Islands Brygge, Sluseholmen). June-August are my favourite months (7.9-8.1/10): 13-20°C, 33-42% rain, days stretching to 22h. From December to February, expect 1-6°C and 38-40% rain: grey skies, days down to 7h of light, hygge (candles, coffee, blankets in cafés). Tivoli opens in November for the Christmas season (lights, rides under the snow). Nyhavn stays the must-do (expensive), Christiania for the hippie utopia in the middle of the city, Nørrebro district for modern Nordic cuisine. Try a smørrebrød at Aamanns or Schønnemann, cinnamon swirl at Hart Bakery. Copenhagen stays very expensive: restaurant meal 25-40€, beer 7-10€. May-June and September are the best price/weather compromises. Direct flight from Paris in 2h.
stockholm:en: Stockholm is built on 14 islands linked by 57 bridges: city of water and archipelago. June-July are my favourite months (7.9/10): 11-22°C, 33-38% rain, midnight sun (almost, 18h of daylight), Skärgården (archipelago) by boat (Vaxholm, Sandhamn), swimming in the Mälaren and Brunnsviken lakes. From December to February, expect -3/2°C and 30-38% precipitation often as snow: very dark skies (4h of light end of December), aurora borealis season some winters and the Skansen Christmas market. Cosy cafés (fika mandatory with kanelbullar), public saunas. Vasa Museum (1628 vessel), Gamla Stan (old town), ABBA Museum are the classics. Stockholm stays very expensive: restaurant meal 25-40€, beer 8-10€. May and September are the best price/weather compromises, just before or after the high season.
oslo:en: Oslo is one of Europe's most natural capitals: forests 15 minutes away (Nordmarka), 40 islands in the Oslofjord. June-July are my favourite months (7.6-7.7/10): 12-21°C, 40-42% rain, 18h days, swimming at the fjord islands (Hovedøya by ferry from Aker Brygge), festivals (Øyafestivalen early August). From December to February, expect -5/2°C and 35-41% precipitation (often snow): cross-country skiing direct from the centre (Holmenkollen), aurora borealis possible some winters. Vigeland Park (sculptures), Munch Museum (relocated in 2021), Opera House whose roof you walk up, Bygdøy for the museums (Viking, Fram, Kon-Tiki) are the classics. Oslo stays very expensive: restaurant meal 30-50€, beer 10-12€. May-June and September are the real sweet spots price/weather. Bergen 6h by train (one of Europe's most beautiful rail journeys).
edimbourg:en: Edinburgh, Scottish capital: 540,000 inhabitants, medieval old town (UNESCO) on extinct volcano, 18th-century Georgian city (also UNESCO = rare double listing). July-September are my favourite months (7.0-7.2/10): 8-18°C, 46-53% rain, this is the August festival season (Edinburgh Festival Fringe, the world's largest performing arts festival, 3000 shows over 25 days, and the Edinburgh International Festival, more traditional). Hotel prices x4-x5 in August, book 6 months ahead. From December to March, expect 2-9°C and 42-52% rain: damp cold, days down to 7h of light end of December, but Hogmanay (Scottish New Year, 30 December-1 January, one of the world's biggest New Year celebrations, torch parade, fireworks, ceilidhs — folk dances). Highlights: Royal Mile (1.8 km of the Old Town, from Castle to Holyrood Palace), Edinburgh Castle (on Castle Rock, perched at 130m, Scottish Crown Jewels), Holyrood Palace (official royal residence in Scotland), Arthur's Seat (251m, ancient volcano in the city centre, 1h hike for the view), Calton Hill (alternative panorama), National Museum of Scotland, Dean Village (charming in the centre). Greyfriars Bobby (statue of a terrier who watched over his master's grave for 14 years, local legend). Excursions: Stirling Castle 1h away, Loch Lomond and the Highlands 2h away, Isle of Skye 5-6h. Try haggis (stuffed sheep's stomach, national dish), neeps and tatties, cranachan, single malt whisky.
istanbul:en: Istanbul is the only city straddling two continents (Europe and Asia separated by the Bosphorus), capital of 3 successive empires. June-July are my favourite months (9.2-9.5/10): 19-29°C, 12-21% rain, terraces over the Bosphorus, ferries between the two continents (Eminönü ↔ Üsküdar for 30 cents). August drops to 6.9/10 due to humid heat (22-30°C). From December to February, expect 4-12°C and 35-43% rain: Istanbul wet and windy, but this is the moment for mosques without crowds (Hagia Sophia, Blue Mosque) and Turkish baths (Çemberlitas, Cağaloğlu hammams). Highlights: Hagia Sophia (back to mosque status in 2020, free access), Blue Mosque, Topkapi, Basilica Cistern (renovated 2022), Grand Bazaar (4000 shops), Egyptian Bazaar (spices). Districts: Sultanahmet (touristy but classic), Beyoğlu (Istiklal Caddesi, nightlife), Karaköy (trendy), Kadıköy on the Asian side (cuisine, markets). Bosphorus cruise mandatory (public ferries 5€ vs touristic tours at 30€+). Try kebab, baklava, simit, döner. May and September-October are the best crowd/weather compromises.
marrakech:en: Marrakech (the "red city" for its rammed-earth ramparts), Morocco's tourist capital: 950,000 inhabitants, UNESCO Medina, Sahara dunes 8h by car. May is my favourite month (9.3/10): 15-30°C, 12% rain, ideal temperatures. From July to August drop to 3.9/10 due to extreme heatwave (21-39°C, sometimes 47°C, feels like 50°C+): impossible to visit in the middle of the day, locals coming out after 21h. From December to February, expect 6-22°C: mild during the day but cool at night (riads aren't heated), good period for athletes (semi-marathon in January, Marrakech opens its winter golf season). Highlights: Place Jemaa el-Fna (UNESCO intangible heritage, snake charmers, storytellers, monkey handlers, transformed in the evening into a giant open-air restaurant), Souks (spices, leather, carpets, metals), Koutoubia Mosque (12th century, sister of Seville's Giralda), Bahia Palace (19th century, mosaics), Saadian Tombs, Ben Youssef Madrasa, Majorelle Garden (Yves Saint Laurent), Médersa Ben Youssef. Excursions: Atlas day trip (Ourika valley, Imlil, Toubkal ascent 4167m), Essaouira 2h30 away (Atlantic coast, hippie atmosphere), Merzouga/Erg Chebbi desert 8h away (2-3 days by 4×4 with bivouac night). Try tagines, couscous (traditionally on Fridays), pastilla, mint tea. Direct flight from Paris in 3h30.
le-caire:en: Cairo, Egyptian capital: 22 million inhabitants in the urban area (Africa's and the Middle East's largest city), chaos, extreme culture shock, ancient civilisation. April is my favourite month (9.4/10): 15-30°C, 2% rain, ideal temperatures for visiting the pyramids. From November to April, it's the high season: 9-30°C, ideal dry climate. From June to August drops to 3.9-6.9/10 due to heatwave (22-38°C, sometimes 45°C), khamsin (sandstorm) in April-May. From September to October, expect 19-36°C: temperatures dropping back. Highlights: Giza Plateau (3 pyramids of Khufu/Khafre/Menkaure, Sphinx, 30 min from the centre, separate tickets to enter Khufu), Egyptian Museum on Tahrir Square (in transition to the Grand Egyptian Museum near the pyramids, gradually opening since 2024, the world's largest archaeology museum), Khan El-Khalili (historic 14th-century souk), Saladin Citadel and Mohamed Ali Mosque, Old Coptic Cairo, Ibn Tulun Mosque (Cairo's oldest, 879). Saqqara 30 km away (step pyramid of Djoser, the world's oldest, 2700 BC), Memphis. Safety: tourist police present everywhere, verbal harassment common for women. Try kushari (national dish), foul, ta'meya (Egyptian falafel), om ali. Direct flight from Paris in 4h45.
petra:en: Petra demands you choose your moment carefully: 2200m altitude in the heart of a desert, so cold in winter, scorching in summer. May is my favourite month (9.8/10): 17-28°C, 4% rain, golden light on the pink sandstone. The hike up to the Monastery (Ad Deir, 40 minutes of stair climbing) stays viable. From June to August, the mercury hits 22-32°C from 9h. It becomes essential to leave before 6h for the Treasury (Khazneh) in morning light, and return before noon. Bedouins serve tea everywhere on the site. From December to February, nights drop to 6°C, rare but possible snowfalls can close the site for a few hours. Wadi Rum 2h by road complements Petra well (1-2 nights bivouacking). The "Petra by Night" 3 times a week is touristy but stays a singular moment.
beyrouth:en: Beirut, capital of Lebanon on the Mediterranean coast: 360,000 inhabitants intra-muros, 2.4 million in the urban area (50% of Lebanon's total population). Historically nicknamed the "Paris of the Middle East" for its cosmopolitanism and chic before the 1975-1990 civil war that destroyed the city. Reconstruction since 1990 but slowed by successive crises (Hariri assassination 2005, Israel-Hezbollah war 2006, catastrophic economic crisis 2019 with 95% devaluation of the Lebanese pound, port explosion on 4 August 2020 = 218 deaths, 7000 injured, one of the largest non-nuclear explosions in history, following 2750 tonnes of ammonium nitrate stored illegally). May-June are my favourite months (9.1-9.7/10): 18-29°C, 3-13% rain, ideal temperatures. September-October stay excellent (8.7-9.0/10). From March to November, it's the tourist high season: 11-30°C. From July to August drop to 6.9/10 due to heat (24-31°C, oppressive humidity). From December to February, expect 9-18°C and 39-46% rain: mild for the Mediterranean, snow possible in nearby mountains (1600m, skiing possible at Faraya Mzaar). Highlights: Corniche (4.8 km seaside promenade, lively atmosphere, joggers, fishermen, families, one of the symbolic places of recovered Beirut), Pigeon Rocks (Raouché Rocks, signature rock formations in the sea facing the Corniche, photo stop), Reconstructed downtown (Beirut Central District = BCD, rebuilt in the 1990s-2000s by Solidere, private company of billionaire Rafic Hariri assassinated 2005, half-pastiche half-traditional atmosphere, Place de l'Étoile with parliament, Mohammad Al-Amin Mosque opened 2007 with signature blue dome, Saint-Georges Greek-Orthodox Cathedral, Saint-Georges Maronite Church side by side = symbol of interfaith coexistence), Gemmayze district (rue Gouraud bohemian-trendy, bars-restaurants-art galleries, lively nightlife), Mar Mikhael (extension of Gemmayze, trendy since 2010-2015), Hamra (historic university district, AUB American University of Beirut campus among the most beautiful in the Middle East), Gouraud quarter (preserved 19th-20th century French colonial architecture), National Museum of Beirut (one of the most important archaeological museums in the Middle East, Phoenician sarcophagi), Sursock Museum (19th-century Arab Renaissance palace with contemporary Lebanese art collection, damaged by 2020 explosion but reopened 2023), Beirut archaeological site (Phoenician, Roman, Byzantine remains in the city centre). Lebanon excursions: Byblos 40 min away (UNESCO, one of the world's longest continuously inhabited cities, 7000 years), Baalbek 2h away (UNESCO, the world's best-preserved Roman sanctuary of Jupiter), Qadisha Valley 2h away (UNESCO, holy valley of Maronite monks, millennial cedars), Anjar 1h30 away (UNESCO, 8th-century Umayyad ruins), Tyre and Sidon in the south (Phoenician-Roman remains). Try Lebanese mezze (hummus, baba ghanouj, tabbouleh, fattoush, kibbeh), shawarma, knafeh, Arabic coffee with cardamom, Lebanese arak (anisette), Bekaa wines (legendary Château Musar). Safety: fragile political situation, sometimes violent protests, southern border with Israel a potential conflict zone (Hezbollah-Israel clashes).
shanghai:en: Shanghai plays its role as economic capital of modern China: futuristic Pudong skyline, colonial Bund heritage, 26 million inhabitants. May and October are my favourite months (8.0/10): 16-25°C, 25-37% rain, mild temperatures between summer heatwave and damp winter. July-August are oppressive (26-32°C, max humidity), Shanghainese leaving on holiday: city strangely quieter. From December to February, expect 2-11°C and 20-31% rain: damp cold that gets through, often grey days. The Bund at sunset for the East-West architectural contrasts, Yu Garden and the old town, Pudong (Shanghai Tower 632m, observatory), French Concession for the village atmosphere and trendy cafés. Try xiaolongbao at Din Tai Fung or Jia Jia Tang Bao, hairy crab (autumn season only). Very efficient metro, Maglev from Pudong airport (430 km/h, 8 min to Longyang). 144h transit visa free possible.
pekin:en: Beijing concentrates 3000 years of imperial history: Forbidden City, Temple of Heaven, Great Wall nearby. May is my favourite month (9.0/10): 15-28°C, 15% rain, the year's clearest sky, Yiheyuan gardens in bloom. From June to August, expect 22-33°C with monsoon in July (38-48% rain) and heavy heat. From December to February, expect -6/6°C: dry cold, often clear sky, but smog sometimes oppressive during thermal inversion (check the AQI before going out). Central heating kicks in on 15 November. Highlights: Forbidden City (booking required, otherwise 1-2h queue), Temple of Heaven (mornings to see locals practising tai chi), Tiananmen Square, Great Wall at Mutianyu (less crowded than Badaling, accessible in 2h). Hutongs (old alleys) around Houhai and Nanluoguxiang. Try Peking duck at Da Dong, jianbing for breakfast. September-October are the real sweet spots tourism/weather.
hong-kong:en: Hong Kong is the vertical megalopolis par excellence: 7 million inhabitants, 8000 skyscrapers (more than Manhattan), and 70% of the territory remaining as nature parks. October-November are my favourite months (8.3-8.4/10): 19-27°C, 21-36% rain, humidity finally bearable. From June to September, it's the monsoon: 70-91% rainfall, oppressive humidity (90%), typhoon risk (signal T8 or T10 = city at a standstill). From December to February, expect 14-22°C: cool and dry, the ideal month for hiking (Dragon's Back, Lantau Peak). Peak Tram (classic views), Star Ferry Central-Tsim Sha Tsui (1$, one of the cheapest and most memorable rides in the world), Temple Street Night Market at Yau Ma Tei. For food: dim sum at Tim Ho Wan (the world's cheapest Michelin-starred), wonton noodles at Mak's Noodle. Lantau for the Big Buddha and the Tai O fishing village. Macao 1h by ferry for the casinos.
seoul:en: Seoul concentrates 50% of South Korea's population (25 million in the urban area). May and June are my favourite months (8.4-8.5/10): 12-27°C, 27-34% rain, end of spring with azaleas and roses at Changgyeonggung. From July to August, it's the monsoon (changma): 52-56% rainfall, oppressive humidity. From December to February, expect -6/3°C and 10-16% rain: dry cold, skiing possible 1h by bus (Yongpyong, Vivaldi Park). October-November are my real sweet spots: 9-25°C, clear sky, momiji in the palaces and the hutongs. Highlights: 5 Joseon palaces (Gyeongbokgung, Changdeokgung with its Secret Garden, Changgyeonggung, Deoksugung, Gyeonghuigung), Bukchon Hanok Village, Insadong, DMZ (demilitarised zone 1h away, mandatory tour). Districts: Gangnam (luxury, K-pop), Hongdae (student), Itaewon (international), Myeongdong (shopping). Try bibimbap, samgyeopsal (Korean barbecue), tteokbokki, soju. T-money card for metro/bus.
hanoi:en: Hanoi, Vietnamese capital, keeps its French colonial charm mixed with Asian buzz: 6 million inhabitants, 8 million scooters. October-November are my favourite months (7.8-8.1/10): 19-29°C, 28-44% rain, mild temperatures, golden light on Hoan Kiem Lake. From June to August, it's the monsoon: 60-80% rainfall, oppressive humidity (90%), heat (26-33°C). From December to March, expect 15-26°C: characteristic drizzle (light but persistent), cool morning temperatures. Highlights: Old Quarter (36 streets) where each street was dedicated to a trade (silk street, paper street, etc.), Hoan Kiem Lake and its temple, Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum, Temple of Literature (Vietnam's first university), French Quarter. Water puppet theatre (local show). Try pho bo (at Pho Gia Truyen), bun cha (Obama popularised it at Bun Cha Huong Lien), banh mi, egg coffee. Halong Bay 3h30 by bus for 1-3 day cruises. Sapa 6h on the night train for terraced rice paddies.
naples:en: Naples is Italy's densest, most chaotic, most alive city. June is my favourite month (9.4/10): 18-27°C, 18% rain, terraces on the Lungomare, ice cream at Gelateria della Scimmia. July-August drop to 6.9/10 due to humid heatwave (22-31°C): Neapolitans fleeing to Capri or Ischia. From December to February, expect 6-15°C and 34-39% rain: nativity scenes in Spaccanapoli (San Gregorio Armeno = the year-round nativity street), pizzas at L'Antica Pizzeria da Michele (the most famous, huge queues) or Sorbillo. Pompeii 30 min by Circumvesuviana train (allow a full day), Herculaneum less crowded but more impressive, Vesuvius by bus from Pompeii to the summit (1h walk). Capri 50 min by hydrofoil, Procida and Ischia more local alternatives. Safety: the Spanish Quarters are lively in the day but warrant caution at night. May and September-October are the real sweet spots tourism/weather.
bologne:en: Bologna, capital of Emilia-Romagna in Italy: 390,000 inhabitants in the heart of the Po plain. Nicknamed "the fat, the red, the learned": the fat as Italy's gastronomic capital (according to many Italians), the red for its ochre-red tile roofs and historically left-wing politics, the learned as home of the University of Bologna = the oldest still-active university in the Western world (founded 1088). June is my favourite month (9.2/10): 18-29°C, 24% rain, ideal temperatures before the heatwave. July-August stay good (8.6-8.8/10).
