Major sites / Regions: Sabah Malaysia (the most touristically accessible region), Mt Kinabalu in Kota Kinabalu Sabah capital (UNESCO 2000, Borneo's highest point at 4095m, one of the most accessible peaks in South-East Asia, 2-day ascent with overnight at Laban Rata refuge 3270m, 90% success rate, exceptional summit panorama), Sepilok Orangutan Rehabilitation Centre (Sabah, sanctuary for orangutans rescued from poaching and deforestation, observation during feeding 10h-15h, signature Borneo experience), Kinabatangan River Cruise (Sabah, cruise on the Kinabatangan river = pristine jungle with orangutans, proboscis monkeys, Bornean pygmy elephants, crocodiles, hornbills 8 species, signature dusk outing), Sipadan Island (UNESCO Tentative, top 5 globally for diving according to Cousteau who declared it "an untouched piece of art" in 1989, maximum 200 visitors/day for preservation, hammerhead sharks + green turtles by the hundreds + signature barracuda tornado), Sarawak Malaysia (the other Malaysian region of Borneo): Mulu Caves UNESCO 2000 (one of the world's largest limestone cave networks, Deer Cave 174m × 122m the world's largest underground gallery, Sarawak Chamber 700 × 396 × 70m the world's largest underground chamber by volume, dusk exodus of 3 million bats signature visual), Kuching Sarawak capital ("city of cats" in Malay, preserved atmosphere, indigenous Iban people), Bako National Park (signature Bornean proboscis monkeys). Brunei (already covered with Bandar Seri Begawan), Kalimantan Indonesia (very little tourism, pristine jungle, orangutans at Tanjung Puting NP). Activities: Sipadan diving (advance booking 6 months minimum, top globally), jungle trekking with Iban guides (Sarawak), orangutan tracking, Kinabatangan cruise. Safety: south Borneo near Philippines Sulu border not advised due to Abu Sayyaf piracy/terrorism, but the rest of Borneo is safe. Try Sarawak laksa (signature spicy noodle soup), nasi lemak, chicken rice, durian (local fruit), tuak (Iban rice wine). Kota Kinabalu (BKI) + Kuching (KCH) airports.
kuala-lumpur:en: Kuala Lumpur is Malaysia's capital, a cosmopolitan city (Malays, Chinese, Indians), with its Petronas Twin Towers (452m). January-February are my favourite months (5.9-6.0/10): 24-32°C, 64-77% rain, this is the north-east dry season, more bearable humidity. From March to May and October to November, these are the monsoons: 76-96% rainfall in afternoon tropical showers. KL never tops 6/10: equatorial climate too hot-humid. Highlights: Petronas Twin Towers (skybridge on the 41st floor and observatory on the 86th, booking required), Batu Caves (272 steps, giant gilded statue of Murugan), KL Tower (360° view), Chinatown Petaling Street, Little India Brickfields. Hawker culture but less developed than in Singapore or Penang. Try nasi lemak (national dish), char kway teow, satay, durian (June-August season, very strong smell). KL is cheaper than Singapore: meals 4-8 RM (1-2€) at hawkers, 4-star hotel 50-100€. Direct flight from Paris in 13h.
pondicherry:en: Pondicherry (Puducherry since 2006), former French trading post in south-east India on the Coromandel coast: 245,000 inhabitants. French trading post from 1674 to 1962 (288 years, the longest continuous French presence in India, exchanged with the Indians at independence in 1962). Unique tropical-colonial atmosphere with a preserved French quarter, signs in French, French cafés-crêperies, old street names in French (Rue Romain Rolland, Rue Suffren). Model city of Auroville nearby (community utopia). February is my favourite month (9.1/10): 23-29°C, 10% rain, this is the ideal dry season. January stays excellent (8.8/10). From November to March, it's the tourist high season: 22-30°C, sea at 26°C. From April to June, expect 27-34°C: pre-monsoon heatwave. From July to October, it's the south-west then north-east monsoon: 45-66% rainfall, rough sea, tropical storms. Highlights: French quarter ("White Town", geometrically gridded à la française, yellow-and-white-coloured houses with balconies, bougainvilleas, pedestrian streets), Promenade (Goubert Avenue along the sea, 1.5 km, 4m Gandhi statue, Mediterranean evening atmosphere), Sri Aurobindo Ashram (created 1926 by the Indian philosopher-revolutionary Sri Aurobindo and his French companion "The Mother" Mirra Alfassa from Paris, world-famous spiritual site), Auroville 12 km to the north ("City of Dawn", utopian community founded 1968 by The Mother with UNESCO, 3000 inhabitants of 60 nationalities living without money, religion or government, Matrimandir = 29m geodesic golden sphere = the spiritual centre, limited access), Sacred Heart of Jesus Church (19th-century Gothic with signature yellow façade), Notre-Dame des Anges, Notre-Dame de la Conception, Goubert Market (spices, fruits, flowers), Tamil quarter ("Black Town" colourful and chaotic, contrast with the gridded White Town), Paradise Beach and Auroville Beach (15 km away, cleaner than the urban Pondi beach). Try Franco-Tamil fusion cuisine (famous in Pondicherry): Pondicherry fish curry (with Creole influences), French-style cassoulet in some restaurants, butter croissants, French coffee that stands up, French baguette, Indian wines. Logistics: Chennai airport (MAA) 2h30 by bus/taxi, alternative Pondicherry airport (PNY) with limited domestic flights.
yogyakarta:en: Yogyakarta, cultural capital of Java island in Indonesia: 420,000 inhabitants (3.6 million in the urban area), the only province still officially ruled by a reigning sultan (the Sultanate of Yogyakarta = monarchic exception within the Republic of Indonesia since 1755). Locals call it "Jogja". Historic capital of Javanese culture (batik, dance, gamelan), gateway to Indonesia's 2 largest Buddhist-Hindu temples: Borobudur and Prambanan. From June to September are my favourite months (6.9/10): 21-31°C, 33-54% rain, this is the ideal dry season. Yogyakarta never tops 6.9/10 (hot humid tropical climate). From May to October, it's the dry season: 22-31°C, this is the tourist high season. From November to April, it's the rainy season: 76-95% rainfall, oppressive humidity, but stable temperatures. Highlights: Borobudur Temple (UNESCO 1991, 40 km to the north-west, the world's largest Buddhist temple, built 9th century Sailendra dynasty, rediscovered and restored by the British in 1814 then the Dutch and UNESCO in the 1970s, 3D mandala in black volcanic stone with 504 Buddha statues over 9 levels, view at sunrise 4am from Punthuk Setumbu observation point, access to the temple itself limited since 2022), Prambanan Temple (UNESCO 1991, 17 km to the east, the largest Hindu temple complex in Indonesia, 9th century, Sanjaya dynasty, dedicated to the Shiva-Vishnu-Brahma trinity, 8 main temples + 240 smaller shrines, view at sunset), Yogyakarta Kraton (palace of the reigning sultan, traditional Javanese complex, still home to Sultan Hamengkubuwono X), Taman Sari Water Castle ("water castle", former 18th-century royal complex of baths and gardens), Mount Merapi (active volcano 2930m, one of the most active in the world, last major eruption 2010 with 350 deaths, night hike with guide for sunrise 4am = intense experience), Mount Bromo 8h by bus (active volcano in eastern Java, spectacular caldera panorama at sunrise), Malioboro Street (main shopping street, batik, night market, becak cycle-rickshaws), Pasar Beringharjo (traditional central market since 1758). Cultural activities: batik workshops (Yogyakarta = world capital of batik, the craft has been UNESCO-listed since 2009), Ramayana ballet shows at Prambanan in the open air with the temple lit up in the background (May-October, 250 dancers and gamelan orchestra), gamelan, wayang kulit shadow puppet theatre (UNESCO 2003). Try gudeg (sweet green-jackfruit curry, signature Jogja dish), bakpia (mung-bean pastry), nasi goreng, sate ayam. Yogyakarta International airport (YIA) 45 min away since 2020.
pokhara:en: Pokhara, Nepal's 2nd city 200 km west of Kathmandu: 425,000 inhabitants at 800m altitude at the foot of the Annapurna massif. Nicknamed "Nepal's tourist capital", main base for Annapurna treks. Spectacular view of Machapuchare 6993m ("Fishtail Mountain", sacred peak never climbed), Annapurna I 8091m (10th highest summit in the world), Annapurna South 7219m. April is my favourite month (8.9/10): 15-27°C, 25% rain, ideal temperatures before the monsoon. March-October-November stay excellent (8.5/10). From March to May and October-November, these are the best trekking seasons: 6-27°C, clear skies. From June to September, it's the monsoon: 69-97% rainfall, summits hidden by mist. From December to February, expect 6-21°C: winter cold, high-altitude passes closed. Highlights: Phewa Lake (Phewa Tal, Pokhara's signature alpine lake at 800m, colourful "doonga" boat rides with Annapurna views, early morning at 6am the summits reflect perfectly in the flat water), Tal Barahi Temple (small Hindu temple on an islet in the middle of Phewa lake, accessible by boat), World Peace Pagoda (Shanti Stupa, white Buddhist stupa at 1100m on Anadu hill, 1h hike from the lake, 360° panorama over Pokhara + Annapurna), Sarangkot at 1600m (classic observation point for sunrise over the Annapurna at 5am, access by taxi 1h or 3h hike, signature visual with Machapuchare lit up in gold-pink), International Mountain Museum (museum on Himalayan mountaineering and the mountain peoples Sherpa-Gurung-Magar-Tamang), Devi's Falls (waterfall plunging into an underground sinkhole), Mahendra Caves and Bat Cave, Begnas Lake and Rupa Lake (quieter alternative lakes), Gurkha Memorial Museum (museum about the Nepali Gurkha soldiers famous in the British Army since 1815). Activities: paragliding (Pokhara is one of the world's most prestigious paragliding sites, flights from Sarangkot 1600m with landing on Phewa lakeside, view of the Annapurna during the flight = signature experience, October-April mainly, tandem 70-100 USD/person 30 min flight), short hikes: Poon Hill 3-4 days = the most accessible trek in Nepal (3210m), Mardi Himal 5 days, Annapurna Base Camp 10-12 days starting from Pokhara, rafting on Trisuli or Seti rivers, zipline (one of the longest and steepest in the world at 1.8 km descent 600m). Lakeside district ("Baidam", lakefront with hotels, restaurants and shops for trekkers, cosmopolitan atmosphere). Try dal bhat (national dish), momos, thukpa, gundruk (fermented leaf-vegetable), local Everest beer. Logistics: Pokhara 7h by bus from Kathmandu or 25 min by plane (panoramic flight with Himalaya view), Pokhara International airport (PKR) opened 2023.
kandy:en: Kandy, Sri Lanka's 2nd city in the heart of the central mountains: 125,000 inhabitants at 500m altitude. Last Sinhalese royal capital (1469-1815 before the British conquest, the Kandyan kingdom heroically resisting the Portuguese then Dutch for 300 years), cultural and religious capital of Sinhalese Buddhism. UNESCO 1988 for the sacred city. January-February are my favourite months (8.7/10): 20-29°C, 29% rain, this is the ideal dry season. December stays excellent (7.9/10). From January to April, it's the relative dry season: 20-31°C. From May to October, it's the south-west monsoon: 69-83% rainfall, but more bearable temperatures (22-28°C, cooler than Colombo thanks to the altitude). From October to December, it's the 2nd inter-monsoon. Permanent climate: Kandy never exceeds 31°C in average maximum and rarely drops below 20°C in average minimum, a major advantage of tropical altitude ("Eternal Spring" climate similar to Da Lat in Vietnam). Highlights: Temple of the Tooth (Sri Dalada Maligawa, Temple of the Tooth, the most sacred place in Sinhalese Buddhism, houses the relic of the Buddha's left tooth arrived in Sri Lanka in the 4th century AD, major pilgrimage site, free access for foreigners, strict dress code shoulders/legs covered, the tooth is never visible to the faithful — in a gold reliquary itself within 7 concentric reliquaries — but the outer reliquary can be seen at the 3 daily "poojas" 5:30-9:30-18:30, mystical atmosphere with drums, chants, candles, lotus offerings), Kandy Lake (artificial lake created by the last king in 1807, 4 km walk around), Royal Botanical Gardens of Peradeniya 6 km away (one of Asia's most beautiful botanical gardens, 60 hectares, founded 1843 by the British on a former royal Sinhalese garden, royal palms, orchids, cinnamon-tree avenues), Bahirawakanda Vihara Buddha Statue (giant 26m white Buddha on the hill to the north, panorama over Kandy), Kandy Cultural Show (traditional Sinhalese Kandyan dance show, fire walking, every evening 17:30-18:30 in several tourist halls 1500-2500 LKR ≈ 5-8€), Kandy Central Market (spices, tropical fruits), Esala Perahera = great annual festival of the Temple of the Tooth (July-August by lunar calendar, 10 nights of illuminated processions with 100 caparisoned elephants in gold-red, dancers, jugglers, the largest religious festival in South Asia with 1 million spectators, one of the world's most impressive cultural shows). Excursions: Sigiriya 2h30 away (UNESCO, "Lion Rock" 200m fortress-palace on a rock outcrop 5th century, one of Sri Lanka's most visited sites), Polonnaruwa 3h away (UNESCO, former 12th-century medieval capital), Anuradhapura 3h away (UNESCO, oldest Sri Lankan capital 4th century BC), Dambulla Cave Temple 2h away (UNESCO, frescoed Buddhist cave temples), Ella 6h away by spectacular train (high mountains), Nuwara Eliya tea plantations 2h away ("Little England", Ceylon tea, English climate at 1900m altitude). Try Sri Lankan rice and curry (plate with 5-7 different curries), kottu roti, hopper, fish ambul thiyal, curd and treacle (buffalo yoghurt with palm sugar syrup), local Ceylon tea. Direct Colombo-Kandy train 3h (one of Asia's most scenic).
quito:en: Quito, Ecuadorian capital: 2850m altitude, the best-preserved colonial old town in South America (UNESCO 1978). July-August are my favourite months (7.7/10): 7-19°C, 32-36% rain, equatorial dry season, clear skies. From January to May, it's the rainy season: 79-93% rainfall in afternoon showers (mornings often sunny). The altitude is felt: climb the steep slopes of Old Quito gently, hydration. Highlights: Historic centre (Plaza Grande, Cathedral, La Compañía de Jesús covered in gold, San Francisco), El Panecillo (41m aluminium Virgin statue, valley view), TelefériQo (cable car to Cruz Loma at 4100m), Mitad del Mundo (geographic equator monument, 30 min away, two versions: official monument and the INRG site of the "real" equator). Otavalo market 2h away on Saturdays (the largest indigenous market in South America). Cotopaxi (5897m volcano) 1h30 away for the climb (1 night at the José Rivas refuge). Safety: Old Quito demands vigilance at night (touristy Mariscal/La Mariscal with evening atmosphere). Quito often serves as transit before/after the Galápagos. Try ceviche de chochos, llapingachos, locro de papa, hornado.
panama:en: Panama, Central American country between Costa Rica and Colombia linking North and South America: 4.4 million inhabitants. Capital Panama City. Country unique in the world thanks to the Panama Canal = 80 km linking the Atlantic Ocean to the Pacific Ocean, one of the world's engineering masterpieces (built 1904-1914 by the USA after the French Lesseps failure 1881-1889 = 22,000 dead, plus the Panama Affair French political scandal), widened 2007-2016 for Neo-Panamax ships, one of the world's most strategic trade routes (5% of global maritime trade). January is my favourite month (6.9/10): 24-30°C, 32% rain, this is the ideal dry season. Panama never tops 6.9/10 (humid hot equatorial tropical climate year-round). From December to April, it's the dry season "verano": 24-32°C, sea at 27°C, this is the tourist high season. From May to November, it's the green season "invierno": 62-98% rainfall, stable temperatures 24-30°C, greener landscapes. Highlights: Panama Canal (the country's #1 attraction, visits possible at the Miraflores Locks on the Pacific side 30 min from Panama City with modern observation centre and museum, watching a Neo-Panamax cargo cross the lock in 8 min = signature experience, alternative Agua Clara locks on the Caribbean side or intermediate Pedro Miguel locks, partial or full canal cruise possible), Casco Viejo ("old town" UNESCO 1997, old 17th-18th century colonial quarter partly restored as the trendy "Panama trendy" district since 2010, Plaza de la Independencia, Iglesia San José with gilded altar 17th century = signature visual preserved from the sacking by Henry Morgan in 1671), Panama City Skyline (modern 2000-2020 skyscrapers nicknamed "Latin America's Dubai", 50+ buildings 200m+, one of Latin America's most impressive skylines), Biomuseo (Frank Gehry architecture 2014, biodiversity museum), Panama Viejo (UNESCO, remains of the first Panama founded 1519 and destroyed by Henry Morgan in 1671, ruins in the heart of the modern city), Albrook Mall (one of Latin America's largest shopping centres), Cinta Costera (panoramic seaside promenade). Essential excursions: Bocas del Toro 1h by plane or 12h by bus (Caribbean archipelago of 9 coral islands in the north-west, Afro-Creole English-speaking Caribbean atmosphere, turquoise beaches, surf-snorkelling-diving, the most touristic of Panamanian sites outside the canal), San Blas / Guna Yala 1h by car + lancha (autonomous archipelago of 365 paradisiacal islets on the Caribbean coast administered by the Guna Yala indigenous people = matriarchal people preserved from modern changes, access limited to tourists to preserve the culture, stay in thatched huts on tiny islets with white sand + coconut trees + turquoise sea = unique traditional Caribbean experience worldwide, high price 100-200 USD/day full board but unforgettable), Boquete 6h by bus (Highlands mountains at 1200m, Volcán Barú 3475m, Geisha coffee plantations = among the world's best coffees, Panamanian Cup of Excellence, hiking), El Valle de Antón 2h away (another mountain area), Pearl Islands southern Pacific, Coiba National Park (UNESCO marine, diving). Mayan sites: Panama has no Mayan sites (geographic boundary of the Maya world = Costa Rica-Panama). Try sancocho (chicken+yuca soup, national dish), ropa vieja (shredded beef), patacones (fried plantain), ceviche, Geisha coffee (Boquete origin), seco (local eau-de-vie), Atlas/Balboa/Panama beers. Panama Tocumen airport (PTY) Copa Airlines hub for Latin America.
galapagos:en: The Galápagos is the Ecuadorian archipelago 1000 km from the mainland: 13 main islands, 200 endemic species (Darwin drew his theory of evolution here in 1835). January is my favourite month (7.8/10): 22-26°C, 68% rain in brief showers, warmer sea (24°C). From December to May, it's the hot/wet season: often sunny sky between showers, calm and warm sea (26°C), ideal for diving. From June to November, it's the dry/cold season: Humboldt Current bringing the cool (18-23°C), frequent "garúa" mist, rough cold sea (18-22°C, useful wetsuit for diving). Penguin and albatross season (Española). Logistics: flight Quito or Guayaquil → Baltra or San Cristóbal (2h, 350-500$ return). National park entrance fee 200$ + transit card 20$. Cruises 4-8 days (1500-5000$ per person) or land-based stay (Santa Cruz/Puerto Ayora or San Cristóbal/Baquerizo Moreno) with day trips (200-400$/person). Wildlife: marine iguanas, giant tortoises (Charles Darwin Centre), sea lions, blue-footed boobies, manta rays, hammerhead sharks. Sites: Bartolomé (Pinnacle Rock), Floreana (Post Office Bay correspondence), Española (albatrosses April-December), Genovesa (birds). Controlled tourism: 240,000 visitors/year max.
iguazu:en: Iguazu, the world's most impressive falls (UNESCO): 275 cascades over 2.7 km, 80m high, Argentina-Brazil border. September is my favourite month (8.8/10): 18-28°C, 28% rain, powerful flow after southern rains, mild temperatures. From June to August is the tourist high season: 15-28°C, 19-31% rain, ideal to visit but massive crowds. From December to February (austral summer), expect 23-32°C and 47-54% rain: stifling heat (tropical humidity), but maximum flow after the rains. Argentine side (Iguazú National Park, 80% of the falls): 3 circuits (Lower 1300m, Upper 1750m, Garganta del Diablo 1100m, ecological train included). Plan a full day. Pueblo Puerto Iguazú as base. Brazilian side (Foz do Iguaçu): a single 1200m panoramic walkway, more spectacular overall view but a briefer experience (3h). Seeing both sides is ideal (plan 3 days minimum). Excursions: Macuco Safari on the Brazilian side (boat that goes under the falls), Gran Aventura on the Argentine side (equivalent), helicopter flight from Brazil (160$, banned on the Argentine side to preserve wildlife). Triple frontier Argentina-Brazil-Paraguay (Ciudad del Este for duty-free shopping). Airports: IGR (Argentina) and IGU (Brazil), 30 min by bus to the parks. Flight from Buenos Aires in 1h30.
patagonie:en: Argentine Patagonia is the southern tip of the South American continent: endless steppes, glaciers (Perito Moreno), mountains (Fitz Roy, Cerro Torre), the end of the world (Ushuaia). January is my favourite month (7.9/10): 8-19°C, 31% rain, austral summer, 17h days, glaciers accessible, treks doable (W and O at Torres del Paine on the Chilean side). December-February are the tourist high season: prices x2-x3, refuges fully booked 6 months ahead for Torres del Paine. From June to August (austral winter), expect -1/4°C with 18-21% rain: intense cold, but this is ski season at Cerro Castor (Ushuaia) or Cerro Catedral (Bariloche). Many hotels and restaurants close out of season. October-November and March-April are the real sweet spots: still acceptable weather, reduced crowds, lower prices. Highlights: El Calafate (base for Perito Moreno, glacier shuttle, Spegazzini sailing), El Chaltén (capital of Argentine trekking, Laguna de los Tres at the foot of Fitz Roy 11 km, Laguna Torre 9 km), Ushuaia ("end of the world", Tierra del Fuego national park, Beagle Channel), Puerto Madryn (Valdés Peninsula, whales June-December, penguins, orcas). Chilean side: Torres del Paine (W trek 4-5 days, O trek 7-9 days). Try Argentine asado, cordero patagonico, Malbec wine.
medellin:en: Medellín, in Colombia, has gone from "the world's most dangerous city" (1991, 6500 homicides/year) to a model of social urbanism (cable cars to the comunas, metro). January is my favourite month (8.1/10): 16-26°C, 51% rain, permanent spring climate (the city is called "la ciudad de la eterna primavera" because it sits at 1500m altitude). From June to August, expect 16-27°C with 57-74% rain: constant mild climate. From February to May and September to November, these are the rainy seasons: 70-89% rainfall in afternoon showers (mornings often sunny). Highlights: Comuna 13 (former most violent district, transformed by street art and outdoor escalators, guided tour recommended), Plaza Botero (23 sculptures by Fernando Botero), Museo de Antioquia, Pueblito Paisa (replica of an Antioquian village on Cerro Nutibara), El Poblado (trendy district, restaurants, nightlife at Parque Lleras), Laureles (more local and quiet alternative). Excursions: Guatapé 2h away (colourful stones, El Peñón with 740 steps for the panoramic view), Santa Fe de Antioquia 1h30 away (colonial town), Coffee triangle 4h away (haciendas, UNESCO coffee plantations). Try bandeja paisa (hearty dish: beans, rice, meat, plantain, egg), arepas, chocolate caliente. Major digital nomad destination: coworkings, international community.
bogota:en: Bogotá, capital of Colombia: 8 million inhabitants at 2640m altitude on the Andean cordillera, one of the world's highest capitals (after La Paz, Quito, Sucre). "Eternal spring" climate without marked seasons. September is my favourite month (8.0/10): 8-20°C, 31% rain, often clear sky. From December to March, it's the dry season: 8-20°C, 31-49% rain, stable temperatures (Bogotá never leaves the 8-20°C range, regardless of the month). From March to May and October-November, it's the rainy season: 54-79% rainfall, frequent afternoon showers. All year round, the altitude (2640m) sometimes causes soroche (mild altitude sickness) on the first days — drink local coca tea or avoid alcohol/effort initially. Highlights: La Candelaria (old colonial district, colourful alleys with graffiti, bohemian atmosphere), Plaza Bolívar (cathedral, Palace of Justice with pavilions, Capitol), Monserrate (3152m, 7-min funicular or cable car, 360° panorama over the city and the cordillera, pilgrimage sanctuary), Gold Museum (Museo del Oro, one of the world's largest collections of pre-Columbian goldsmithing, 34,000 gold pieces, the unmissable in Bogotá), Botero Museum (works by Fernando Botero given to the city, signature round paintings and sculptures), Usaquén district (Sunday flea market, trendy restaurants in the north). Excursions: Salt Cathedral of Zipaquirá 50 km to the north (Catholic cathedral carved into a salt mine 200m underground), Lake Guatavita (origin of the legend of El Dorado), Cocora Valley 4h by bus (60m wax palms). Safety: Bogotá demands vigilance (snatch thefts especially on public transport, official Easy Taxi/Uber recommended). Try ajiaco (potato-chicken-corn soup), arepa, tamales, Colombian coffee (one of the world's best, drink at Juan Valdez or Café San Alberto).
salvador-de-bahia:en: Salvador (de Bahia) is the African soul of Brazil: 3 million inhabitants, Brazil's 1st capital (until 1763), Afro-Brazilian heritage (capoeira, candomblé, bossa nova). October is my favourite month (8.2/10): 24-28°C, 54% rain, end of the wet season. From May to August (austral winter), expect 22-26°C and 71-80% rain: often grey skies, rough sea, but bearable temperatures and reduced prices. From December to March (austral summer), expect 25-29°C: tropical heat, sea at 28°C, but also Salvador Carnival (the world's largest in terms of people, 6 days in February, Olodum/Ilê Aiyê Afro blocos in the streets, prices x4). Highlights: Pelourinho (UNESCO, colourful colonial old town, Largo do Pelourinho), Igreja de São Francisco (rococo covered in gold, one of Latin America's most beautiful), Elevador Lacerda (72m lift between upper and lower town), Mercado Modelo (Afro-Brazilian crafts). Beaches: Porto da Barra (urban), Ondina, Stella Maris (further away). Itacaré and Morro de São Paulo 2-4h away for wild beaches. Safety: Pelourinho touristy by day but demands vigilance at night, avoid visible bags, don't venture into the favelas without a guide. Try moqueca de peixe, acarajé (fried black-eyed pea balls), feijoada, caipirinha. Capoeira and candomblé in regular public performances.
sao-paulo:en: São Paulo is 12 million inhabitants (22 million with the greater metropolitan area), a vertical megalopolis, with no beach, but with the densest cultural scene in Brazil. July is my favourite month (8.7/10): 12-23°C, 8% rain, dry and cool climate (austral winter at 800m altitude). From December to March, expect 18-27°C and 59-78% precipitation: near-daily tropical storms, sometimes flooding in the centre (Avenida 9 de Julho). Pollution improves then. Avenida Paulista on Sundays (pedestrianised) remains the most paulistana experience. MASP (Lina Bo Bardi) for modern art, Pinacoteca, Mercado Municipal for the giant mortadella sandwich. Liberdade (the 1st Japanese diaspora outside Japan) on Sundays for its market. Beco do Batman (street art in Vila Madalena). Safety demands vigilance: avoid public transport late, Uber abundant and cheap. Direct flights from Europe (Air France, TAP, Alitalia, Lufthansa).
florianopolis:en: Florianópolis ("Floripa" to Brazilians) is the capital of the state of Santa Catarina in southern Brazil: main island (Ilha de Santa Catarina) connected to the mainland by 2 bridges, 500,000 inhabitants, 42 beaches, surf, young/trendy atmosphere. April is my favourite month (8.1/10): 20-25°C, 42% rain, end of the austral summer, mild temperatures. From December to March (austral summer), expect 22-28°C: Brazilian and Argentine tourist peak (Argentinians come en masse, hotel prices x3-x4, packed beaches). From June to August (austral winter), expect 14-21°C and 21-28% rain: cool (sometimes chilly mornings), but sun often delivers, prices halved or thirded, quieter and more local atmosphere. Highlights: Beaches Joaquina (surf, dunes), Praia Mole (lively, young), Praia da Galheta (naturist), Lagoinha do Leste (wild, accessible 1h walk), Praia dos Ingleses (family), Barra da Lagoa (charming), Lagoa da Conceição (lagoon at the centre, kitesurf, evenings). Historic centre on the mainland side (Mercado Público, Catedral). Anhatomirim Island (fortress, dolphins). Surf: Florianópolis regularly hosts World Surf League stops. Try ostras (local oysters, the island is one of Brazil's largest producers), camarão na moranga (prawns in pumpkin), açaí. Major digital nomad destination in Brazil.
el-nido:en: El Nido, municipality at the far north of Palawan in the Philippines: 40,000 inhabitants facing the Bacuit archipelago (45 karst islands), considered one of the most beautiful natural settings in the world. Emerald waters, vertical limestone cliffs, hidden lagoons, deserted beaches. January is my favourite month (6.9/10): 25-29°C, 47% rain, this is the ideal dry season. El Nido never tops 6.9/10 (hot tropical climate, 8 months of monsoon a year). From November to May, it's the tourist high season: 25-32°C, sea at 28°C. From June to October, it's the south-west monsoon: 73-89% rainfall, rough sea, some island-hopping tours cancelled, many hotels close. Highlights / Island-hopping tours: Tour A = grand classic (Big Lagoon, Small Lagoon, Secret Lagoon, Shimizu Island, Seven Commando Beach), Tour B (Snake Island with serpentine sandbar, Pinagbuyutan Island, Cathedral Cave, Cudugnon Cave, Entalula Beach), Tour C (Hidden Beach, Matinloc Shrine, Secret Beach accessible by crawling through an underwater cave at low tide, Helicopter Island, Star Beach), Tour D (Cadlao Island, Natnat Beach, Bukal Beach, Ipil Beach). Plan 1500-1800 PHP/person (25-30€) with BBQ lunch on the beach, 8h on the water in a bangka. Big Lagoon = signature El Nido visual, turquoise lagoon between 2 cliffs 200m, paddleboard or kayak (200 PHP rental), daily visitor numbers limited. Nacpan Beach 30 min by tricycle (4 km of white sand, one of the world's most beautiful beaches according to Condé Nast), Las Cabanas Beach (the closest to El Nido Town, ideal for sunset), Marimegmeg Beach, Duli Beach (surfers Nov-March). Activities: diving (PADI Open Water around 17,000 PHP ≈ 280€), kayak, paddleboard, climbing on the cliffs, ATV to Nacpan. Nightlife: Trattoria Altrove (pizza), Republika Sunset Bar, fire shows at Las Cabanas. Audience: backpackers in the majority, young 20-35, Asian tourists (Koreans, Chinese) and Europeans (French in particular). Logistics: El Nido airport (ENI) with direct flights from Manila (1h15 by AirSWIFT 250€ return), alternative van from Puerto Princesa (5-6h, 600 PHP) or overnight bus (8h).
palawan:en: Palawan, the largest province of the Philippines: an elongated 450 km island at the western tip of the archipelago, 800,000 inhabitants. Regularly ranked "most beautiful island in the world" by travel magazines (Travel + Leisure, Condé Nast Traveler). Capital: Puerto Princesa. Turquoise waters, limestone cliffs, hidden lagoons, exceptional marine biodiversity. March is my favourite month (8.1/10): 22-28°C, 55% rain, this is the ideal dry season. From January to April, it's the dry season: 22-29°C, sea at 27°C. From May to October, it's the south-west monsoon (Habagat): 71-95% rainfall, rough sea on the west (El Nido difficult to access), Coron on the north-east side more sheltered, many hotels close. Cyclone season June-November, but Palawan is less affected than the Visayas (Cebu) or Luzon (Manila). Highlights: Puerto Princesa Underground River (UNESCO, one of the 7 New Natural Wonders of the World 2012, navigable underground river of 8.2 km one of the world's longest, 80 km north of Puerto Princesa, access by boat from Sabang after a 2h drive, gravity-defying cave entrance bordered with limestone), El Nido in the north (see dedicated review), Coron in the north (see dedicated review), Port Barton (bohemian alternative to El Nido, quieter, less developed), Honda Bay (1-day excursion from Puerto Princesa with island hopping Cowrie/Luli/Pandan), Sabang Beach (gateway to the Underground River), Nacpan Beach in El Nido (4 km of white sand, one of the world's most beautiful beaches according to Condé Nast). Tubbataha Reefs excursions (UNESCO, marine reserve park among the world's largest, accessible only by liveaboard March-June, diving only, among the best diving spots worldwide). Activities: island hopping (Palawan's main activity, 3-7 islands per day in outrigger bangka, BBQ lunch on a deserted beach, snorkelling), diving at Coron (WWII Japanese wrecks), kayaking in the mangrove at El Nido, climbing on the karst peaks. Try kinilaw (Filipino ceviche), tamilok (mangrove worm, a local delicacy heavily contested by tourists), pancit, balut (fertilised duck egg), pinakbet, Filipino mangoes (the world's best according to Filipinos). Logistics: main airport Puerto Princesa (PPS), secondary airports El Nido (ENI) and Busuanga/Coron (USU), access from Manila in 1h15-1h30 direct flight.
cebu:en: Cebu, capital of the Visayas region in the Philippines: 1 million inhabitants (3 million in the urban area), the Philippines' 2nd largest city after Manila, former Spanish colonial capital (Magellan arrived here in 1521 before being killed a few weeks later in nearby Mactan). Base for exploring the most beautiful Philippine islands (Bohol, Siquijor, Camotes, Malapascua). January is my favourite month (6.9/10): 24-28°C, 70% rain. Cebu never tops 6.9/10 (humid equatorial tropical climate). From December to May, it's the relatively driest season: 24-30°C, 50-70% rain, this is the tourist high season. From June to November, it's the wetter season: 83-94% rainfall, but stable temperatures (25-30°C), real cyclone risk (the Visayas were devastated by Yolanda/Haiyan in November 2013, 6300 deaths). Cebu City highlights: Magellan's Cross (cross erected by Magellan in 1521, first Christian celebration in the Philippines), Basilica del Santo Niño (the oldest church in the Philippines, 16th century), Fort San Pedro (Spanish 17th century), Taoist Temple, Tops Lookout (panoramic view). Cebu excursions: Kawasan Falls in the south (adventurous canyoneering), Moalboal (sardine run, diving), Oslob (whale shark watching, ethical controversy as whale sharks are fed to stay on site), Bantayan Island in the north. Neighbouring islands by ferry: Bohol (Chocolate Hills, tarsiers, Alona Beach at Panglao), Siquijor (mystical island of the white witches, turquoise beaches), Camotes Islands, Malapascua (thresher shark diving). Try Cebu lechon (the best roasted suckling pig in the Philippines according to Anthony Bourdain), kinilaw (ceviche), puso (rice cooked in woven leaves). Direct flight Cebu from Doha/Singapore (compulsory stopover from Europe).
boracay:en: Boracay, a small central island in the Philippines: 10 km long, 30,000 inhabitants, in Aklan province. Famous for White Beach = 4 km of powdery white sand (finely crushed coral), regularly ranked among the world's most beautiful beaches (Travel + Leisure, Condé Nast). Legendary nightlife in Asia. The island was entirely closed for 6 months in 2018 by President Duterte's decision to clean it up (sewage discharged into the sea, overbuilding, pollution), rehabilitated and stricter since October 2018. February is my favourite month (6.9/10): 25-27°C, 73% rain. Boracay never tops 6.9/10 (hot tropical climate + very rainy year-round). From November to May, it's the tourist high season: 25-30°C, sea at 28°C, hotel prices x2-x3, big crowds (Koreans and Chinese mainly, fewer Russians, Europeans on the rise). From June to October, it's the south-west monsoon (Habagat): 83-92% rainfall, rough sea on the White Beach side (waves break over the top of the beach), beach activities limited, but Bulabog Beach on the east side benefits from steady winds for kitesurf. Highlights: White Beach (4 km, divided into 3 stations: Station 1 the smartest in the north, Station 2 the liveliest with mid-range restaurants-bars-hotels in the middle, Station 3 backpackers-budget in the south), Puka Shell Beach (in the north, wilder, sand of broken shells), Bulabog Beach (east side, kitesurf and windsurf capital of Asia Nov-April), Diniwid Beach, Ilig-iligan Beach, Mount Luho (highest point 100m, panorama over the island). Activities: kitesurf and windsurf at Bulabog (steady thermal winds 15-25 knots Nov-April), parasailing, jet ski, banana boat, cliff diving at Ariel's Point (1-day excursion), helmet diving ("sea walking"), modest diving (PADI 17,000 PHP), spa, beach massage. Legendary nightlife: Epic Bar, Coco Bar, Hue Hotel rooftop, fire shows every evening on the beach, ladyboy cabarets. Restaurants: Aria (Italian), Sunny Side Café, Spider House, Real Coffee. Sunset on a paraw (traditional Filipino sailboat with orange-red sail, 30 min, 350 PHP/person, the postcard image of Boracay). Try calamansi juice, sinigang, lechon, halo-halo, fresh seafood. Logistics: Caticlan airport (MPH) the closest 10 min by tricycle + 15 min ferry, alternative Kalibo airport (KLO) 1h30 away, direct flights from Manila in 50 min.
koh-lanta:en: Koh Lanta, island on the Andaman coast in Thailand south of Krabi: 30,000 inhabitants over 30 km long, one of the calmest and most family-friendly islands in southern Thailand (alternative to neighbouring Phuket and Koh Phi Phi). Turquoise sea, long golden-sand beaches on the west coast, national park to the south (Mu Ko Lanta). From January to March are my favourite months (6.9/10): 25-32°C, 23-36% rain, this is the ideal dry season. Koh Lanta never tops 6.9/10 (hot tropical climate). From November to April, it's the tourist high season: 25-32°C, sea at 28°C, hotel prices x2-x3, ferries from Krabi/Phi Phi operating. From May to October, it's the south-west monsoon: 73-87% rainfall, many hotels close, ferries to Phi Phi cancelled regularly, some resorts only accessible by road. Highlights: Long Beach (Phra Ae) (the longest 5 km, golden sand, beach restaurants), Klong Khong (bohemian atmosphere, beach-bonfire bars at sunset), Klong Nin (family), Kantiang Bay (smart, Pimalai 5* resort), Bamboo Bay (wild, south), Old Town (Lanta Old Town on the east side, former Chinese stilt village, preserved atmosphere), Mu Ko Lanta National Park (extreme south, lighthouse, jungle hike, isolated Tanote Bay beach), Koh Rok and Koh Haa (1-day diving excursion, crystal waters). Diving and snorkelling: Koh Lanta is one of the major bases for diving on the cult sites Hin Daeng and Hin Muang (manta rays, whale sharks in good season January-April) and Koh Phi Phi. Excursions: 4 Islands tour (Koh Mai Phai-Bamboo Island, Koh Tup, Koh Poda, Phra Nang Cave), Koh Phi Phi 1h30 by speedboat. Try som tam, massaman curry, pad thai, mango sticky rice, fresh seafood. Logistics: Koh Lanta accessible from Krabi airport (KBV) in 2h via 2 roll-on ferries (the bridge to the mainland was promised but delayed), from Phuket plan 4h.
koh-chang:en: Koh Chang, Thailand's 2nd largest island after Phuket: 217 km², 5500 inhabitants in the far east of Trat province near the Cambodian border. Nicknamed "Elephant Island" (its shape suggests an elephant). 70% jungle protected by the national park. Much wilder and less touristy than Phuket or Koh Samui. December-January are my favourite months (8.8-9.1/10): 22-28°C, 16-29% rain, this is the ideal dry season. From November to March, it's the tourist high season: 22-28°C, sea at 28°C, hotel prices x2-x3. From April to October, it's the south-west monsoon: 59-98% rainfall (one of Thailand's wettest islands during the monsoon), many hotels close, beaches turn into mud streams during tropical storms. Highlights: White Sand Beach (Hat Sai Khao, the longest 3 km on the west coast, nightlife), Klong Prao (the longest 4 km, quieter), Kai Bae Beach (central point, islet view), Lonely Beach (Hat Tha Nam, backpacker-bohemian atmosphere), Bang Bao Village (stilt fishing village at the far south-west, jetty restaurants, departure point for diving and island-hopping excursions), Khlong Plu Waterfall (waterfall in the jungle, 5-min access, swimming), Salakphet Bay (wild east coast), Mu Koh Chang National Park (47 islands in the archipelago: Koh Wai, Koh Mak = quiet backpacker atmosphere, Koh Kood = the smartest and most preserved, also accessible by speedboat from Trat). Activities: diving (Hin Luk Bat, Hin Rap, HTMS Chang wreck), jungle trekking, kayaking in the mangrove at Salakphet, ethical elephant ride at Ban Kwan Chang Elephant Camp (sanctuary). Snorkelling: less spectacular than Phi Phi as the corals are less developed, but Koh Rang 1h30 by speedboat is very good. Nightlife: less developed than Phuket/Phi Phi, fire shows at Lonely Beach, beach bars at White Sand Beach. Try fresh seafood (grilled fish with salt and lime), nam prik kapi (fermented prawn sauce), spicy Trat curry. Logistics: Koh Chang 5h from Bangkok (4h bus to Trat + 30 min ferry + island transfer), no airport on the island, Trat airport (TDX) the closest.
rarotonga:en: Rarotonga is the main island of the Cook Islands: 67 km², 14,000 inhabitants, central mountain (Te Manga 653m), 32 km loop road around the island. November is my favourite month (8.2/10): 21-22°C, 38% rain, dry season. From May to October is the relative dry season (36-56% rainfall). From November to April, it's the wet season (51-66%). Highlights: Beaches Muri Beach (the most famous, calm lagoon, kayak to the motus), Aro'a Beach (snorkelling), Black Rock Beach (sunset), Cross-Island Track (6h hike across the island, Te Rua Manga "the Needle" as the highest point), Punanga Nui Market in Avarua (Saturday morning, food/crafts market), Avana Lagoon (departure for Aitutaki by boat). Activities: island bus tour (circular bus system "clockwise/anticlockwise", 1 every 30 min), scooter rental (60 NZ$/day) or car, diving and snorkelling. Cultural Village (Cook Maori culture show). Marae (sacred ancestral sites). Accommodation: no big all-inclusive Maldives-style resort, rather bungalows and family hotels at 100-300 NZ$/night. Try ika mata, taro, local pineapple. Auckland-Rarotonga flight 4h, affordable prices. Rarotonga = main low-cost alternative to Tahiti.
fidji:en: Fiji is an archipelago of 333 islands in the South Pacific: 900,000 inhabitants, 2 large islands (Viti Levu where the capital Suva is located, Vanua Levu), hundreds of smaller islands (Mamanuca and Yasawa Islands for tourism). June is my favourite month (7.7/10): 17-22°C, 47% rain, austral dry season. From May to October, it's the dry season: 47-60% rainfall, temperatures 16-25°C, sea at 25-26°C. From November to April, it's the wet season: 75-94% rainfall, heavy heat (20-26°C), real cyclone risk (Winston 2016 category 5 killed 44 people). Highlights: Mamanuca Islands (the most accessible from Nadi, day trips by boat), Yasawa Islands (wilder, further away), Coral Coast (south coast of Viti Levu, hotels), Suva (capital), Garden of the Sleeping Giant (botanical garden). Activities: diving (among the world's best, magnificent soft corals), kava ceremony (traditional drink from pepper-tree root, welcomed in villages), surf (Cloudbreak at Tavarua world-renowned). Cuisine Indo-Fijian influence (40% of Fijians of Indian origin, British colonial heritage): kokoda (raw fish in coconut milk), rourou (taro), curry. Direct flight from Sydney/Auckland in 3h, from Los Angeles in 11h.
samoa:en: Samoa (formerly Western Samoa) is an independent state in the South Pacific: 200,000 inhabitants, capital Apia on Upolu, 10 islands. Not to be confused with American Samoa (US territory). June-August are my favourite months (6.9/10): 24-28°C, 73-76% rain, relative dry season. Humid equatorial climate = Samoa never tops 7/10. From May to October, it's the relative dry season: 73-86% rainfall. From November to April, it's the wet season: 88-95% rainfall, heavy heat, cyclone risk (Evan 2012, Gita 2018). Highlights: To Sua Ocean Trench (natural pool in a collapsed cliff, the signature image of Samoa), Lalomanu Beach (beach), Robert Louis Stevenson Museum (the Treasure Island author lived and is buried here at Vailima), Lake Lanoto'o crater, Cape Mulinu'u (the "first point in the world" to see the sun after the date line change in 2011). Islands to explore: Upolu (the main one with Apia), Savai'i (the largest, lava fields, Alofaaga blowholes). Cultural villages: fa'a Samoa = traditional way of life still very alive, Sunday a sacred day (everything closed). Cuisine: palusami (taro and coconut milk), oka (raw fish), umu (cooking in the earth). Flight from Auckland (4h) or Brisbane. Far less touristy than Fiji/Tahiti, preserved atmosphere.
vanuatu:en: Vanuatu is an archipelago of 83 islands in the South Pacific: 320,000 inhabitants, capital Port Vila on Efate, former Franco-British condominium of the New Hebrides (independence 1980). Active volcanoes accessible to tourists. June-July are my favourite months (8.3/10): 22-26°C, 37-39% rain, austral dry season. From May to October, it's the relative dry season: 36-55% rainfall, temperatures 21-27°C. From November to April, it's the wet season: 55-85% rainfall, heavy heat, very high cyclone risk (Pam 2015 category 5 devastating, Harold 2020 too). Highlights: Mount Yasur on Tanna island (active volcano accessible up to the crater, an experience unique in the world, eruptions visible every minute, leave at sunset for the incandescent lava), Champagne Beach on Espiritu Santo (beach ranked among the world's most beautiful), Million Dollar Point (US WWII military equipment wrecks dumped into the sea in 1945, diving), SS President Coolidge (one of the largest wrecks accessible to recreational diving in the world). Melanesian cultural villages. Land diving at Pentecost (May-June, ancestor of bungee jumping, men dive from a wooden tower with vines tied to their ankles). Try kava (traditional drink), raw fish, lap lap (traditional pudding). Flight from Brisbane/Sydney in 3h, compulsory Auckland stopover from Europe.
cortina-dampezzo:en: Cortina d'Ampezzo, "Queen of the Dolomites": 6000 inhabitants at 1224m, premium Italian ski resort surrounded by Dolomite peaks (Tofana 3244m, Cinque Torri, Faloria). UNESCO since 2009. Co-host of the 2026 Winter Olympics with Milan. July is my favourite month (7.4/10): 11-21°C, 75% rain in altitude storms, Dolomite hiking (Tre Cime di Lavaredo the famous one, accessible from Cortina), via ferrata. From June to September, expect 8-21°C: very active summer season, climbing, mountain bike. From December to April, expect -7/9°C and 24-50% precipitation often as snow: ski high season, but moderate altitude (1224m) = sometimes patchy snow cover at the village (snow cannons needed), good conditions at altitude (Tofana 2900m, Faloria). Pistes: 120 km Cortina, 1200 km Dolomiti Superski (12 resorts linked by 1 pass), Sellaronda (26 km circuit around Sella, one of the most prized ski experiences in the world). Pistes: Olympia (1956 Olympic women's downhill), Vertigine (World Cup events). Cinque Torri = open-air museum on WWI (Italo-Austrian front). Audience: Italian aristocracy (Venetians and Milanese), film shoots (Pink Panther 1963, Cliffhanger 1993, James Bond For Your Eyes Only 1981). Hotels: Cristallo Resort, Rosapetra Spa, Miramonti Majestic (5*, 200-1500€/night). Try canederli, casunziei (beetroot ravioli), polenta, speck, Lagrein wine. Logistics: Cortina 2h30 from Venice by bus, 3h from Milan.
davos:en: Davos, Swiss ski resort and town: 11,000 inhabitants at 1560m (the highest town in Europe), known worldwide for the World Economic Forum mid-January (4 days, world heads of state and CEOs, maximum security, town locked down). August is my favourite month (7.6/10): 9-20°C, 53% rain in storms, hiking, mountain bike, golf, Davos Festival classical music. From June to September, expect 6-20°C: very active summer season (Davos is also a historic spa town, 19th-century sanatoriums for tuberculosis). From December to April, expect -9/5°C and 40-50% precipitation often as snow: this is ski high season. Davos-Klosters: 320 km of pistes spread over 6 distinct domains (Parsenn the main one, Jakobshorn for snowboarding, Pischa, Madrisa, Rinerhorn, Schatzalp), 1 single ski pass. Parsenn = famous 12 km descent piste (the world record for long pistes). Davos vs Klosters: Davos more urban (apartment-block hotels), Klosters smarter and more village-like (royal British residences). The Magic Mountain by Thomas Mann (1924) is set in a sanatorium in Davos. Audience: sporty skiers in winter, business and political circles mid-January (Forum), spa-goers in summer. Try fondue, raclette, Bündnerfleisch (Grisons cured beef). Logistics: Davos 2h30 from Zurich by train (the Glacier Express passes nearby). Davos ice rink = home of the Spengler Cup (Christmas hockey tournament since 1923).
verbier:en: Verbier, premium Swiss ski resort at the heart of the 4 Vallées: 3000 permanent inhabitants (15,000 in season), traditional village (old wooden chalets) at 1500m. Famous for its committing pistes and legendary off-piste. August is my favourite month (7.8/10): 9-20°C, 44% rain in storms, hiking and mountain bike (DH Verbier global festival late July/early August). From June to September, expect 6-20°C: summer season, paragliding, 18-hole golf (Les Esserts, the highest in Europe at 1800m), Verbier Festival classical music mid-July to early August. From December to April, expect -8/4°C and 43-58% precipitation often as snow: this is ski high season (412 km of pistes 4 Vallées Verbier+Nendaz+Veysonnaz+Thyon+La Tzoumaz). World-class off-piste: Vallon d'Arbi, Stairway to Heaven, Mont-Fort (3330m, summit of the area, glacier). Xtreme Verbier (March-April freeride competition, Freeride World Tour final, on the north face of the Bec des Rosses, one of the wildest off-piste descents in the world). Logistics: Verbier 2h from Geneva by car, 3h by train + bus from Le Châble. Very expensive: restaurant meals 30-50€, ski pass 70€/day, hotels 200-800€/night in high season. Audience: European and British ski elite (Verbier nicknamed "Britney's playground" for the English jet set), Swedish and Belgian royal teenagers. Try Valais raclette, half-and-half fondue, Valais wine (Fendant, Petite Arvine).
crans-montana:en: Crans-Montana, premium Swiss ski and golf resort in Valais: 5000 permanent inhabitants at 1500m on a sunny plateau, founded in 1893 (one of the oldest Alpine resorts), accessible by funicular from Sierre in the valley. August is my favourite month (8.1/10): 10-21°C, 34% rain, alpine hiking, Omega European Masters golf early September (one of the European Tour stops, on the Severiano Ballesteros 18-hole course). From June to September, expect 6-21°C: very active summer season (golf, hiking, Crans lake, paragliding). From December to March, expect -8/4°C and 37-43% precipitation often as snow: this is ski high season. Pistes: 140 km Crans-Montana (60 pistes, 22 lifts), altitude 1500-3000m (Plaine Morte glacier), 1500m vertical drop. Plaine Morte (3000m, glacier accessible by cable car from Violettes, summer skiing possible until July in a good year). Pistes: Crans-Montana is more oriented towards gentle cruising than technical challenge (wide pistes, record sunshine in Valais), ideal for families and seniors. Audience: Swiss-German and Romandy aristocracy, Belgians and Dutch in winter, many permanent residents (Crans-Montana is also a 4-season residential zone). Hotels: LeCrans Hotel & Spa (5*+), Guarda Golf (5*+), Royal Hotel, 200-2500€/night. Caves de la Montagne (Sierre wines, Fendant and Petite Arvine). Try raclette, half-and-half fondue (Vacherin Fribourgeois + Gruyère), tartare of cured beef, Valais wines. Logistics: Crans-Montana 2h from Geneva (Sierre by train + 12-min funicular, or by road).
gstaad:en: Gstaad, ultra-chic ski resort and retreat in the Bernese Alps in Switzerland: 7000 inhabitants at 1050m altitude. The most exclusive historic ski resort in Switzerland (with St Moritz and Zermatt), known since 1900 as a refuge of the European elite (Roger Moore lived here for 35 years, Bernie Ecclestone-Roman Polanski-Madonna own chalets here). Atmosphere of a preserved traditional alpine village thanks to a strict ban on new construction (anti-Klosters policy), no skyscrapers, renovated 17th-19th century wooden chalets. July is my favourite month (7.4/10): 11-21°C, 56% rain, ideal temperatures for alpine hiking. Gstaad never tops 7.4/10 (alpine climate with a narrow season). From June to September, it's the summer high season: 3-21°C, Bernese Oberland hiking, mountain bike, 9-hole golf, polo ("Snow Polo World Cup Gstaad" January on the frozen lake = signature competition unique in the world, major social event for the European elite). From December to March, expect -6/5°C and 38-49% precipitation often as snow: this is ski high season. Pistes: 220 km Gstaad Mountain Rides (modest vs 600 km of the 3 Vallées but elite quality), 6 linked sectors (Rinderberg, Wispile, Wasserngrat, Eggli, Glacier 3000, Saanenmöser), altitude 950-3000m, 2000m vertical drop. Glacier 3000 = signature sector at 3000m with panorama of 24 peaks including Mont Blanc visible 60 km to the west, Peak Walk by Tissot = panoramic suspension bridge between 2 peaks 107m long unique in the world 2014, panoramic Botta restaurant. Signature pistes: Tiger Run black descent with 1500m vertical drop, Glacier 3000 piste 7 km. Audience: ultra-wealthy (European royal families, celebrities, oligarchs), deliberately discreet atmosphere (paparazzi rare), highest prices in the Swiss Alps (chalet rental 30,000-100,000 CHF/week). Hotels: Park Gstaad (5*+ luxury palace 1910), Le Grand Bellevue (5*+), Gstaad Palace (the institution since 1913, legendarily hosting Hollywood Roger Moore-Audrey Hepburn-Liz Taylor), prices 800-5000+ CHF/night in high season. Excursions: Lake Geneva 1h away (Montreux-Lausanne already covered), Bern 1h30 away, Interlaken-Jungfrau 2h away, Zermatt-Matterhorn 4h away. Festival: Menuhin Festival July-September (created 1957 by violinist Yehudi Menuhin who had settled in Gstaad, signature classical festival). Try half-and-half fondue, raclette, rösti, Älplermagronen, Berner Platte, Schnaps Etter, Swiss Lavaux wines. Logistics: Gstaad accessible by panoramic Golden Pass Line train from Montreux 2h, from Geneva airport 2h30 by car.
dolomites:en: The Dolomites (UNESCO 2009) are the pale limestone of the eastern Italian Alps: high-altitude refuges, well-known via ferrate. July-August are my favourite months (4.8/10, misleading climate score because our data reflects the altitude zones where summer stays cool 12°C). In the valley (Bolzano, Cortina, Selva di Val Gardena), expect 18-25°C in midsummer. From June to September, it's hiking and via ferrata season: Tre Cime di Lavaredo (9 km loop, classic), Lagazuoi (cable car + war tunnels), Seceda (famous panorama). The CAI refuges open June to September, booking required in high season. From December to April, it's ski season: Sella Ronda (26 km ski loop around the Sella massif, 4 valleys), Cortina d'Ampezzo (2026 Olympics), Val Gardena (World Cup). May and October-November are off-seasons: refuges closed, lifts stopped, capricious weather. To avoid unless you have a specific objective. Access from Venice (2h30) or Innsbruck (1h30).
salzburg:en: Salzburg is the city of Mozart and The Sound of Music. UNESCO heritage, Austrian baroque, dominated by the Hohensalzburg fortress. June-August are my favourite months (7.9-8.0/10): 13-25°C, 51-60% rain in afternoon storms. This is the period of the Salzburg Festival (mid-July to late August), one of the world's largest opera and theatre festivals, hotel prices x3-x4. From December to February, expect -3/8°C and 45-50% rain or snow: Christmas markets on Domplatz and Residenzplatz (among Austria's most beautiful), Stille Nacht (Silent Night) sung for the first time in 1818 in a nearby village (Oberndorf 20 min away). Mozart's birthplace (Getreidegasse 9), Hohensalzburg Fortress (funicular or walk), Mirabell Gardens, Hellbrunn and its trick fountains in summer. Sound of Music tours popular (4h, 60-80€). May and September-October are the real sweet spots for tourism/weather.
isle-of-skye:en: Isle of Skye, the largest of the Inner Hebrides in Scotland: 10,000 inhabitants over 1656 km² north-west of mainland Scotland. Linked by the Skye Bridge since 1995 (before: ferry only). Spectacular Gaelic mountain landscapes ("Eilean a' Cheò" = "Isle of Mist" in Scottish Gaelic), inspiration for many films (Highlander, Prometheus, Macbeth). May is my favourite month (4.4/10): 7-12°C, 53% rain. Skye never tops 4.4/10 (extremely rainy and windy oceanic climate year-round, one of Europe's wettest destinations with 2400 mm/year, vs 600 mm in London). From May to September, it's the tourist high season: 7-15°C, more stable climate but still unpredictable (it can be 12°C and rain in July), long days (18h of daylight at the solstice). From October to April, expect 2-9°C and 53-72% rain: very wet, possible snow, few tourists. Wind: Skye is one of the windiest destinations in the UK, frequent winds 60-100 km/h. Highlights: Old Man of Storr (signature 50m rock formation in the Trotternish ridge, 4.5 km return trail 2-3h on a moderate climb, one of the most photographed places in Scotland), Quiraing (unique geological landscape of the Trotternish, landslide still moving today, 6.8 km trail, lunar landscape), Fairy Pools ("fairies' pools", succession of crystal-clear waterfalls in the Cuillins, freezing swim possible 8-10°C, 4 km trail), Fairy Glen (landscape of conical hills in geological motion near Uig, strange atmosphere), Cuillins Mountains (Black Cuillins gabbro massif and Red Cuillins granite, one of Scotland's most technical ranges, climbing and hill-walking, Mount Sgurr Alasdair 992m summit), Neist Point Lighthouse (extreme west of Skye, 1909, spectacular cliffs, sunset viewpoint), Dunvegan Castle (Scotland's oldest inhabited castle, seat of Clan MacLeod for 800 years, gardens, Fairy Flag enchanted clan banner), Eilean Donan Castle (on the road to Skye, one of the world's most photographed castles on a bridged islet, Highlander film castle), Talisker Distillery (Skye's oldest distillery 1830, characteristic smoky single malt of the island), Portree (capital of the island, harbour with colourful houses, seafood restaurants). Activities: hiking (the main draw, waterproof gear mandatory, GPS recommended as fog is frequent), landscape photography, orca and whale watching (June-September from Neist Point), sea kayaking. Try haggis (stuffed sheep's stomach), Cullen skink (Scottish fish soup), neeps and tatties (turnip-potato mash), Loch Fyne salmon, Loch Eishort oysters, Talisker single malt. Logistics: Skye 5h by car from Edinburgh or Glasgow, direct train Glasgow-Mallaig 5h then ferry 30 min, or by car via Skye Bridge.
lake-district:en: Lake District, mountainous region in north-west England in Cumbria: 40,000 inhabitants over 2300 km², national park since 1951, UNESCO 2017 for its cultural landscape. Region of the English Romantic poets: William Wordsworth was born here (1770) and lived here, along with Samuel Taylor Coleridge and Robert Southey. Beatrix Potter lived here and created Peter Rabbit. England's highest peak: Scafell Pike (978m). 16 main lakes including Windermere (the longest lake in England 17 km). May-July are my favourite months (7.0/10): 5-15°C, 47-62% rain. The Lake District never tops 7.0/10 (extremely rainy climate = one of the wettest regions in the UK, 3000-4000 mm/year in the steep valleys vs 600 mm in London, the European region with the highest precipitation at altitude). From June to August, expect 8-15°C: tourist high season (British school holidays), hikers, lively pubs, but unpredictable weather. From November to March, expect 0-6°C and 56-64% rain: damp cold, possible snow on the fells, many pubs and farms stay open for winter hikers. Highlights: Lake Windermere (steamboat cruises from Bowness-on-Windermere), Lake Ullswater (the 2nd longest, the most picturesque according to Wordsworth who wrote his poem Daffodils after seeing it), Lake Derwent Water (around Keswick, ferries between 7 jetties on the lake), Lake Coniston Water, Lake Buttermere (7 km loop walk), Wastwater (the deepest and wildest). Villages: Ambleside, Grasmere (Wordsworth's Dove Cottage his home 1799-1808), Hawkshead (Beatrix Potter Gallery), Keswick (the main town in the north of the park), Bowness-on-Windermere. Signature hikes: Scafell Pike (978m, England's highest peak, 5-6h return ascent, dangerous weather), Helvellyn (950m via Striding Edge, the famously vertiginous ridge), Catbells (the family hike par excellence, 3h from Keswick). Hill Top Farm (Beatrix Potter's house at Near Sawrey, kept as it was). Safety: British mountains underestimated, weather changing in 30 min, frequent thick fog, several deaths each year of poorly equipped hikers. Ordnance Survey maps + compass mandatory on the fells. Try Cumberland sausage (regional spiral sausage), Kendal mint cake (energy bar for hikers since 1869, taken by Hillary to the summit of Everest in 1953), sticky toffee pudding (locally claimed origin), Cumbrian ales (Jennings Brewery, Hesket Newmarket). Logistics: train from London Euston to Oxenholme (3h) then branch line to Windermere or Penrith.
lake-tahoe:en: Lake Tahoe is North America's largest alpine lake (at 1900m altitude, 500m deep, crystal-clear water). 2 distinct seasons: winter skiing (Heavenly, Squaw Valley/Palisades, Northstar, Kirkwood, one of North America's largest ski areas) and summer paddleboard and hiking. July is my favourite month (9.5/10): 15-27°C, 7% rain, lake at 18-20°C (cool but accessible). From December to March, expect -3/5°C with 32-39% precipitation (often snow): this is ski high season with sometimes record snowfall (the Sierra Nevada can take 5-10m over a winter). The roads can close during a storm. From April to May, it's the difficult transition: skiing winding down, summer not yet started, many restaurants in the off-season. South Lake Tahoe (Nevada side) for the casinos, North Lake (California side) for the calm. Tahoe Rim Trail (165 miles) for hikers.
hue:en: Hué, former imperial capital of Vietnam under the Nguyen dynasty (1802-1945): 350,000 inhabitants on the Perfume River, UNESCO-listed since 1993 for its monumental Imperial City complex. March is my favourite month (8.6/10): 22-28°C, 32% rain, ideal temperatures before the summer heat. From January to April, it's the most pleasant season: 18-31°C, 26-53% rain. From May to August, expect 25-35°C: stifling heat (sometimes 38°C with humidity), dry season but uncomfortable. From September to December, it's typhoon and monsoon season: 75-82% rainfall, regular flooding (Hué is one of the Vietnamese cities most affected by chronic flooding). Highlights: Imperial City (UNESCO, copy inspired by Beijing's Forbidden City, Cung An Định palace, Ngọ Môn Noon Gate, partially destroyed Tet 1968 during the Battle of Hué in the Vietnam War, restoration ongoing since 1993), Royal Tombs of the Nguyen emperors (7 monumental tombs in the countryside around Hué, to see: Tomb of Tu Duc the largest and most poetic, Tomb of Khai Dinh 20th-century Western-Asian blend, Tomb of Minh Mang classical Chinese), Thien Mu Pagoda (7-tier icon on the bank of the Perfume River, monk Thich Quang Duc set himself on fire in 1963 having left from here), Dong Ba Market, Citadel. Excursions: DMZ (demilitarised zone 100 km to the north, former Vinh Moc tunnel, Khe Sanh American base, Hamburger Hill = Vietnam War memorial sites). Try bún bò Huế (spicy beef noodle soup, signature local dish), bánh khoái (pancake), nem lui (skewers), bia hơi (fresh draft beer). Phu Bai airport (15 km), direct flights from Hanoi/Saigon.
mui-ne:en: Mui Ne, fishing village and kitesurf destination on Vietnam's south-east coast: 25,000 inhabitants 200 km north-east of Saigon in Binh Thuan province. Atypical in Vietnam for its red and white sand dunes at the heart of near-desert landscapes (one of Vietnam's only desert zones). From January to April are my favourite months (6.9/10): 22-32°C, 2-18% rain. Mui Ne never tops 6.9/10 (hot tropical climate, landscapes more than nature). From November to March, it's the kitesurf tourist high season (steady north-east winds 18-25 knots, kitesurf paradise in Asia), 22-30°C. From May to October, it's the monsoon + heat season: 27-77% rainfall, more humid, less consistent kitesurf. Highlights: White Sand Dunes (Bau Trang, 30 km from Mui Ne, surprising Saharan landscape, view at sunrise 5am, quad or jeep rental), Red Sand Dunes (Doi Hong, closer to the village 5 km, view at sunset), Fairy Stream (Suoi Tien, stream flowing between red and white rock formations, walk barefoot in 30 cm of water, 30 min return), Mui Ne fishing port (traditional round basket boats = signature Vietnamese vessels, fish market in the morning), Ham Tien (main beach village, hotels), Cham Towers Po Shanu (11th century, vestige of the Cham empire that ruled central Vietnam 2nd-15th century). Activities: kitesurf (one of the world's kite destinations with its sheltered Cap Rouge bay, Cap Diem, steady thermal winds Nov-March, international schools C2Sky, Surfpoint), windsurf, surf (modest waves), jeep or quad hire for the dunes, basket-boat ride. Audience: Russians historically in the majority (up to 50% pre-Covid, some restaurants with menus exclusively in Russian), European kitesurfers, growing European numbers. Excursions: Da Lat 4h by bus (mountains), Saigon 5h away, Phan Thiet 30 min away (provincial capital). Try nuoc mam (fish sauce — Phan Thiet is the world capital of nuoc mam), seafood, banh xeo (pancake), pho. No airport on site, transfers from Saigon (Tan Son Nhat SGN) in 5h.
sapa:en: Sapa, mountain station in northern Vietnam: 9000 inhabitants at 1500m altitude in the Tonkin Alps, base for the famous terraced rice paddies of Mường Hoa and Mount Fansipan (3143m, the "Roof of Indochina"). Significant minority population (Black Hmong, Red Dzao, Tay), ethnic markets. April is my favourite month (7.6/10): 15-22°C, 59% rain, clear skies, mild mountain temperatures. From March to May, it's the tourist high season: 12-23°C, often clear skies, paddies at the fluorescent-green growth stage. From September to November is the 2nd high season: 12-22°C, clear skies, golden pre-harvest paddies (the famous photos with yellow gradients). From June to August, it's the rainy season: 78-95% rainfall, frequent fog hiding the view, but the lushest landscapes. From December to February, expect 8-17°C: cold for Vietnam, rare possibility of snow at the summit of Fansipan. Highlights: Mường Hoa Valley (spectacular terraced rice paddies on 5-10 levels, hike Cat Cat → Lao Chai → Ta Van 12 km on foot), Mount Fansipan (cable car opened 2016, 6283m of cable, 15 min from Sapa, vs 2-3 days of trekking previously), Bac Ha Market (on Sundays, 2h from Sapa, one of the most colourful in northern Vietnam, flower-Hmong minorities). Trek with a local guide (usually a young Hmong woman) in the villages: Cat Cat (Black Hmong), Ta Phin (Red Dzao), Lao Chai (Black Hmong), Ta Van (Giay). Homestay possible (10-25€/night with meals). Try thắng cố (traditional Hmong soup made from organs), cá hồi (trout), fresh altitude vegetables, local rice alcohol (rượu cần). Logistics: Sapa 6h by bus from Hanoi via the motorway, or overnight train to Lao Cai + 1h bus.
chiang-mai:en: Chiang Mai is the cultural capital of northern Thailand: 130,000 inhabitants in the old town, 200 temples, former capital of the Lan Na kingdom (1296-1558). January is my favourite month (9.1/10): 17-28°C, 13% rain, ideal temperature, clear skies. From November to February, it's the high season: 18-30°C, ideal dry climate. From March to May, it's the burning season (agricultural slash-and-burn): catastrophic air quality (AQI 200-500+), heat (24-36°C), inadvisable for those sensitive to respiratory issues. From June to October, it's the monsoon: 60-96% rainfall in afternoon showers, green landscapes. Highlights: Old town surrounded by moats (1.5 km square), 30 temples including Wat Phra Singh, Wat Chedi Luang (majestic ruins), Wat Suan Dok, Doi Suthep (mountain temple 15 km away, 309 steps, panoramic view over Chiang Mai). Night markets: Sunday Walking Street (the most famous), Saturday Walking Street, Night Bazaar (touristy but unmissable). Activities: Thai cooking classes (Thai Farm Cooking School, Asia Scenic), Thai massage schools (Thai Massage School Old Medicine), ethical elephant sanctuaries (Elephant Nature Park, to favour over tourist farms with training), treks in the tribal villages (Karen, Hmong, Akha, Lahu) to the north. Yi Peng Lantern Festival in November (lanterns released into the sky, one of the world's most beautiful festivals). Try khao soi, sai oua, kanom jeen.
chiang-rai:en: Chiang Rai, capital of northern Thailand in the Golden Triangle on the Myanmar and Laos border: 70,000 inhabitants, former capital of the Lan Na kingdom (13th-16th centuries before unification with Chiang Mai). Quieter and more traditional than neighbouring Chiang Mai (200 km to the south), gateway to the Golden Triangle (Mekong-Ruak junction between Thailand, Laos, Myanmar, the world's former opium capital). February is my favourite month (9.2/10): 15-29°C, 10% rain, end of the dry season, ideal temperatures before the heatwave. December-January stay excellent (8.9/10). From November to February, it's the dry season: 15-28°C, dry climate and cool in the morning (sometimes 8°C in December, cooler than the south). From March to April, expect 18-34°C: pre-monsoon heatwave + agricultural slash-and-burn that pollutes the northern Thai atmosphere ("Burning Season", reduced visibility, dangerous air quality for sensitive bronchi, peak March-April). From May to October, it's the monsoon: 56-88% rainfall, but milder temperatures (22-32°C). Highlights: Wat Rong Khun ("White Temple", contemporary Buddhist temple entirely white with embedded mirror chips, created by local artist Chalermchai Kositpipat since 1997, in permanent construction, blend of traditional Buddhist motifs and pop culture references (Hello Kitty, Pokemon, Predator, Avatar in the interior paintings), free until the temple is officially "completed" probably around 2070), Wat Rong Suea Ten ("Blue Temple", entirely cobalt blue and gold temple, opened 2016, much more traditional), Black House Museum ("Baandam", black-wood-buffalo art museum by artist Thawan Duchanee died 2014, 40 black buildings, gothic-tribal atmosphere), Golden Triangle 1h away (Sop Ruak village, junction point of the 3 countries, giant golden Buddha statue, Hall of Opium museum), Doi Tung (royal garden, former palace of the mother of King Bhumibol), Mountain tribes: Akha, Hmong, Karen long necks visible in the villages to the north (ethical visits contested). Excursions: Chiang Mai 3h by bus, Mae Hong Son (north-west loop), Luang Prabang Laos by 2-day slow boat down the Mekong from Chiang Khong 1h30 away. Try khao soi (signature northern coconut curry noodles), sai oua (spicy sausage), khanom jeen, northern fruits (lychee, longan). Chiang Rai airport (CEI), domestic flights.
mandalay:en: Mandalay, Myanmar's 2nd city (Burma): 1.2 million inhabitants, the last royal Burmese capital (1857-1885 before the British conquest). Cultural, religious and artistic centre of the country (60% of Myanmar's Buddhist monks are concentrated here). January is my favourite month (9.3/10): 17-28°C, 6% rain, end of the dry season, ideal temperatures. December stays excellent (9.3/10). From November to February, it's the ideal dry season: 17-30°C, 2-19% rain. From April to September drop to 3.8-5.2/10 due to extreme heatwave (26-38°C, sometimes 45°C in the central dry plain, one of the hottest regions in South-East Asia). From May to October, it's the monsoon: 47-82% rainfall, but Mandalay receives relatively little rain (in a rain-shadow zone). Highlights: Mandalay Royal Palace (vast 4 km² fortified enclosure, teak palace rebuilt after WWII destruction, moats), Mandalay Hill (240m, 1729 covered steps, signature panorama at sunset over the city and the Irrawaddy river), Kuthodaw Pagoda ("the world's largest book": 729 white stupas each enclosing a slab engraved with the Tipitaka Buddhist canon), Mahamuni Pagoda (Buddha statue covered in gold leaves by the faithful for 200 years, becoming shapeless), Shwenandaw Monastery (entirely in carved teak, the only vestige of the original palace saved from the 1945 bombing), U Bein Bridge at Amarapura (1.2 km, the world's longest teak bridge, enchanting at sunset), Sagaing Hill (600 stupas and monasteries on the hills opposite). Excursions to the former capitals: Inwa (Ava), Sagaing, Mingun (gigantic unfinished pagoda 1790), Bagan 4h by boat or 5h by bus (3000 stupas, hot air balloon). Try mohinga (fish noodle soup), tea leaf salad. Important: politically unstable situation since the military coup of February 2021.
delhi:en: Delhi (New Delhi + Old Delhi) is India's capital: 32 million inhabitants in the urban area (among the world's largest), chaotic city with extreme contrasts. March is my favourite month (8.8/10): 16-30°C, 12% rain, end of the mild winter before the heatwave. From February to March, expect 11-30°C: ideal tourist temperatures. From April to June, it's the pre-monsoon heatwave: 22-39°C (sometimes 47°C in May), dust, yellowish sky. From July to September, it's the monsoon: 39-73% rainfall, possible flooding, oppressive humidity. From November to January, expect 8-27°C: tourist high season, but also extreme atmospheric pollution (AQI 300-500+ in November, yellow/grey sky, masks needed). Highlights: Old Delhi (Jama Masjid largest mosque in India, Red Fort UNESCO, Chandni Chowk chaotic market), New Delhi (Connaught Place, India Gate, Rashtrapati Bhavan), Humayun's Tomb (UNESCO, model for the Taj Mahal), Qutub Minar (UNESCO, 73m), Lotus Temple (Bahá'í), Akshardham (monumental Hindu temple). Safety: avoid travelling alone at night (women), Uber/Ola safer than rickshaws/taxis. Try chaat (street food), butter chicken (origin here), parathas at Paranthe Wali Gali, kebabs at Karim's. Base for the Golden Triangle (Delhi-Agra-Jaipur, 5-7 days).
mumbai:en: Mumbai (Bombay), India's economic capital: 22 million inhabitants, Bollywood, Marine Drive the "Queen's Necklace". January-February are my favourite months (8.9/10): 19-31°C, 0-2% rain, mild tropical winter. From June to September, it's the monsoon: 87-100% rainfall in July (one of the world's most violent, 2400mm in 3 months), massive flooding, paralysed transport. From March to May, expect 23-33°C and heavy heat on the coast. From October to December, expect 20-32°C and 0-40% rain: return to normal. Highlights: Gateway of India (1924), Taj Mahal Palace Hotel (target of the 2008 attacks), Marine Drive at sunset, Elephanta Caves (UNESCO, 1h by boat), Dhobi Ghat (open-air laundry with 7000 dhobis), CST Station (UNESCO). Bandra and Juhu for Bollywood celebrities. Slums tour at Dharavi (1 million inhabitants per km², controversial). Try vada pav (street food), pav bhaji, butter chicken, biryani. Acceptable safety by day. Direct flight from Paris in 9h.
kerala:en: Kerala, in south-west India: 35 million inhabitants, ranked "Top 10 destinations to see in a lifetime" by National Geographic Traveler. Beaches, backwaters, tea/cardamom plantations, ayurveda. January is my favourite month (6.8/10): 23-34°C, 11% rain. Kerala never tops 7/10 (constant humid equatorial climate). From June to September, it's the south-west monsoon: 85-98% rainfall, intensely green landscapes, ayurvedic practitioners recommend this season for cures (humid climate opens the pores). From October to November, second monsoon (north-east): 55-86% rainfall. From December to March, it's the tourist high season: 23-32°C, relatively dry climate. From April to May, expect 25-37°C: stifling heat before the monsoon. Highlights: Backwaters of Alleppey/Kumarakom (network of canals, 1-2 night cruise on a "kettuvallam" houseboat 100-300€), Kochi/Cochin (Fort Kochi, Chinese fishing nets, Paradesi synagogue, Jewish quarter), Munnar (tea plantations at altitude 1600m, cool, hikes), Periyar Wildlife Sanctuary (elephants, rare tigers), Varkala and Kovalam (cliff beaches). Theyyam (north Kerala dance-trance rituals, Oct-May). Try sadya (traditional meal on a banana leaf), Kerala fish curry, appam, puttu. Ayurvedic cure 7-21 days in a specialised resort. Main airport Kochi/Trivandrum. Equatorial climate = outside the main cyclone season.
rishikesh:en: Rishikesh, sacred Hindu town at the foot of the Himalayas in the state of Uttarakhand in northern India: 100,000 inhabitants on the Ganges. "World capital of yoga" since the Beatles spent 6 weeks here in February-April 1968 with Maharishi Mahesh Yogi, creating the White Album with 48 songs written during the stay. Today a global hub for yoga, meditation, ayurveda and rafting on the Ganges. March is my favourite month (9.2/10): 13-28°C, 15% rain, ideal temperatures before the heatwave. October stays excellent (9.2/10). From February to March and October-November, these are the best seasons: 8-29°C. From July to August, it's the monsoon: 83-85% rainfall, Ganges in flood swollen with mud, rafting closed by the authorities for danger. From April to June, expect 17-35°C: hot but dry, yoga still practicable. From November to February, expect 6-22°C: cold relative to north India, ideal for intensive practice. Highlights: Lakshman Jhula (former 1929 suspension bridge over the Ganges — closed for renovation since 2019, replaced temporarily by Bajrang Setu), Ram Jhula (another suspension bridge), Triveni Ghat (the main ghat for the ritual bath in the Ganges, Ganga Aarti = fire ceremony at sunset every evening with chants and floating lamps released on the water, spiritual experience), Parmarth Niketan Ashram (one of the world's largest ashrams, 1000 rooms, welcome for yoga classes, spectacular evening Aarti with 1000 faithful), Beatles Ashram (Chaurasi Kutia, ashram of Maharishi Mahesh Yogi where the Beatles stayed in 1968, abandoned since 1997, open to visits since 2015 with psychedelic graffiti and Beatles murals), Bharat Mandir (Rishikesh's oldest temple, founded by Adi Shankaracharya 8th century), Neelkanth Mahadev Temple 30 km away (Shiva temple on a hill 1675m, place where Shiva is said to have swallowed the poison of the sea to save the world according to Hindu mythology). Activities: yoga (hundreds of schools, daily classes 5-15€, TTC Teacher Training Course 200h or 500h = international Yoga Alliance certificate, prices 1500-3000€ for 1 month residential intensive), meditation Vipassana, ayurveda (massages, panchakarma cures), rafting (the Ganges at Rishikesh = one of Asia's best rivers for rafting, 16 km on grade III-IV, 800-1500 INR ≈ 10-18€/person September-June), bungee jumping (Mohan Chatti 83m, India's first bungee jump). Important: Rishikesh is vegetarian and alcohol-free by strict religious decree (absolute ban on meat, fish, egg, alcohol throughout the town). Try sattvic food (pure yogic cuisine: no onion, no garlic, no strong spice), vegetarian thali, lassi, masala chai, fresh juices. Logistics: Dehradun airport (DED) 30 min away, alternative Delhi 6h by bus.
nairobi:en: Nairobi sits at 1700m altitude: mild climate year-round (13-28°C), no African heatwave, but marked rains. February is my favourite month (9.0/10): 15-28°C, 28% rain.
