Logistics: 3h30 by bus from Hanoi (motorway), boarding at Tuan Chau Marina or Halong City. Try local seafood, cha muc (squid rolls). April and September are the best crowd/weather compromises.
phu-quoc:en: Phu Quoc, Vietnam's largest island: 580 km², 100,000 inhabitants in the Gulf of Thailand in the south-west of the country (15 km from the Cambodian coast, 45 km from the Vietnamese coast). Special "Special Economic Zone" status since 2014 = 30-day visa exemption for all countries. Massive hotel development (several 5*+ resorts: InterContinental, JW Marriott, Premier Village). January is my favourite month (8.4/10): 24-29°C, 33% rain, this is the ideal dry season. From November to March, it's the tourist high season: 24-30°C, sea at 28°C, hotel prices x2-x3 (Russians, Koreans in the majority, more Europeans). From May to October, it's the south-west monsoon: 89-97% rainfall, many hotels stay open but water activities limited, turbid beaches. Highlights: Sao Beach ("Star Beach" in the south-east, the finest white sand on the island, turquoise water, one of Vietnam's most beautiful beaches according to rankings), Long Beach (Bai Truong, 20 km of continuous beach on the west coast, main hotel zone), Khem Beach ("Cream Beach" south, turquoise water, JW Marriott), Vinpearl Land (theme park + safari + beach), Coconut Tree Prison ("Phu Quoc Prison", Viet Cong prisoner camp used by the French then by the South Vietnamese, sombre memorial), Sunset Sanato Beach Club (driftwood beach sculptures), Duong Dong Night Market (seafood street food), Phu Quoc National Park (50% of the island, preserved jungle, difficult hikes), Ham Ninh Fishing Village (traditional stilt village), Nam Du Islands excursion (wild archipelago), An Thoi Archipelago (in the south, 15 islands, diving and snorkelling, access by speedboat or by Hon Thom Cable Car = the world's longest cable car over the sea at 7900m, opened 2018). Try Phu Quoc nuoc mam (Vietnam's most renowned fish sauce, AOC), bun quay (fish soup), fresh seafood, sea urchin, Phu Quoc cultured pearls, Phu Quoc pepper (grown on the island). Phu Quoc airport (PQC) with direct flights Saigon/Hanoi/Bangkok/Seoul.
da-lat:en: Da Lat, altitude city in the central highlands of Vietnam: 230,000 inhabitants at 1500m altitude in Lam Dong province, nicknamed "Little Paris" or "City of Eternal Spring". Founded in 1893 by Alexandre Yersin (Franco-Swiss doctor, discoverer of the plague bacillus) as a hill resort for French colonials fleeing the heat of Saigon. Remarkable French colonial architecture, temperate climate year-round thanks to altitude. February-March are my favourite months (8.7/10): 14-25°C, 18-28% rain, this is the ideal dry season. From November to April, it's the dry season: 14-26°C, permanent spring climate. From May to October, it's the monsoon: 49-95% rainfall, frequent mists (but the landscapes stay spectacular under mist). Permanent climate: Da Lat never exceeds 26°C in average maximum and rarely drops below 14°C in average minimum, this is the major advantage of tropical altitude. Highlights: Hang Nga Crazy House ("Crazy House", organic architecture inspired by Gaudí and Tolkien created by architect Dang Viet Nga since 1990, hotel + tourist attraction), Xuan Huong Lake (central artificial lake, 5 km walk around), Dalat Cathedral (Notre-Dame de Dalat, neo-Gothic 1942 French heritage), Chua Linh Phuoc ("Glass Pagoda", entirely decorated in coloured broken-glass mosaics), Da Lat Market (central market, flowers and vegetables of the highlands: artichokes, strawberries, berries, temperate-climate leafy vegetables unique in Vietnam), Coffee plantations (Da Lat = Vietnamese capital of Robusta coffee and rarer arabica, Vietnam is the 2nd world producer after Brazil), Flower plantations (Da Lat = Vietnam's floral capital, exports to the whole country), Datanla Falls (waterfalls with alpine slide opened 2007), Pongour Falls 50 km away (Vietnam's largest waterfall in the rainy season). Activities: canyoning (Da Lat is the centre of canyoning in Vietnam), Mount Lang Biang hike 2167m (the highest point in the highlands), mountain bike, horse riding. Excursions: Mui Ne 4h by bus (coast), Saigon 6h by bus, Nha Trang 3h away (coast). Try banh trang nuong ("Vietnamese pizza" = grilled rice flatbread topped), banh can, weasel coffee (Vietnamese kopi luwak), Da Lat wines (one of the few Vietnamese vineyards), artichokes in broth. Lien Khuong airport (DLI) 30 min away.
ninh-binh:en: Ninh Binh, province in northern Vietnam 100 km south of Hanoi: 160,000 inhabitants. Nicknamed the "Halong Bay on land" for its spectacular karst landscapes (limestone peaks rising from the rice paddies and rivers) similar to those of Halong Bay but in green farming country. UNESCO 2014 for the Trang An landscape complex. April is my favourite month (8.1/10): 23-28°C, 30% rain, ideal temperatures. From February to April and October-November, expect 16-28°C: excellent period. From May to August, expect 25-34°C: heavy heat, but also the rice paddies in gold at harvest time (June = spectacular landscapes with karst peaks against golden paddies). From September to December, expect 19-31°C and 33-77% rain, season of the 2nd harvest (landscapes also golden in October). From January to February, expect 15-23°C: relative cold for Vietnam, more mystical mists. Highlights: Tam Coc ("3 caves", boat ride pulled by the rowers' feet on the Ngo Dong river through 3 natural tunnels crossing the karst peaks, 2h return, signature landscapes), Trang An (UNESCO, alternative to Tam Coc, longer 3h return, crossing 9 caves/temples, more traditional but more touristy, choice between 3 itineraries), Hoa Lu (former capital of Vietnam in the 10th-11th centuries before the transfer to Hanoi in 1010, temples Dinh Tien Hoang and Le Dai Hanh), Bich Dong Pagoda ("Crystal Pagoda", 3 levels in a 15th-century cave), Hang Mua ("Mua Caves", 500 steps to climb to the summit of the Lying Dragon peak, signature panorama over Tam Coc, one of the viral photos of Vietnam), Pagoda Bai Dinh (the largest Buddhist pagoda complex in South-East Asia, created in 2003-2010, 100-tonne bronze Buddha), Cuc Phuong National Park (Vietnam's oldest national park, created 1962, primates in sanctuary). Activities: cycling in the countryside (ideal alternative to the overloaded boats), trekking in the rice paddies, horse rides. Excursions: Hanoi 2h by train or bus, Halong Bay 3h away, Mai Chau 4h away (Thai minorities valley). Try goat (Ninh Binh specialty, very locally renowned, thit de = goat grilled with sesame), com chay (crispy burnt rice), bun cha. No airport, train transfer from Hanoi 2h.
riviera-maya:en: Riviera Maya, Caribbean coast of Mexico in the state of Quintana Roo in the south-east of the country: 130 km of coastline from Cancún to Tulum, 1.5 million inhabitants. Tourist capital of Mexico with more than 13 million visitors/year (Cancún alone = the busiest airport in Mexico, 27 million passengers/year). Powdery white-sand beaches + turquoise water + cenotes (natural freshwater wells sacred to the Mayans). March is my favourite month (8.8/10): 24-28°C, 27% rain, this is the ideal dry season. From January to April, it's the dry season: 22-29°C, sea at 27-28°C, this is the tourist high season (American/Canadian snowbirds in the majority, Europeans, hotel prices x3). From May to June, expect 26-30°C with 46-67% rain: start of the wet season. From July to October, it's the active cyclone season: 54-72% rainfall, real hurricane risk (the Riviera Maya was devastated by Wilma in 2005, Dean in 2007, Delta in 2020). Sargassum season = a major problem since 2014: brown algae invading the beaches mainly May-October, especially in June-August, the coast from Tulum to Playa del Carmen more affected than Cancún or Holbox, hotels invest in daily cleaning but the beach experience is sometimes spoiled. Highlights / Main resorts: Cancún (the best known, Hotel Zone 23 km of beaches with all-inclusive resorts, dynamic Coco Bongo nightlife, public beach Playa Delfines with signature "CANCUN" Insta-photo), Playa del Carmen ("Playa" to regulars, smarter and more modern cosmopolitan town than Cancún, 5th Avenue 30-block pedestrian street with restaurants-bars-shops, ferry departures to Cozumel 30 min), Tulum (the smartest and most bohemian, Mayan ruins perched on a cliff above turquoise sea = cult signature visual, preserved wild beaches, eco-chic boutique hotels, hippie-yoga atmosphere), Akumal ("turtle bay" in Mayan, snorkelling with green turtles guaranteed), Puerto Morelos (preserved fishing village between Cancún-Playa del Carmen). Mayan archaeological sites: Chichén Itzá 2h away (UNESCO, one of the 7 New Wonders of the World 2007, signature El Castillo pyramid, ball court, observatory), Tulum Ruins (the only seaside Mayan ruins in the world, exceptional setting on a turquoise cliff, access at 8am to avoid crowds + heat), Cobá 1h away (Nohoch Mul pyramid 42m still climbable until 2020, jungle trails), Ek Balam 2h away (little touristy, preserved stucco friezes). Cenotes = signature of the Riviera Maya (underground freshwater natural wells formed in the limestone of the Yucatán peninsula, more than 6000 cenotes recorded, crystal-clear water 22-25°C constant year-round, sacred Mayan place for rituals): Cenote Dos Ojos at Tulum (the most famous, diving), Gran Cenote at Tulum (the most touristy but splendid), Cenote Ik Kil at Chichén Itzá (the most photogenic with hanging vines), Cenote Suytun (circular platform with sun ray filtering at noon). Excursions: Isla Mujeres 25 min by ferry from Cancún (quieter island, turquoise beaches, snorkelling whale sharks June-September), Cozumel 30 min by ferry from Playa del Carmen (world-renowned diving island, top 5 diving globally according to Cousteau), Bacalar Lagoon 4h away ("lake of 7 colours", extraordinary turquoise lagoon), Sian Ka'an Biosphere Reserve 1h from Tulum (UNESCO, preserved mangrove biosphere reserve). Try ceviche, cochinita pibil (Mayan pork roasted in a banana leaf), tacos al pastor, mole, tamales, mezcal, tequila, Sol/Corona/Pacifico beer. Cancún airport (CUN).
isla-holbox:en: Isla Holbox, small island in the north of Yucatán in Mexico in the Yum Balam Biosphere Reserve: 2000 permanent inhabitants over 41 km long and 2 km wide, sandy island where no cars are allowed (transport: golf carts = electric golf carts, bicycles, scooters). Described as the "Tulum of 20 years ago" = preserved atmosphere, bohemian, no big resorts, deserted beaches, still little touristy. January-February are my favourite months (8.4-8.8/10): 22-28°C, 26-34% rain, this is the ideal dry season. From November to April, it's the tourist high season: 22-31°C, sea at 26-27°C, hotel prices x2-x3 (smart-alternative clientele, little American mass tourism), little sargassum (the only major advantage over the Riviera Maya). From May to August, expect 24-32°C: tropical heat, turquoise waters, this is the whale shark season (June-September, swimming-snorkelling with these gentle 12-15m giants guaranteed 95% of outings, one of the only worldwide destinations for this experience). From September to October, it's the cyclone season: 57-75% rainfall, but the island is partially sheltered by its position north of Yucatán. Highlights: Holbox Beach (15 km of continuous powdery white-sand beach facing north towards the Gulf of Mexico, shallow water 50 cm over 100m allowing you to walk in the sea = signature visual of Holbox, sometimes wild flamingos can be spotted in the lagoons), Punta Cocos (extreme west, spectacular deserted beach at sunset), Punta Mosquito (extreme east, sandbar accessible on foot at low tide, extremely clear water), Yalahau Cenote ("fountain of youth", cenote fed by underground springs, historic baths of Mayan Indians), Isla Pasión ("Passion Island", neighbouring islet with wild beach), Isla de los Pájaros ("Bird Island", colonies of migratory birds: pink flamingos, frigates, pelicans, herons), Holbox Town (preserved fishing village with sandy streets, colourful street art frescoes on every corner, bohemian atmosphere). Activities: swim with whale sharks (June-September, the Holbox signature, outing 6h-13h by boat with biologist-guide, snorkelling close to 5-15 whale sharks depending on luck, life jacket mandatory, price 2000-2800 MXN ≈ 100-140€/person, advance booking), bicycle or golf cart hire (the island can only be crossed in 4-5h on foot end-to-end, golf cart = local standard 200 MXN/hour ≈ 10€), kitesurf (Punta Mosquito main spot, steady winds), SUP, kayaking in the mangrove in the inner lagoons with wild flamingo observation, bioluminescent diving (June-October the Holbox beaches light up at night thanks to the bioluminescent phytoplankton, view towards Punta Cocos without moon). Hotels: Casa Las Tortugas, Villas HM Palapas del Mar, Casa Sandra (smart boutique hotels), 200-800€/night. Try Holbox pizza (signature of the village, created by an Italian expat, dough cooked over a wood fire), fresh seafood (squid, grilled fish, ceviche), tacos de pescado (signature fish tacos), local mojito. Logistics: Holbox 2h30 from Cancún (taxi van to Chiquilá port 2h30 + 30-min ferry, 100 MXN/person), no airport on the island.
guanajuato:en: Guanajuato, UNESCO colonial city embedded in a mining valley at 2000m altitude. The dry season holds the tourism. March is my favourite month (9.6/10): 12-28°C, 4% rain, ideal for climbing the Pípila or exploring the callejones. From June to September, it's the rainy season: 47-58% storm showers, often in late afternoon (mornings often stay sunny). Temperatures dropping back to 14-27°C thanks to the altitude. The Festival Cervantino in October transforms the city for 3 weeks: international companies, hotel prices x3, bookings 6 months in advance. The mummies of the Museo de las Momias attract the curious year-round. 30 minutes away, San Miguel de Allende offers another smarter colonial atmosphere. November-April remain the most stable and most photogenic season.
san-miguel-de-allende:en: San Miguel de Allende, UNESCO-listed, has become the favourite Mexican town of expat Americans (10-15% of the population). At 1900m altitude, near-constant temperate climate year-round. April is my favourite month (9.2/10): 14-29°C, 10% rain, jacarandas in bloom, pink Parroquia silhouetted against blue sky. From June to September, it's the rainy season (40-49% showers), often in late afternoon, refreshing to 14-26°C. The town centres come alive in the evening after the showers. Día de los Muertos late October and Día de la Independencia mid-September are the two cultural peaks. Bookings 4-6 months ahead. The art scene (Fábrica La Aurora), the hot springs around Taboada, and the food tours of Guanajuato cuisine are the classics. Guanajuato 1h by bus complements the stay well. Gentrification has driven prices up: plan 80-150€/night for a boutique hotel.
merida:en: Mérida is the capital of Yucatán: 1 million inhabitants, Spanish colonial city built on the ruins of the Mayan city T'ho. January is my favourite month (8.6/10): 19-28°C, 18% rain, mild climate after the infernal summer. From March to August, expect 22-36°C: extreme heat of the Yucatán interior (sometimes 42°C), Méridans coming out after 19h, plazas waking up in the evening. From June to October, it's the rainy season: 39-57% rainfall, but relative respite from the heat. Highlights: Plaza Grande (Catedral San Idelfonso, Casa de Montejo, Palacio Municipal), Paseo de Montejo (avenue inspired by the Champs-Élysées, 19th-century mansions), Mercado Lucas de Galvez, Gran Museo del Mundo Maya. Excursions: Chichén Itzá 1h30 away (UNESCO, sunrise to avoid crowds and heat), Uxmal 1h away (UNESCO, Puuc ruins less frequented than Chichén), Izamal 1h away ("yellow city", 16th-century Franciscan convent), Cenotes of Cuzamá (3 cenotes by mule train), Celestún 2h away (pink flamingos). Friday evening: free open-air music on Plaza Grande. Try cochinita pibil (pork marinated in achiote, cooked in the earth), poc chuc, papadzules, sopa de lima, marquesitas. Has become a growing digital nomad destination since 2020.
puerto-escondido:en: Puerto Escondido ("The Hidden Port"), fishing village on the Pacific coast of Oaxaca in Mexico: 50,000 inhabitants, famous for its Playa Zicatela which produces the "Mexican Pipeline" = one of the largest waves in the world (up to 6m+, considered among the 10 biggest waves globally with Pipeline Hawaii, Teahupoo Tahiti, Nazaré Portugal). Surfers from around the world come from May to September for the WSL competitions. Bohemian atmosphere, surfers, backpackers, much less developed than Cancún or Tulum. January-February-March-April are my favourite months (6.9/10): 21-32°C, 2-4% rain, this is the dry season. Puerto Escondido never tops 6.9/10 (hot tropical climate + Pacific cyclone season). From November to April, it's the tourist high season: 21-32°C, dry climate, calmer sea (but Zicatela stays wavy). From May to October, it's the rainy season: 21-36% to 78% rainfall, but also big-wave season on Zicatela (paradox: the surfer high season corresponds to the general public low season). Pacific cyclone season from June to November. Beaches: Zicatela (the long, big-wave one, danger for non-surfer swimmers), Carrizalillo (small cliff-dive cove, 167-step staircase, calm water ideal for swimming and beginner surfing, photogenic), Manzanillo (calm water, snorkelling), Bacocho (calm, pelicans), Puerto Angelito. Activities: surf (school for beginners at Carrizalillo or La Punta Zicatela, lesson 30€), turtle release (release of baby turtles in the evening, Vivemar conservation association), bioluminescent lagoon at Manialtepec 30 min away (lagoon phosphorescent at night, view at new moon), swimming with dolphins. Adventure tours: Mazunte 1h away (former turtle-fishing village converted into a hippie New Age village, Mermejita beach), San Agustinillo (between Mazunte and Zipolite), Zipolite (Mexican nudist beach, hippie atmosphere since the 70s). Try Oaxacan mole (Oaxaca's signature sauce, 7 variants), tlayuda, Oaxacan artisanal mezcal, ceviche, fish tacos. Logistics: direct flight from Mexico City (1h15), flight via Oaxaca city, or 6h bus from Oaxaca. Flight from Europe via Mexico City mandatory.
pattaya:en: Pattaya, beach resort on the east Thai coast on the Gulf of Thailand 150 km south-east of Bangkok: 110,000 inhabitants. Formerly a fishing village, the American GI made it their main R&R during the Vietnam War (1965-1975), causing the explosion of the sex tourism industry it struggles to shed. Today a mix of family beach resorts and night districts aimed at Western tourists. From November to February are my favourite months (6.9/10): 23-31°C, 19-34% rain, this is the ideal dry season. Pattaya never tops 6.9/10 (hot tropical climate + sea not turquoise like the Andaman coast). From November to April, it's the tourist high season: 23-33°C, sea at 27-29°C, big crowds (Russians, Chinese, Europeans). From May to October, it's the monsoon: 46-80% rainfall, rough sea, many hotels stay open. Highlights: Walking Street (the signature pedestrian night street, hostess bars, neon signs, openly decadent atmosphere), Beach Road (4 km seafront), Sanctuary of Truth ("Sanctuary of Truth" = carved wooden temple still under construction since 1981, 105m high, Buddhist-Hindu syncretism), Big Buddha of Pattaya (Wat Phra Yai, 18m), Nong Nooch Tropical Garden (240-hectare botanical garden with topiary sculptures, elephant shows — ethical controversy — and Thai cabaret), Tiffany's Show (transgender cabaret since 1974, one of Asia's most famous), Cartoon Network Amazone (water park), Mini Siam (world monument scale models). Beaches: Pattaya Beach (urban, not the most beautiful), Jomtien Beach (5 km to the south, calmer and more family-friendly), Wong Amat (to the north, calmer), Koh Larn ("Coral Island" 7 km away, 30-min ferry, more beautiful beaches than Pattaya itself: Tawaen, Samae). Excursions: Bangkok 2h by bus, Cha-am and Hua Hin 4h away, Koh Samet 2h away. Try Thai seafood, som tam, tom yam, massaman, but also Russian and Indian cuisines (strong presence). Audience: Russians historically in the majority (15-30% of tourism), Chinese on the rise, Western Europeans 30-60 years old, Indians on group visits. U-Tapao airport (UTP) 30 min away, domestic flights, international charters.
hua-hin:en: Hua Hin, royal beach resort on the west coast of the Gulf of Thailand 200 km south-west of Bangkok: 60,000 inhabitants. Thailand's first beach resort, launched in 1911 when Rama VI had the royal summer palace Klai Kang Won ("Far from worries") built there, today the favourite residence of the late King Bhumibol and the current King Vajiralongkorn. Smarter and quieter than Pattaya, family atmosphere. December is my favourite month (9.0/10): 24-29°C, 13% rain, this is the best month due to cool temperatures and absence of rain. From November to February are the tourist high season: 23-31°C, sea at 27°C, hotel prices x2-x3 (Bangkokians on weekends + European snowbirds). From March to May, expect 25-34°C: intense heat, more humid. From May to October, it's the south-west monsoon: 43-78% rainfall (more sheltered than the Andaman coast thanks to the Tenasserim mountains on the Burmese side), many hotels stay open. Highlights: Hua Hin Beach (5 km of urban beach), Khao Takiab ("Monkey Mountain" at the extreme south, Chinese temple with semi-wild macaques, panoramic view), Cicada Market (weekend market Friday-Saturday-Sunday, art-craft, food court, one of Thailand's smartest), Hua Hin Night Market (Soi 72, street food), Plearnwan (60s-70s Thai nostalgia theme park), Klai Kang Won Palace (royal palace, occasional visits), Hua Hin Railway Station (one of Asia's most beautiful railway stations, royal colonial architecture 1926), Black Mountain Water Park, Vana Nava Water Jungle. Excursions: Pranburi 30 min away (Khao Sam Roi Yot national park with Phraya Nakhon cave sheltering a royal pavilion lit naturally by a hole in the vault, 30-min steep hike), Cha-am 30 min away (less smart beach), Petchaburi 1h away (Khao Wang royal palace on a hill), Bangkok 2h30 by train or bus. Golf: Hua Hin is one of the golf capitals of Thailand (10+ courses within a 30 km radius, the most famous: Royal Hua Hin Golf 1924, the oldest in South-East Asia). Try beach-stall seafood (the Khao Takiab fresh fish market), pad thai, mango sticky rice, local coffee. Direct train from Bangkok 4h or bus 3h30, Hua Hin airport (HHQ).
malacca:en: Malacca (Melaka), historic city in south-west Malaysia: 460,000 inhabitants on the Strait of Malacca (one of the world's most strategic maritime trade routes for 2000 years). UNESCO 2008 (with Penang) for its triple colonial heritage: Portuguese (1511-1641), Dutch (1641-1824), British (1824-1957). Cradle of the Peranakan people (straits-born Chinese-Malay), unique Sino-Portuguese atmosphere. February is my favourite month (6.9/10): 24-30°C, 43% rain. Malacca never tops 6.9/10 (humid equatorial tropical climate). From January to March, it's the relatively drier season: 24-31°C. From April to November, it's the wetter season: 71-92% rainfall year-round. Highlights: Stadthuys (Dutch "Town Hall" 1650, the oldest surviving Dutch building in Asia, painted signature brick red, houses the Melaka History Museum), Christ Church (1753, one of the oldest active Anglican churches in South-East Asia, painted red), Dutch Square (red ensemble with Queen Victoria fountain 1904 + clock tower), A Famosa ("The Famous One", Portuguese fortress 1511, largely demolished by the British 1806, only the Porta de Santiago gate survives = the signature visual of Malacca, one of the oldest European fortifications in Asia), Saint Paul's Church (on the hill, Portuguese remains 1521, temporary tomb of Saint Francis Xavier Jesuit missionary before transfer to Goa), Cheng Hoon Teng Temple (the oldest Chinese temple in Malaysia, founded 1645), Kampung Kling Mosque (syncretic Malay-Sumatran 18th-century architecture), Jonker Walk district (Jonker Street, Sino-colonial atmosphere with 2-storey shophouses, weekend night market on Friday-Saturday-Sunday evenings, antiques, street food, cult local karaoke), Babas and Nyonya Heritage Museum (Peranakan museum in a 19th-century shophouse, to understand this unique syncretic culture), Maritime Museum (full-scale replica of the Portuguese galleon Flor de la Mar wrecked near Malacca in 1511 with a legendary treasure never recovered), Trishaw Tour (ridiculously decorated cycle-rickshaws — Hello Kitty, flowers, flashing lights — to cross the city, openly kitsch but a local experience). Try Nyonya cuisine = the Chinese-Malay blend signature of Malacca: Nyonya laksa, chicken rice ball (Hainan chicken served with rice balls, Malacca specialty), satay celup (skewers to dip in a communal peanut soup), cendol (Jonker 88 institution), devil's curry (spicy Portuguese curry), gula melaka (local palm sugar), Portuguese settlement also worth seeing (descendants of the 16th-century Portuguese soldiers still present with the Kristang Creole dialect). Kuala Lumpur airport (KUL) 2h by bus (Malacca 150 km south-east of KL).
kota-kinabalu:en: Kota Kinabalu ("KK"), capital of the state of Sabah on the island of Borneo in Malaysia: 500,000 inhabitants on the west coast, base for visiting Mount Kinabalu (4095m, the highest peak in South-East Asia between the Himalayas and Papua) and the Bornean jungle. February is my favourite month (6.9/10): 23-28°C, 48% rain, this is the relatively drier season. KK never tops 6.9/10 (hot humid equatorial climate year-round). From February to April, it's the relative dry season: 23-30°C, this is the best period to climb Mount Kinabalu. From May to October, it's the wet season: 64-83% rainfall. From November to January, it's the north-east monsoon: 67-83% rainfall. Kota Kinabalu highlights: Kota Kinabalu Night Market ("Filipino Market", street food seafood, exotic tropical fruits: durian, mangosteen, rambutan, jackfruit), Kota Kinabalu City Mosque (Masjid Bandaraya, "floating" mosque surrounded by water, one of the most beautiful in Malaysia), Sabah State Mosque (Masjid Negeri Sabah), Signal Hill Observatory (panorama over the bay). Essential excursions = this is why people come to KK: Mount Kinabalu 2h by bus from KK (4095m, mandatory 2-day climb with guide from Kinabalu Park HQ 1900m, overnight at Laban Rata refuge 3273m, 2am start for the Low's Peak summit 4095m at sunrise, descent the same day, physically demanding experience but accessible to fit beginners, permit and guide mandatory 800-1500 RM = 160-300€), Tunku Abdul Rahman Marine Park 30 min by speedboat (5 islands with snorkelling: Manukan the most developed, Sapi via bridge to Gaya, Mamutik, Sulug, Gaya the largest), Sandakan 6h by bus on the east coast (orangutans at Sepilok Orangutan Rehabilitation Centre = the most famous sanctuary in Asia along with Tanjung Puting Kalimantan, sun bears at the neighbouring Bornean Sun Bear Conservation Centre), Sukau Kinabatangan River (river cruise in Bornean jungle, observation of wild orangutans, proboscis long-nosed monkeys endemic to Borneo, Bornean pygmy elephants, crocodiles, birds), Sipadan (legendary diving island top 5 globally, limited access 120 divers/day permits), Mantanani (snorkelling island), Tip of Borneo (Tanjung Simpang Mengayau, extreme north, panorama). Try hinava (Sabah ginger ceviche), tuaran mee (noodles), Sabah tea (the only large-scale organic tea grown in Asia, plantations at 1000m altitude), fresh seafood. Kota Kinabalu International airport (BKI) with flights from KL (2h30), Singapore, Hong Kong.
sapporo:en: Sapporo is Japan's 5th city: capital of Hokkaido, 1.9 million inhabitants, modern grid-pattern city (rare in Japan), eponymous beer. July is my favourite month (8.2/10): 18-25°C, 40% rain, ideal temperatures when the rest of Japan is stifling, festivals (Sapporo Summer Festival late July, Sapporo Bon Odori in August). From December to March, expect -10/4°C and 40-52% precipitation (often abundant snow, Sapporo receives 5m of snow/year, among the snowiest in the world): this is the season of the Sapporo Snow Festival (1st week of February, Odori Park, giant ice sculptures, 2 million visitors), of skiing at Niseko (2h by bus), Furano (2h30) or closer Teine (1h). Highlights: Clock Tower (1878, symbol of the city), Mount Moiwa (cable car for the night view over the city, among the 3 best night panoramas in Japan with Hakodate and Nagasaki), Susukino (entertainment district), Hokkaido University (historic campus with poplar avenue), Otaru 30 min away (romantic canal, glassware). Try Sapporo-style miso ramen (at Sumire or Aji no Sanpei), Genghis Khan (grilled lamb, local dish), Sapporo beer (museum and brewery visitable). Direct Tokyo-Sapporo flight in 1h30, Hokkaido Shinkansen train (4h from Tokyo).
nara:en: Nara was Japan's first fixed capital (710-784) before Kyoto: 350,000 inhabitants, 8 UNESCO sites, sacred deer roaming free (1300 sika deer in the park). May is my favourite month (8.1/10): 13-24°C, 39% rain, ideal temperatures before the monsoon. From June to mid-July, it's the tsuyu (rainy season). July-August drop to 6.9/10 due to the humid heatwave (23-32°C). From October to November, momiji possible, 14-22°C, clear skies. From December to February, expect 0-11°C: dry cold, Nara park deserted, calmer deer. Highlights: Tōdai-ji (Daibutsu = 15m bronze Great Buddha, one of the world's largest Buddhist statues, the building itself is one of the world's largest wooden structures), Kasuga Taisha (3000 stone and bronze lanterns, illuminated twice a year at Mantoro in February and August), Kōfuku-ji (50m pagoda, 2nd tallest in Japan), Hōryū-ji 30 min away (UNESCO, oldest standing wooden buildings in the world, 607 AD), Nara Park (deer revered as messengers of the gods, shika senbei biscuits on sale, beware they can be insistent). Naramachi (old merchant district). Try kakinoha-zushi (sushi wrapped in persimmon leaf), narazuke (vegetables in sake). Day trip from Kyoto (45 min by train) or Osaka (35 min) very easy.
nagasaki:en: Nagasaki, the 2nd city to suffer an atomic bombing on 9 August 1945 (74,000 deaths), keeps a singular history in Japan: the only port open to Europeans (Dutch and Portuguese) during the Edo isolation (1639-1854), Christian influence unique in Japan. October is my favourite month (8.2/10): 18-23°C, 27% rain, ideal temperatures before winter. From June to mid-July, it's the tsuyu. July-August drop to 6.9/10 due to the heatwave. From December to February, expect 5-12°C and 28-35% rain: mild thanks to the maritime effect, sometimes clear skies. Highlights: Atomic Bomb Museum (memorial to the bombing), Peace Park (peace statue), Hypocenter Park, Glover Garden (house of Scottish merchant Thomas Glover 19th century, panoramic view over the port), Oura Cathedral (UNESCO, oldest wooden church in Japan, 1864), Dejima (artificial island where the Dutch were confined), Shinchi Chinatown, Mount Inasa (panoramic night view, among the 3 best in Japan). Excursions: Hashima/Gunkanjima by boat (abandoned coal-mining island, UNESCO, James Bond Skyfall), Hirado (former Portuguese base). Lantern Festival in Chinese February (15 days, more than 15,000 lanterns). Try Nagasaki champon (Sino-Japanese fusion noodles), castella (cake of Portuguese origin), turkish-rice. 2h by Shinkansen from Fukuoka.
fukuoka:en: Fukuoka, Japan's 5th city by size, economic capital of Kyushu: 1.6 million inhabitants, milder climate than Honshu, base for exploring the south. May is my favourite month (8.0/10): 15-23°C, 37% rain, ideal temperatures before the monsoon, last cherry blossoms (sakura mid-March to early April). From June to mid-July, it's the tsuyu. July-August drop to 6.9/10 due to the humid heatwave (24-32°C). From October to November, expect 10-22°C with 28-36% rain: clear skies, momiji. From December to February, expect 3-11°C: mild for Japan (more subtropical climate), rare snow. Highlights: Ōhori Park (central pond, Japanese garden), Fukuoka Castle (ruins, Maizuru Park), Kushida Shrine, Canal City Hakata (shopping centre). Yatai = street stalls characteristic of Fukuoka, open in the evening along the Naka river and at Tenjin (150 stalls), specialty of the city. Excursions: Dazaifu 30 min away (Tenmangu shrine for students), Yufuin 2h away (onsen village at the foot of Mount Yufu), Beppu 2h30 away (8 thermal "hells"), Nagasaki 2h by Shinkansen. Try Hakata ramen (origin here, Ichiran and Ippudo born in Fukuoka), motsunabe (tripe), mentaiko (spicy cod roe). Very accessible from Seoul (1h by plane), Shanghai, Taipei.
kanazawa:en: Kanazawa, in the Ishikawa prefecture on the west coast of Japan: 460,000 inhabitants, former capital of the powerful feudal Maeda clan during the Edo period (1583-1868), spared by WWII bombings so one of the only Japanese cities with preserved traditional architecture. Considered "the little Kyoto without the crowds". June is my favourite month (8.1/10): 17-25°C, 44% rain, mild temperatures before the summer humidity. From May to June and September-October, expect 13-26°C: mild climate. From July to August, expect 22-31°C with 49-51% rain: hot and humid. From December to February, expect 0-9°C and 68-80% precipitation often as snow (the west coast of Japan receives huge amounts of snow, Kanazawa is one of the snowiest large cities in the world 3-4m/season). Highlights: Kenroku-en (one of the 3 most beautiful traditional gardens in Japan with Kairaku-en in Mito and Koraku-en in Okayama, 11.4 hectares created over 200 years 1620-1840 by the Maeda lords, particularly spectacular in winter with its pine trees suspended on ropes "yukitsuri" so they don't break under the snow), Kanazawa Castle (next to Kenroku-en, complex under reconstruction since 2001), Higashi Chaya district (one of the 3 preserved geisha districts in Japan with Kyoto Gion and Tokyo Asakusa, 19th-century wooden houses still in operation, possibility of taking tea in a historic teahouse), Nagamachi district (former samurai residences), Kanazawa 21st Century Museum of Contemporary Art (circular SANAA architecture, one of Japan's most-visited contemporary museums), Omicho Market ("Kanazawa's kitchen", fish market since 1721, more than 170 stalls), Teramachi district ("temple district" with Ninja Temple Myoryu-ji = not really a ninja temple but a temple with secret traps built for defence). Try Sea of Japan sushi (among the best in Japan thanks to proximity), kaga ryori regional cuisine, jibu-ni stew, gold leaf ice cream (Kanazawa produces 99% of Japanese gold leaf used to decorate temples and lacquers).
hakone:en: Hakone, 80 km south-west of Tokyo, is Japan's most famous spa town: view of Mount Fuji on a clear day, hot springs (onsen), Lake Ashi. August is my favourite month (7.7/10): 18-24°C, 58% rain, cool temperatures at altitude (Hakone at 700m). July-September are the most pleasant months (7.3-7.7/10) with 56-62% rainfall in afternoon storms. From December to March, expect -6/7°C: possibility of snow, Mount Fuji visible more often (dry sky), onsen under snow (a striking experience). From April to June, expect 3-19°C with 49-61% rain: late spring, frequent mists. Highlights: Hakone Shrine (red torii in Lake Ashi, very Instagrammed photo), Owakudani (volcanic valley, sometimes closed depending on activity, kuro-tamago black eggs cooked in sulphur), Hakone Open Air Museum (sculptures including Picasso), Hakone Ropeway (cable car), cruise on Lake Ashi on a pirate boat. Hakone Free Pass (5000-7000¥/2 days): unlimited transport between all the sites + train from Tokyo. Onsen ryokan: Yamanochaya, Gora Kadan, Hakone Ginyu (200-800€/night with meals). Try kaiseki ryori in a ryokan, local soba. Day trip from Tokyo possible (1h30 by Romance Car train) but 1 night in a ryokan recommended for the full onsen experience.
nikko:en: Nikko, 130 km north of Tokyo, is the shrine of Tokugawa Ieyasu (founder of the Edo shogunate): 100 UNESCO temples and shrines. August is my favourite month (7.3/10): 20-26°C, 90% rain, mild temperatures at altitude (Nikko at 600m). From June to September, it's the wettest season (62-90% rainfall), frequent fogs that make the shrines mysterious. From October to November is the momiji season (autumn colours, peak mid-October to mid-November depending on altitude), absolute tourist peak, prices x2-x3. From December to March, expect -5/8°C and 36-49% precipitation often as snow: partially frozen waterfalls (Kegon-no-Taki), isolated Lake Chuzenji. From April to May, expect 4-18°C: late spring, last altitude cherry blossoms. Highlights: Tōshōgū (the most ornate shrine in Japan, Ieyasu mausoleum, sculpture of the 3 monkeys "see no, hear no, speak no"), Rinnōji (3 great Buddhas), Futarasan-jinja, Sacred Bridge Shinkyō (lacquered red). Lake Chuzenji 1h by bus with hairpin road (Iroha Zaka, 48 bends = 48 Japanese syllables), Kegon Falls (97m). Yumoto Onsen (hot springs). Day trip from Tokyo (2h by train) possible but overnight stay recommended.
jeju:en: Jeju (Jeju-do), volcanic island of South Korea 100 km south of the mainland: 670,000 inhabitants over 1849 km² (Korea's largest island), UNESCO-listed 2007 (lava-tube volcanoes and unique ecosystem), reputed as "the honeymoon island" for Korean couples. May is my favourite month (7.9/10): 15-22°C, 36% rain, ideal temperatures, cherry blossoms (sakura, southern Korea blooms late March-early April, Jeju the first ones in early March). From May to September, it's the tourist high season: 15-30°C. From July to August, expect 25-30°C with 57-59% rain: heavy and humid heat, but also Korean tourist peak (school holidays). From December to March, expect 2-13°C and 40-46% rain: mild for Korea (Jeju is the warmest island in Korea), snow possible on Hallasan in winter. Highlights: Mount Hallasan (1947m, the highest peak in South Korea, dormant volcano with crater lake, 9 km ascent via Seongpanak or Gwaneumsa trail, 4-5h up, sometimes closed in winter for snow), Manjanggul (UNESCO, one of the world's longest lava tubes at 7.4 km, 1 km accessible to visitors with lanterns, cool 11°C constant year-round), Seongsan Ilchulbong ("Sunrise Peak", UNESCO, 5000-year-old volcanic crater on the extreme east, 182m, 30-min ascent, spectacular sunrise view), Jusangjeolli Cliff (basalt cliffs in geological hexagonal columns), Cheonjeyeon Falls and Jeongbang Falls (waterfalls), Cheonjiyeon Waterfall, Olle Trails (network of 425 km of coastal trails in 27 segments, popularised since 2007 on the model of the Camino de Santiago), Beaches: Hyeopjae (white sand, turquoise water), Hamdeok, Jungmun. Cultural haenyeo phenomenon ("women of the sea", apnea divers without equipment who fish abalone, sea urchins, abalone up to 70-80 years old, millennia-old matriarchal tradition UNESCO 2016, in the process of disappearing — 4500 in the 1960s, fewer than 4000 today of whom 60% are over 70). Try heuk dwaeji (Jeju black pork), abalone porridge, hallabong (Jeju mandarin), omija tea. Direct Seoul-Jeju flight in 1h (one of the busiest air routes in the world, 80,000 flights/year).
busan:en: Busan is Korea's 2nd city and the country's largest port: 3.4 million inhabitants, 7 beaches, 4 fish markets. May-June are my favourite months (8.2/10): 13-26°C, 28-38% rain, good temperature for Haeundae beach without the summer crowds. From July to September, expect 20-30°C and 38-53% precipitation: typhoon season (Maemi 2003 left a mark), but also the Busan International Film Festival early October. From December to February, expect -3/8°C and 12-18% rain: dry cold, sea too cold for swimming but hiking on the Haeundae and Galmet trails. Highlights: Haeundae and Gwangalli for the urban beaches, Gamcheon Culture Village (the "Korean Santorini", colourful houses), Jagalchi Fish Market (the largest in Korea), Beomeosa (major Buddhist temple), Diamond Bridge at night. Try milmyeon (cold noodles specialty), seafood porridge, dwaeji gukbap. Direct flight from Paris or via Seoul by KTX (2h30).
yongpyong:en: Yongpyong, South Korean ski resort in Gangwon province 200 km east of Seoul: 5000 permanent inhabitants (15,000 in ski season). Host of the PyeongChang Winter Olympics 2018 (alpine slalom + giant slalom events, opening-closing ceremonies 6 km away at Pyeongchang Olympic Plaza), massively modernised for the Olympics (KTX high-speed train lines directly from Incheon Seoul airport in 2h). Pistes: 28 km Yongpyong Resort (modest vs Alps: 600 km 3 Vallées, but one of the largest areas in South Korea), altitude 750-1458m, vertical drop 708m. June-July are my favourite months (7.6-7.9/10): 13-24°C, 43-57% rain, ideal Korean summer temperatures. Yongpyong never tops 7.9/10 (Korean continental humid climate). From June to September, it's the summer high season: 8-25°C, Mount Balwangsan hiking, 36-hole golf, water parks, active summer-tourism resort atmosphere. From December to March, expect -10/7°C and 16-28% precipitation often as snow: this is ski high season. Signature pistes: Rainbow Slope (Rainbow 1-2-3-4, signature exposed descent of the 2018 Olympics, one of the most difficult descents in South Korea), Mega Green (family), Pink Slope. Particularities: Yongpyong = filming base of the famous K-drama "Winter Sonata" (Winter 2002) with Bae Yong-Joon = cult Korean romantic drama that popularised Yongpyong throughout East Asia and triggered the "Hallyu" = Korean cultural wave 2003-2010, place of pilgrimage for Japanese and Chinese fans, statue of the Winter Sonata scene to see at the foot of the slopes. Off-piste: forbidden in South Korea (protected national parks). Modern Snowpark for freestyle. Winter activities: alpine ski, snowboard, cross-country ski, snowmobile, ice luge, Korean hot spring spa experiences (jjimjilbang) traditional must in winter. No "aurora season" at this latitude (37°N). Audience: Koreans in the majority (Seoul 2h by KTX), Chinese and Japanese tourism groups for the Winter Sonata drama, few Western Europeans (prefer Niseko Hokkaido Japan or the Alps). Hotels: Dragon Valley Hotel, Yongpyong Resort Tower Condominium, prices 100-300€/night cheaper than Niseko. Excursions: Pyeongchang Olympic Plaza 6 km away (2018 Olympics memorial with Olympic Cauldron), Alpensia Resort 5 km away (host of nordic + biathlon 2018 Olympics events), Gangneung 1h away (Sea of Japan coastal city, host of 2018 Olympics ice events, beaches in summer), DMZ North Korea border 4h by bus ("Demilitarized Zone", essential excursion from Seoul, the most militarised border in the world, Joint Security Area JSA Panmunjeom where Trump met Kim Jong-un in 2019), Seoul 2h by KTX (capital, already covered by BDW). Try bibimbap (Korean mixed-rice national dish), bulgogi (grilled beef), galbi (grilled ribs), kimchi (omnipresent fermented cabbage), tteokbokki (spicy rice cakes), soju (Korean national alcohol), makgeolli (fermented rice wine). Yangyang airport (YNY) 1h away, alternatively Incheon Seoul airport (ICN) 2h by KTX high-speed train.
bariloche:en: Bariloche is the "little Argentine Switzerland": northern Patagonia, lakes and araucaria forests, chalet architecture. January is my favourite month (9.3/10): 10-24°C, 11% rain, 14h days, austral summer ideal for the Circuito Chico (65 km panoramic road), Cerro Catedral (summer lift, view over Nahuel Huapi), Refugio Frey (hike to the glacial lake). From June to August (austral winter), it's the ski season on Cerro Catedral (the largest resort in South America, 120 km of pistes): temperatures at 1-6°C at the bottom, snow guaranteed from July to mid-September. From April to November, expect 1-14°C and 26-53% rain: humid Patagonian climate, but the autumn-coloured forests (May) stay photogenic. Bariloche is famous for its chocolate: Calle Mitre concentrates the chocolatiers (Mamuschka, Rapanui, Fenoglio). El Bolsón 2h away for the Argentine hippies. Cerro Tronador (active volcano) for a day hike.
mendoza:en: Mendoza is the land of Argentine Malbec: 1200 bodegas, late harvests at 1000m altitude. The neighbouring Andes (Aconcagua at 6960m, the highest peak in the Americas) regulate the climate. November is my favourite month (9.5/10): 16-28°C, 14% rain, vineyards in full growth. The harvests (vendimia) take place in March: Fiesta Nacional de la Vendimia early March, one of the largest events in Argentina. From June to August (austral winter), the mercury drops to 4-16°C with freezing nights. Ski season at Las Leñas (3h by road, 24 km of pistes). The sky stays mostly clear (9-11% rain). December to March are the tourist high season: ideal temperatures, but also peak rain (14-23%). The Argentine Cajón del Maipo (Cordón del Plata) and the road to Uspallata complete the stay. Cordoba 600 km away by bus is another lovely stopover.
santiago:en: Santiago is dominated by the Andes: atmospheric pollution in winter (the thermal inversion blocks the smog in the basin), dry Mediterranean summer. December is my favourite month (9.8/10): 14-29°C, 3% rain, 14h days. From June to August (austral winter), the mercury drops to 7-15°C with heavy weekday smog. Hiking to Cerro San Cristóbal becomes unbreathable on some days. It's by contrast the ski season at Valle Nevado (1h30 from the city): 60 ski days per year on average. Valparaíso and Viña del Mar 1h30 away remain a weekend classic. The Cajón del Maipo (Andean canyon) is hikeable from October to April. May and September are decent transitions: less smog, lower hotel prices, pleasant Maipo Valley vineyards (Concha y Toro 30 minutes away).
valparaiso:en: Valparaíso, port city of Chile: 300,000 inhabitants 110 km west of Santiago on the Pacific, UNESCO-listed 2003 for its historic ensemble unique in Latin America. City built on 42 hills ("cerros") with colourful wooden and corrugated-iron houses, accessible by 15 historic funiculars ("ascensores") dating from the 19th-20th century (8 still in service, 7 in restoration). Cultural capital of Chile (poets Pablo Neruda and Vicente Huidobro lived here). January is my favourite month (8.8/10): 14-20°C, 2% rain, this is the austral summer, constant clear sky. From November to March, it's the tourist high season: 11-20°C, ideal Mediterranean climate. From June to August, it's the austral winter: 9-14°C, 16-22% rain, mild but sky often grey. Highlights: Cerro Concepción and Cerro Alegre (the 2 most touristy hills, cobbled alleys, exceptional street art — Valparaíso is one of the world capitals of street art, with murals everywhere), La Sebastiana (Pablo Neruda's house-museum, panoramic view over the bay, one of the poet's 3 houses after Isla Negra and La Chascona in Santiago), Ascensor Concepción (1883, the oldest funicular still in service in Chile), Plaza Sotomayor (main square at the port edge with the Monumento a los Héroes de Iquique), Mercado Cardonal (picturesque central market), Museo de Bellas Artes (Palacio Baburizza), Plaza Aníbal Pinto, Reloj Turri (English clock tower), Iglesia Anglicana San Pablo. Festivals: Año Nuevo (31 December, one of the world's largest fireworks displays over the bay, 1 million spectators on the hills). Viña del Mar 10 min away by metro ("la Ciudad Jardín", the "real" smart Chilean seaside resort, beaches, casino, song festival in February that brings together all of Latin America). Excursions: Isla Negra 1h away (Pablo Neruda's house on the Pacific), Santiago 1h30 away. Try chorrillana (chips with meat/onion/egg), pichanga (street food), pastel de jaiba (crab pie), Maipo and Casablanca wines.
manaus:en: Manaus, capital of Amazonas in the heart of the Brazilian Amazon: 2.2 million inhabitants at the confluence of the Rio Negro and the Rio Solimões (the 2 rivers join to form the Amazon proper, the "Encontro das Águas" = spectacular phenomenon of the 2 rivers flowing side by side without mixing for 6 km due to differences in temperature/density/PH). Port city emerging from the jungle in the 19th century during the rubber boom (1879-1912), world capital of rubber and one of the richest cities in the world at that time (before the collapse caused by Asian rubber cultivation). June is my favourite month (6.9/10): 24-30°C, 62% rain. Manaus never tops 6.9/10 (constant equatorial rainy climate). From June to November, it's the (relatively) "dry" season: 24-32°C, 31-64% rain, low waters of the Amazon (exposed land, river beaches, accessible jungle paths). From December to May, it's the rainy season: 24-30°C, 76-88% rainfall, high waters rising +12m, flooded jungle ("igapó"), navigation possible in the canopy. Manaus highlights: Teatro Amazonas (1896, Italian Belle Époque opera house built in the heart of the jungle for the rubber boom, dome covered with 36,000 tiles in the colours of the Brazilian flag made in France, stage opening onto the square with phonola = parquet on rubber dampers in local rubber to avoid disrupting performances, annual opera festival in May), Mercado Adolpho Lisboa (1882, miniature copy of Les Halles in Paris, iron structure, market for giant Amazonian fish pirarucu/tambaqui), Encontro das Águas (5h boat excursion, the absolute must of Manaus). Amazon excursions: Manaus is the base for jungle stays (3-5 day lodges in the canopy, wildlife observation toucans/macaws/monkeys/sloths/caimans/pink dolphins/anacondas, 200-1500€/night depending on standing, reference lodges: Anavilhanas Jungle Lodge in UNESCO river archipelago, Juma Amazon Lodge). Important: Manaus is only accessible by plane or by boat (4 days from Belém on a passenger cargo ship, unique experience of the classic Amazon journey). No land routes to the rest of Brazil. Try tambaqui (Amazonian fish), tucumã (Amazonian palm), açaí natural (the real açaí, bitter blackcurrant base, not the Western sweetened smoothie), guaraná Antártica (local soft drink).
chili:en: Chile, South American country: 19 million inhabitants over 4300 km from north to south (one of the longest countries in the world), from the Atacama, the most arid desert, to Patagonia and Tierra del Fuego. 5 distinct climate zones depending on latitude. Seasons are reversed (southern hemisphere). January-February are my favourite months (8.4/10): 7-19°C on average (Santiago), 7-11% rain, this is the austral summer. From November to March, it's the Chilean tourist high season: 2-19°C, dry and warm climate, ideal for Atacama and the southern lakes. From June to August, it's the austral winter: -5/6°C, skiing in the Andes (Portillo, Valle Nevado, Farellones 2h from Santiago), Patagonia closed and inaccessible. Regions to know: Atacama Desert (San Pedro de Atacama, the world's most arid desert, some areas have never recorded rain, Valley of the Moon, Tatio Geysers at 4300m, Salar de Atacama, star observation at ALMA Observatory), Wine valleys (Casablanca, Maipo, Colchagua = Chilean wines among the world's best, Carmenere signature varietal), Lake District (Pucón, Frutillar, Puerto Varas, conical Osorno volcano, Llanquihue lake), Chilean Patagonia (Torres del Paine National Park = W trek 4-5 days or O 8-10 days with glaciers, turquoise lakes, guanacos, condors, among the most spectacular landscapes in the world), Easter Island (UNESCO, 887 moai sculpted by the Rapanui, flight from Santiago 5h). Santiago is the capital (already covered), Valparaíso UNESCO port city (already covered). Try empanada de pino, completo (Chilean-version hot dog), pastel de choclo, Chilean ceviche, pisco sour (national cocktail, debate with Peru for the origin), Carmenere wines.
portillo:en: Portillo, Chilean ski resort in the Andes Cordillera: 165 km north-east of Santiago at 2880m altitude, on the border with Argentina, one of the oldest ski resorts in South America (founded 1949). 1 single hotel (Hotel Portillo, 5*, signature yellow visible from afar on the edge of Lago del Inca), 450 beds maximum, exclusive atmosphere of an "international family chalet" rather than a commercial alpine resort. Reversed seasons (southern hemisphere). February is my favourite month (6.9/10): 7-16°C, 27% rain, austral summer, alpine hiking, kayaking on Lago del Inca, but the resort only opens for skiing in winter. From June to October, it's the ski season (austral winter): -5/9°C, 25-31% precipitation often as snow (10-12m/season, one of the snowiest in South America thanks to the Cordillera barrier effect). Portillo is famous for its summer skiing for nordic teams (US Ski Team, Norwegian, Austrian, have trained here in July-August during the North American summer to prepare for the northern World Cup). Pistes: 35 pistes, 14 lifts (including 5 "slingshot" pull-back lifts rare in the world, platforms tractioning 8 people up steep slopes at 70 km/h without pylons, created in 1961 by the Austrian founders), altitude 2600-3300m, vertical drop 700m, treeless open terrain (off-piste skiing possible everywhere). Style: Portillo is ultra-exclusive, 1 single hotel = sense of "private club at altitude", international clientele (Brazilians in the majority, Americans, Europeans, few local Chileans), no shops or village. Tariff: 7-day all-inclusive week packages 2500-5000$/person (lodging + meals + skiing). Logistics: Portillo 2h from Santiago by car/transfer (road snaking through the Andes Camino Internacional towards Argentina, sometimes closed for snow), Santiago airport.
cordoba-argentine:en: Córdoba is Argentina's 2nd city, oldest Jesuit university in South America (1613, UNESCO). Student city, surrounding sierras. November is my favourite month (9.0/10): 16-28°C, 33% rain, jacarandas in bloom as in Buenos Aires. From June to August (austral winter), expect 5-19°C and 5-8% rain: dry and cool, ideal for hiking in the Sierras de Córdoba (Cerro Champaquí at 2790m). From December to February, expect 19-30°C and 31-44% storm showers: bearable heat at altitude (400m), violent late-afternoon storms. The Manzana Jesuítica and the Jesuit estancias of the region (Alta Gracia, Santa Catalina, Jesús María) are the major cultural attraction. Alta Gracia is home to Che Guevara's house. Mendoza 11h by bus, Buenos Aires 1h by plane. The Sierras Chicas (Capilla del Monte, La Cumbrecita) attract Argentinians for the weekend.
natal-bresil:en: Natal, capital of Rio Grande do Norte in the Nordeste of Brazil: 900,000 inhabitants on the Atlantic coast at the closest point in the Americas to Africa. The sunniest city in Brazil (300+ days per year), nicknamed "Cidade do Sol". Famed for its spectacular shifting dunes (Genipabu) and its signature urban beach Ponta Negra. September-October are my favourite months (8.2/10): 23-29°C, 34-36% rain, this is the driest season. From September to February, it's the tourist high season: 23-30°C, 32-49% rain, steady Atlantic breezes. From March to June, it's the rainy season ("inverno do Nordeste" ironically called winter despite stable temperatures 23-29°C): 65-75% rainfall, but typical brief afternoon showers. Highlights: Praia de Ponta Negra (signature urban beach with Morro do Careca = 120m sand dune-cliff falling directly onto the beach, signature visual of Natal), Forte dos Reis Magos (16th-century Portuguese star-shaped fort with 5 points, at the mouth of the Rio Potengi, official founding of Natal on 25 December 1599), Centro Histórico (Cidade Alta, 17th-18th century Portuguese colonial architecture: Catedral Velha, Igreja do Bom Jesus das Dores, Palácio Felipe Camarão), Parque das Dunas (1170 hectares of shifting dunes within the city itself, protected trails). Beach and dune excursions (Natal's major attraction): Genipabu 30 min to the north (the famous shifting dunes of Brazil = signature landscape for advertisements, buggy ride with local bugueiros, sandboarding, Pititinga freshwater lake, dromedaries by the sea brought from the Canaries), Praia da Pipa 1h30 to the south (one of the most beautiful beaches in Brazil according to many rankings, red cliffs, dolphins, bohemian atmosphere), São Miguel do Gostoso, Galinhos. Try moqueca de camarão, carne de sol, tapioca with coconut/banana/coalho cheese, água de coco. Direct flight from Lisbon, or via São Paulo/Recife.
porto-alegre:en: Porto Alegre, capital of Rio Grande do Sul in southern Brazil: 1.4 million inhabitants at the confluence of 5 rivers forming Lago Guaíba. Subtropical climate as opposed to tropical Brazil, "gaúcho" atmosphere (South American cowboy) recalling neighbouring Argentina or Uruguay (mate is consumed daily as in Buenos Aires, equestrian traditions). December is my favourite month (8.7/10): 19-29°C, 34% rain, this is the start of the austral summer. From November to March, it's the austral summer: 17-30°C, 32-41% rain, sometimes scorching (38°C+ with oppressive humidity). From April to May and September-October are the best seasons: 14-24°C. From June to August, it's the austral winter: 10-21°C, mild but sky often grey. Highlights: Mercado Público (1869, central 19th-century market in Portuguese architecture), Casa de Cultura Mario Quintana (cultural centre former Hotel Majestic Art Nouveau), Praça da Matriz (historic square with Catedral Metropolitana), Parque Farroupilha ("Parque da Redenção", 37-hectare central park, craft market on Sundays), Usina do Gasômetro (former gas plant converted into a cultural centre, signature view of the sunset over Lago Guaíba — Gaúchos say Porto Alegre has "the most beautiful sunset in the world"), Memorial of Rio Grande do Sul, Jardim Botânico. Excursions: Gramado 2h away (Bavarian town in the Serra Gaúcha, year-round Christmas atmosphere with the Natal Luz market, massive Brazilian domestic tourism), Canela (Cascata do Caracol), Bento Gonçalves and Vale dos Vinhedos (Brazilian wine region, 19th-century Italian immigration), Atlantic Coast 2h away (Torres, Capão da Canoa). Try churrasco (the real gaúcho churrasco, not tropical Brazilian: meat with salt on a sword, cowboy tradition), chimarrão (gaúcho mate), arroz carreteiro, dulce de leche, Vale dos Vinhedos wines.
bretagne:en: Brittany, peninsula at the far west of France: 27,000 km², 3.3 million inhabitants, 2700 km of coastline, strong Celtic identity (Breton language, fest-noz, music). July-August are my favourite months (8.5-8.6/10): 14-24°C, 28-30% rain, tourist peak. From June to September, expect 13-23°C with 30-39% rain: high season; capricious weather but often pleasant. From November to March, expect 3-13°C and 38-45% rain: humid oceanic, sky often grey, but less violent than the Côte d'Émeraude in winter (legend of Breton storms). Highlights: Pink Granite Coast (Perros-Guirec, Ploumanac'h, Trégastel — pink rocks, Customs Officers' Path), Côte d'Émeraude (intra-muros Saint-Malo, Cap Fréhel, Dinan), Côte Sauvage at Quiberon, Belle-Île-en-Mer by boat (45 min, Aiguilles de Port-Coton painted by Monet), Pointe du Raz and Pointe du Van (extreme west), Carnac (3000 aligned menhirs, the world's largest megalithic site), Vannes and Gulf of Morbihan (40 islands), Brest and Forêt de Brocéliande (Arthurian legends). Festivals: Fest-noz (UNESCO, traditional night festivals), Festival Interceltique de Lorient (August), Vieilles Charrues at Carhaix (July, one of the largest French festivals). Try galettes/crêpes (buckwheat/wheat), kouign-amann, far breton, cider, chouchen, seafood (Cancale oysters).
normandie:en: Normandy is the west of France: 30,000 km², 3.3 million inhabitants, 600 km of coastline, cliffs, D-Day landing beaches (1944), camembert and calvados. July-August are my favourite months (8.3-8.4/10): 14-23°C, 33% rain, mild temperatures. From June to September, it's the high season: 13-22°C, but capricious weather (Normandy rainy but often sunny, rapid alternation). From November to March, expect 5-13°C and 41-45% rain: humid oceanic, sky often grey, many seaside resorts dormant. Highlights: Mont-Saint-Michel (UNESCO, one of France's most-visited sites, the strongest tides in Europe ±15m, view at night or at high tide, mandatory parking 2.5 km away + shuttle or walk), D-Day Landing Beaches (Omaha Beach, Utah Beach, Juno, Gold, Sword, Pointe du Hoc — American and British cemeteries, Caen Memorial), Bayeux (11th-century Tapestry telling the conquest of England by William the Conqueror in 1066, UNESCO, 70m long), Etretat (Aval-Manneporte-Amont cliffs, painted by Monet, oversaturated in season), Honfleur (picturesque port, painted by Monet and Boudin), Deauville-Trouville (smart 19th-century seaside resorts, American Film Festival in September), Rouen (Gothic cathedral painted 30 times by Monet, place of Joan of Arc's execution), Giverny (Claude Monet's house and gardens, view from April to October). Channel Islands (Jersey, Guernsey) by ferry from neighbouring Saint-Malo. Try camembert, livarot, Pont-l'Évêque (3 AOC cheeses), tarte normande, andouille de Vire, calvados, cider, teurgoule. May-June and September = best compromises.
provence:en: Provence covers 4 departments: Vaucluse, Bouches-du-Rhône, Var, Alpes-de-Haute-Provence. June is my favourite month (9.3/10): 17-29°C, 21% rain, lavender starting its blossoming on the Valensole plateau (mid-June to mid-July, peak around 14 July depending on the year). July-August are scorching (19-33°C, sometimes 40°C+): Provençaux taking refuge in the cool interior of the old stone houses, frequent mistral (north-west wind, drying out everything). From December to February, expect 2-14°C and 20-26% rain: mild for France, often clear sky, green olive trees. Highlights: Avignon (Palais des Papes, Pont Saint-Bénézet, Festival in July), Aix-en-Provence (cours Mirabeau, Cézanne), Arles (Roman arena, Van Gogh), Les Baux-de-Provence, Gordes and Roussillon (perched villages of the Luberon), Sénanque (Cistercian abbey with lavender), Mont Ventoux (Tour de France). Try ratatouille, aïoli, tapenade, Aix calissons, AOC Châteauneuf-du-Pape, Gigondas, Bandol wines. May and September-October are the best tourism/weather compromises (lavender still visible in early July).
annecy:en: Annecy, the "Venice of the Alps": 130,000 inhabitants, alpine lake (the purest in Europe according to analyses), surrounding mountains (La Tournette 2351m). July-August are my favourite months (8.8-8.9/10): 16-27°C, 31-35% rain, lake swimming (water at 22°C in midsummer), paragliding from Le Semnoz, lake circuit by bike (40 km). From December to February, expect 0-10°C and 39-45% rain or snow: skiing at La Clusaz, Le Grand-Bornand or Megève (45 min). The International Animation Film Festival mid-June (one of the largest in the world) animates the city. Highlights: Old town with its canals and the Palais de l'Île, Annecy Castle, Pont des Amours, Sunday morning markets (very famous). Lake Annecy: boat ride, public beaches (Albigny free, Impérial paid), picturesque villages (Talloires, Veyrier-du-Lac). Try tartiflette, savoyarde fondue, omble chevalier (lake fish). Geneva 45 min by car, Chamonix 1h away. May-June and September are the best crowd/weather compromises. Direct flight Geneva or Lyon, TGV from Paris (3h45).
cote-azur:en: The Côte d'Azur stretches from Cassis to Menton, 200 km of varied coastline. July is my favourite month (9.7/10): 21-28°C, 8% rain, constant clear sky, sea at 24°C. August excellent (9.3/10) but absolute tourist peak: prices x2-x4, A8 motorway saturated, paid beaches stormed. From December to February, expect 5-14°C and 20-23% rain: mild for France, often clear sky, palm and orange trees in full form. Nice Carnival mid-February, Menton Lemon Festival in February-March. Major cities: Nice, Cannes, Saint-Tropez, Monaco, Menton, Antibes, Saint-Paul-de-Vence (art village), Eze (perched village). Beaches: most are pebble beaches (except Pampelonne, Antibes). Calanques de Cassis and Estérel Massif for nature. Yachting: Saint-Tropez and Monaco concentrate the activity (Yacht Show Monaco late September). Try ratatouille, daube provençale, tarte tropézienne, salade niçoise. May-June and September are the real sweet spots tourism/weather.
bath:en: Bath, spa town in Somerset England: 95,000 inhabitants 200 km west of London. UNESCO twice (1987 Roman and Georgian city, 2021 "Great Spa Towns of Europe"). The only natural site in Great Britain with hot springs (45°C, 1 million litres/day) that the Romans were already exploiting in the 1st century BC. Exceptionally preserved 18th-century Georgian architecture in golden Bath stone. July-August are my favourite months (7.7-7.8/10): 13-20°C, 38-40% rain, typical mild English temperatures. From May to September, it's the tourist high season: 8-20°C, cool and unpredictable British climate (umbrella mandatory). From November to March, expect 2-9°C and 40-46% rain: damp cold, rare snow, but Bath keeps a particular charm in the rain (the Roman baths under the mist). Highlights: Roman Baths ("Aquae Sulis", 1st-4th century Roman thermal complex among the best preserved in the world, sacred spring dedicated to Sulis Minerva, water still hot springing at 46°C, audio guide with Bill Bryson voice, view also at night with illuminations), Bath Abbey (15th-century late Gothic, the last great Gothic church in England, signature fan vault ceiling), Royal Crescent (signature visual of Bath, Georgian crescent of 30 terraced houses in golden stone 1767-1774 by John Wood the Younger, one of the most beautiful Georgian urban compositions in the world), The Circus (1768 Georgian circular square by John Wood the Elder), Pulteney Bridge (18th century, one of the 4 bridges in the world with shops along its entire length after the Ponte Vecchio Florence and Ponte di Rialto Venice), Thermae Bath Spa (modern thermal centre opened 2006, signature rooftop terrace with heated pool and view over the city), Jane Austen Centre (the novelist lived in Bath 1801-1806, mentions in Northanger Abbey and Persuasion), No. 1 Royal Crescent Museum (visit of a typical Georgian house). Excursions: Stonehenge 1h away (UNESCO, already covered by BDW), Cotswolds 1h away (traditional English countryside), Bristol 30 min away (Banksy), Wells and Glastonbury. Try Sally Lunn bun (local brioche since 1680), Bath bun, scones with clotted cream, fish and chips, local ales (Bath Ales). Direct train from London Paddington 1h30.
york:en: York, historic city in North Yorkshire in England: 200,000 inhabitants. 2000 years of continuous history: Roman capital of Britannia Inferior founded 71 AD (Eboracum), Anglo-Saxon capital (Eoforwic 7th century), Viking capital of Jorvik (9th-10th centuries), kingdom of Northern England (10th-13th centuries), 2nd city of England in the Middle Ages, royal stage on the York-Edinburgh road. Nicknamed the "capital of the North". UNESCO universal value not yet listed but 1 of the 10 most-visited English heritage sites. August is my favourite month (7.8/10): 13-20°C, 39% rain, ideal temperatures. From June to August, it's the tourist high season: 11-20°C, long days (17h of daylight at the solstice). From November to March, expect 2-9°C and 35-46% rain: damp cold, rare snow, St Nicholas Fair Christmas market (one of the oldest in England since the 13th century). Highlights: York Minster (the gothic cathedral: the largest gothic cathedral in England, construction 13th-15th century, one of the largest in the world, Great East Window stained glass 15th century = the largest medieval stained-glass surface in the world 23m × 9m, 275-step ascent of Central Tower panorama, 12th-century Norman crypt with ancient remains), The Shambles (medieval narrow street with 15th-century timber-framed houses with overhanging upper storeys nearly touching above the street, former butchers' street "Shambles" = butchery in old English, the inspiration for Diagon Alley in Harry Potter with witch shops today), City Walls (medieval ramparts 4.5 km, one of the only English cities with complete walls accessible on foot, panorama above the city), Jorvik Viking Centre (immersive museum with reconstruction of the 10th-century York Viking quarter discovered during excavations 1976-1981), York Castle (Clifford's Tower, 1245 keep), National Railway Museum ("national railway museum", the world's largest railway museum, free, Mallard locomotive holder of the steam speed record 203 km/h in 1938, replica of Stephenson's Rocket 1829), Yorkshire Museum (Roman and Viking artefacts including the 15th-century Middleham Jewel = signature medieval English jewel), Treasurer's House (haunted house according to legend, Roman ghosts spotted in the cellar which was on the former Roman road), Stonegate quarter (historic shopping street). Yorkshire excursions: Yorkshire Dales and North York Moors National Parks (hill landscapes, ruined abbeys), Whitby 1h away (Viking port, ruined abbey that inspired Bram Stoker for Dracula 1897), Castle Howard 30 min away (18th-century baroque palace filming location of Brideshead Revisited), Harrogate 30 min away (Victorian spa town), Edinburgh 2h30 by train. Try Yorkshire pudding (the traditional version), Wensleydale cheese (PGI Yorkshire cheese), parkin (traditional ginger oat cake), fish and chips, Yorkshire ales (Black Sheep, Theakston). Direct train London King's Cross 2h.
cotswolds:en: Cotswolds, rural region of central-western England: 200,000 inhabitants spread across a succession of villages classified AONB ("Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty" since 1966), covering 6 counties (Gloucestershire, Oxfordshire, Wiltshire, Worcestershire, Warwickshire, Bath and North East Somerset). The postcard image of the classic English countryside: golden "Cotswold stone" houses with tiled or thatched roofs, flowery cottage gardens, traditional pubs, sheep in the meadows. July is my favourite month (7.8/10): 13-20°C, 42% rain, mild temperatures ideal for hiking. From May to September, it's the tourist high season: 8-20°C, unpredictable English climate (can be freezing even in July), long days (15h of daylight in June), gardens in full bloom June-July. From November to March, expect 1-10°C and 36-45% rain: damp cold, few tourists, unique charm of the English winter countryside (smoking chimneys, pubs with wood fires). Signature villages to visit: Bibury (often called "the most beautiful village in England" by William Morris in the 19th century, Arlington Row = former 14th-century wool granary converted into cottages, on a 19th-century British banknote, viral Instagram photo), Bourton-on-the-Water ("Venice of the Cotswolds" for its Windrush river crossing the centre with stone bridges, model village in the gardens), Stow-on-the-Wold (old market village, the imposing church of Saint Edward with door between 2 thousand-year-old yews — likely inspiration for Tolkien's "Door of Durin"), Castle Combe ("most beautiful village in England" according to others, preserved 14th century, filming location of several films including War Horse Spielberg 2011), Lower and Upper Slaughter ("slaughter" = comes from old English "slo" = wet place, no massacre, two villages on the edge of a river), Snowshill, Painswick, Chipping Campden, Burford, Broadway. Castles and gardens: Blenheim Palace at Woodstock (UNESCO, 18th-century baroque palace, birthplace of Winston Churchill 1874), Hidcote Manor Garden and Sudeley Castle. Cotswold Way (long-distance trail 164 km Chipping Campden-Bath, 7-10 day hike). Excursions: Bath 1h away, Oxford 30 min-1h away, Stratford-upon-Avon (Shakespeare's birthplace). Try traditional cream tea (scones + clotted cream + jam Cornish or Devonshire = regional war over the order of cream then jam or vice versa), local pub ales (Hook Norton, Donnington's), warm pies. Logistics: no central station in the Cotswolds, better to rent a car from London, Bristol or Birmingham.
hallstatt:en: Hallstatt, Austrian village of 800 inhabitants on the edge of the Hallstättersee: UNESCO-listed in 1997 for its heritage of pre-Roman salt mines (active for 7000 years, one of the oldest in the world). Postcard alpine setting (mountains, lake, bell towers), oversaturated since 2010 (10,000 visitors/day in summer for 800 inhabitants). August is my favourite month (7.9/10): 15-25°C, 59% rain in altitude storms, mild temperatures. From July to September, it's the high season: 12-24°C, but extreme crowds (the "Hallstatt Selfie Spot" on the lakeside promenade is overloaded, crowds of Asian tourists by bus from Salzburg or Vienna). The municipality installed barriers in 2023 to manage the flow. From December to March, expect -2/8°C and 43-55% precipitation often as snow: Hallstatt under the snow is spectacular, with skiing nearby (Dachstein-West 10 min away, Krippenstein), far fewer tourists (except weekends). Highlights: Marktplatz (main square), Protestant Church (signature bell tower with the lake in the background), Salzwelten (the world's oldest salt mines, accessible by funicular from the village + small train in the galleries, panorama over 5 lakes from the World Heritage Skywalk at the summit 350m above the lake), Eis Riesenwelt 1h away (the world's largest ice cave, at Werfen, open May-October). Logistics: Hallstatt 1h15 from Salzburg by car (mandatory parking before the village, pedestrian access only), Hallstatt-Markt train + 10-min ferry from the station. Try Wiener Schnitzel, local cheeses. Plan 1 night minimum to enjoy the atmosphere outside tour-operator hours.
dresde:en: Dresden, capital of Saxony in Germany: 560,000 inhabitants on the edge of the Elbe. Nicknamed the "Florence of the Elbe" ("Elbflorenz") for its exceptional baroque architecture, former capital of the Kingdom of Saxony (Wettin dynasty 13th century-1918). Massively bombed by the Allies from 13 to 15 February 1945 at the end of the war (35,000 deaths, 90% of the old town razed, controversy over the necessity of the bombing still debated today), patient reconstruction over 50 years, Frauenkirche rebuilt last in 2005. July is my favourite month (8.6/10): 15-25°C, 36% rain, ideal temperatures. From June to August are the tourist high season: 14-25°C. From November to March, expect -1/9°C and 36-46% rain: continental cold, possible snow, Striezelmarkt Christmas market (the oldest in Germany since 1434). Highlights: Frauenkirche ("Notre-Dame de Dresden", baroque 18th century, 96m stone dome considered one of the most beautiful in Europe, destroyed 1945 and left in ruins for 50 years as a memorial, rebuilt identically 1994-2005 thanks to international donations, some original blackened stones are incorporated into the rebuilt façade = signature dark/light chequer effect), Zwinger (baroque palace 1709-1728, one of the baroque masterpieces of Germany, houses the Gemäldegalerie Alte Meister with the Sistine Madonna by Raphael 1512 = one of the best-known works of the Renaissance), Semperoper (neo-Renaissance opera house 1841-1878 by Gottfried Semper, one of the most prestigious in Germany), Residenzschloss (royal palace of the Wettin, houses the Grünes Gewölbe = "Green Vaults" = one of the largest collections of court treasures in Europe, 4000 precious objects), Brühlsche Terrasse ("balcony of Europe" = elevated promenade by the Elbe), Fürstenzug ("Procession of Princes", 102m fresco in Meissen porcelain 1907 showing 35 Saxon rulers, miraculously spared by the 1945 bombings), Cathedral of the Holy Trinity (Hofkirche, former Catholic church of the Saxon court), Neustadt ("new town" on the north bank, alternative quarter Kunsthofpassage, bohemian atmosphere). Excursions: Saxon Switzerland 30 min away (Sächsische Schweiz, national park with the famous Bastei Bridge rock formations, spectacular hikes), Meissen 30 min away (porcelain town, first European manufactory 1710), Berlin 2h by train, Prague 2h by bus. Try Sauerbraten (marinated roast), Kartoffelsuppe (potato soup), Eierschecke (three-storey Saxon cake), Christstollen (PGI Christmas cake from Dresden since the 15th century).
hawaii:en: Big Island (officially Hawaiʻi) is the largest and youngest of the archipelago: active volcanoes (Kilauea, Mauna Loa), 11 climate zones on a single island, observatories on Mauna Kea (4205m). July is my favourite month (8.8/10): 20-27°C, 32% rain on the dry west side (Kona/Kohala), much wetter at Hilo on the east (4500mm/year, one of the rainiest cities in the USA). Volcanoes National Park remains the essential stop: unpredictable eruptions, Kilauea has erupted several times since 2020. Mauna Kea at sunset (4205m, plan a down jacket even in July) with public stargazing twice a week. From December to May, expect 15-24°C with 38-56% rain on the east side: humpback whale season offshore. The high tourist season remains December-April (dry season): prices x2-x3. For the diversity (snorkelling at Kealakekua, black/green beaches, national park), plan 5-7 days minimum.
nashville:en: Nashville, capital of Tennessee, is "Music City": world capital of country music, 180 recording studios, Grand Ole Opry since 1925. September-October are my favourite months (8.3-8.5/10): 12-29°C, 26-32% rain, ideal temperatures, foliage in Tennessee. From June to August, expect 20-32°C: hot and humid, but it's the season for outdoor concerts and festivals (CMA Fest early June, Bonnaroo 1h away in June). From December to February, expect -1/13°C and 35-46% rain: mild for the south but greyish. From April to May: ideal temperatures, flowers everywhere. Highlights: Country Music Hall of Fame, Ryman Auditorium (Mother Church of Country, former Grand Ole Opry), Grand Ole Opry House (concert every evening), Honky Tonks on Broadway (Tootsie's, Robert's Western World, free, live music from 10am to 3am), RCA Studio B (where Elvis recorded 200 songs). Try Hot Chicken (Prince's or Hattie B's, spicy at the "shut the cluck up" level), BBQ at Martin's, biscuits at Loveless Cafe. Vanderbilt and Belle Meade Plantation for the southern historical context.
charleston:en: Charleston, in South Carolina: 150,000 inhabitants, English colonial city (1670), preserved historic centre, major role in slavery and the Civil War. Regularly ranked among the most beautiful cities in the USA. May is my favourite month (8.7/10): 20-27°C, 38% rain, end of spring with azaleas and magnolias in full bloom. April-June are the tourist high season: 16-29°C, 24-54% rain. From June to September, it's the cyclone season (US east coast): 38-64% rainfall, oppressive humidity (Charleston can hit records of feels-like heat). From December to February, expect 6-17°C and 28-32% rain: mild for the south, low tourist footfall. October-November are my real sweet spots: 11-25°C, clear skies after the cyclone season. Highlights: Old town (200 colonial houses pre-1860 preserved, more than any other US city), Battery Park and Rainbow Row (colourful houses, signature photo), Old Slave Mart Museum (former slave market, memorial), Fort Sumter (first shot of the Civil War in 1861, ferry from Liberty Square), Magnolia Plantation and Drayton Hall (former plantations 30 min away, sensitive but educational topic), Boone Hall Plantation (centuries-old oak avenue used in The Patriot and Forrest Gump). Try shrimp and grits (Lowcountry dish), she-crab soup, fried green tomatoes, sweet tea. 1h from Hilton Head Island. Direct flight from NY/DC/Boston.
san-diego:en: San Diego is the most stable Californian climate of the south: morning clouds (May Gray, June Gloom) then afternoon sunshine, never extreme heat thanks to the cold California Current. July-August are my favourite months (9.3/10): 18-25°C, 0-2% rain, sea at 19-21°C (cool for Europeans). From December to March, expect 8-17°C and 18-28% rain: this is the wet Californian season, but it stays bearable and lets you avoid the crowds. The border with Tijuana 30 minutes away opens onto a different world (cross on foot rather than by car). Balboa Park (free museums on Tuesday for residents), La Jolla (sea lions, kayaking), Coronado (golden beach, cycling) are the classics. Comic-Con in late July fills the city completely for 4 days.
portland:en: Portland, in Oregon (USA): 650,000 inhabitants in the urban area, on the Willamette River (which crosses the city and flows into the Columbia 30 km to the north).
