Highlights: Aso Rock ("Rock of Aso", 400m granite monolith dominating Abuja, signature visual of the capital, houses the presidential residence Aso Villa at its foot), Abuja National Mosque (1984, one of the largest mosques in sub-Saharan Africa, signature golden dome and 4 minarets), Our Lady of Assumption National Cathedral ("Our Lady Queen of Nigeria", large modern Catholic cathedral 2005), Eagle Square (official central square where official state ceremonies take place), Millennium Park (the largest public park in Abuja, opened in 2003 by Queen Elizabeth II), Jabi Lake (recreational urban lake), Zuma Rock 1h away (other 725m granite monolith signature of Abuja landscapes, more impressive than Aso Rock), Wuse Market, Ushafa Pottery Village (traditional Yoruba-Igbo potter craftsman village). Nigeria excursions: Lagos 1h flight or 8h by bus (economic capital 21 million inhabitants in urban area = the largest sub-Saharan African megacity, already covered by BDW), Yankari National Park 5h away (the most accessible park in Nigeria, elephants + lions + thermal Wikki Warm Springs), Calabar in the south-east (renowned for its annual December carnival, one of the largest carnivals in Africa, festive atmosphere). Safety: Abuja globally safer than Lagos but increased France Diplomacy vigilance: Boko Haram Islamist terrorism active in the north-east of the country (Borno + Yobe + Adamawa states near Lake Chad), tourist kidnappings possible, moderate urban crime in Abuja, attacks in Abuja in 2014 + 2022 targeting shopping centres and bus stations. Try jollof rice (West African signature, eternal culinary dispute Nigeria vs Ghana vs Senegal for the best version), egusi soup (melon seed soup), suya (spicy chilli + peanut skewers signature of the Hausa), pounded yam, pepper soup, palm wine, Star Lager + Gulder beer (Nigerian beers). Abuja Nnamdi Azikiwe airport (ABV).
accra:en: Accra, capital of Ghana on the Gulf of Guinea coast: 2.5 million inhabitants intra-muros, 4 million in urban area. Ghana = first British colony in sub-Saharan Africa to obtain independence (6 March 1957 under Kwame Nkrumah, influential pan-African leader). Today considered one of the most stable democracies in West Africa, rapidly growing economy (2nd world cocoa producer after Ivory Coast), West African cultural capital. August is my favourite month (8.1/10): 24-27°C, 18% rain, this is the ideal dry season. From July to February, it's the relative dry season: 23-31°C with short "little dry season" in July-August in the heart of the south-west monsoon. From March to June and September-October, expect 23-32°C with 28-79% rain: rainy season ("major rainy season" May-June, "minor rainy season" September-October). From December to February, harmattan wind = dry dusty Saharan wind that refreshes but reduces visibility. Accra highlights: Independence Square ("Black Star Square", 3rd largest public square in the world after Tiananmen and Red Square, with independence arch and pan-African signature black star), Kwame Nkrumah Memorial Park (mausoleum of the father of Ghanaian independence, statue, museum in his former house), Jamestown (historic fishing quarter, James fort 17th century British slave trade place, popular traditional atmosphere), Osu Quarter (the liveliest in Accra, restaurants and nightlife, Oxford Street the most famous in West Africa), Makola Market (the largest traditional market in Accra, West African atmosphere, kente fabrics, spices), National Museum of Ghana (Ashanti and other Ghanaian ethnic group traditional artefacts), W.E.B. Du Bois Memorial Centre (the African-American intellectual father of Pan-Africanism lived and is buried here), Labadi Beach (the urban beach of Accra, lively at the weekend), Bojo Beach 30 min away (more peaceful beach). Essential Ghana excursions: Cape Coast and Elmina Castles 3h away (UNESCO 1979, former British and Dutch slave fortresses 15th-19th century = the "Door of No Return" through which millions of slaves were embarked towards the Americas between 1500-1850, overwhelming memorial place frequented by African-American descendants in roots pilgrimage, Barack Obama gave a memorable speech here 2009), Kakum National Park 3h away (jungle canopy accessible by Canopy Walkway = suspended walkways 350m at 30m high between the trees, one of the longest canopy walkways in the world), Mole National Park in the north (safari park with elephants observable on foot = unique in Africa), Lake Volta (the largest artificial lake in the world by surface area 8500 km², created Akosombo dam 1965). Try jollof rice (the most famous West African dish, rice in spicy tomato sauce, culinary war between Ghana/Senegal/Nigeria for paternity), banku and tilapia, fufu (kneaded cassava/yam ball), kelewele (fried spicy plantain), waakye (rice beans), bissap (hibiscus juice). Kotoka Accra airport (ACC).
kumasi:en: Kumasi, 2nd city of Ghana and cultural capital of the Ashanti region: 2.5 million inhabitants in urban area. Historic capital of the Ashanti / Asante Kingdom 1670-1957 = one of the most powerful West African kingdoms 17th-19th century, famous for its gold (Gold Coast = colonial name of Ghana) and its prolonged armed resistance against the British (5 Anglo-Ashanti wars 1823-1900 until final defeat). Today the Asantehene = king of the Ashantis Otumfuo Osei Tutu II still reigns symbolically over 5 million Ashantis with his Golden Throne = major Ghanaian cultural signature that was never taken by the British unlike the other African thrones. July is my favourite month (7.4/10): 22-29°C, 50% rain, this is the relative "little dry season". Kumasi never tops 7.4/10 (humid equatorial climate year-round). From June to August, it's the "little dry season": 22-30°C. From December to February, it's the relative dry season with Saharan harmattan: 22-33°C. From March to May and September-November, these are the rainy seasons: 39-78% rainfall. Kumasi highlights: Manhyia Palace (royal palace of the Asantehene king, Manhyia museum with Ashanti royal regalia + historical photos + signature ceremonial objects), Kumasi Central Market ("Kejetia Market", one of the largest open-air markets in West Africa with 11,000 stalls, extraordinary atmosphere), Ashanti Cultural Centre and National Cultural Centre (traditional Ashanti craftsmanship: signature colourful kente fabrics worldwide recognised Ghanaian cultural + Akan wood sculpture + gold jewellery), Saint Peter Cathedral, Tomb of Yaa Asantewaa (tomb of the Queen Mother Yaa Asantewaa 1840-1921 Ghanaian national heroine who led the last Ashanti rebellion against the British 1900 = pan-African feminist symbol), Lake Bosomtwe 30 km away (sacred Ashanti meteorite crater lake, one of the only natural lakes in Ghana, traditional ritual swimming), Owabi Wildlife Sanctuary. Essential Ghana excursions: Cape Coast Castle and Elmina Castle 4h south (UNESCO 1979, former Portuguese-Dutch-British slave forts 15th-19th century on the Gold Coast, embarkation places of millions of West African slaves to the Americas, signature memorial of the transatlantic slave trade overwhelming = African-American Roots Heritage Tour pilgrimage place), Kakum National Park 4h south (equatorial forest park, Canopy Walkway = canopy walkways at 30m high signature Ghana tourist), Mole National Park in the north (the largest national park in Ghana, elephants + lions + buffaloes), Accra 4h by bus (capital, already covered by BDW). Safety: Kumasi and Ghana in general globally among the most stable and safe African countries ("Africa's Gold Star"), moderate urban crime. Try fufu (signature Ghanaian culinary hand-pounded cassava-plantain ball), banku (fermented corn balls), kelewele (fried spicy plantain), jollof rice (eternal culinary dispute Nigeria vs Ghana vs Senegal for the best version), waakye (rice beans), red red, palm wine, Star + Club + Guinness Foreign Extra Stout (the African version of Guinness brewed locally). Kumasi airport (KMS).
dakar:en: Dakar, capital of Senegal: 1.1 million inhabitants (3 million in urban area), at the western tip of the African continent (westernmost point of continental Africa = Cap-Vert peninsula). West African hub (seat of several pan-African institutions, regional air hub). Considered one of the most stable and democratic capitals in West Africa (Senegal has never had a coup since independence 1960). January-December are my favourite months (9.1/10): 20-28°C, 1-2% rain, this is the dry season with refreshing trade winds from the Atlantic. From November to May, it's the tourist high season: 19-30°C, 0-1% rain, ideal dry and cool climate. From June to October, it's the rainy season ("hivernage"): 22-49% rainfall, but brief afternoon showers, stable temperatures 23-29°C. Highlights: Gorée Island (UNESCO 1978, 20 min by ferry from Dakar, former centre of transatlantic slave trade 15th-19th century, House of Slaves with its "Door of No Return" poignant memorial — although historians debate the actual numerical importance of Gorée in the trade, the site remains symbolically major, visited by Obama 2013, Mandela, Pope Francis), Sandaga Market (the largest in Dakar), Plateau (administrative centre and historic colonial), African Memory Cathedral (1936), Mosque of the Divinity (modern architecture 1973), African Renaissance Monument (49m statue inaugurated 2010 by Wade for the 50th anniversary of independence, one of the most controversial in Africa), Museum of Black Civilisations (2018), Soumbédioune Beach (fish market), Pink Lake / Lake Retba 30 km away (natural pink-fuchsia water due to algae + salt, former arrival of the Paris-Dakar Rally 1979-2008). Excursions: Saint-Louis 4h north (UNESCO 2000, former French colonial capital), Saly and Petite Côte in the south (beach resorts), Casamance in the south (green region, totally different from the north, accessible by boat or plane). Try thieboudienne ("rice with fish" national dish), yassa with chicken, mafé (peanut sauce), bissap (hibiscus drink).
senegal:en: Senegal, country in West Africa on the Atlantic coast: 18 million inhabitants. Former French colony 1659-1960 (one of the oldest French colonies in Africa), historic gateway for the transatlantic slave trade. Country among the most democratically stable in francophone West Africa (never had a military coup, regular peaceful political alternations). Capital Dakar, official language French, vehicular language Wolof. November is my favourite month (6.8/10): 21-30°C, 4% rain, this is the ideal dry season. Senegal never tops 6.8/10 (hot sub-Saharan tropical climate). From November to May, it's the dry season: 17-31°C, pleasant dry and hot climate, trade winds refresh the Atlantic coast, harmattan Saharan wind December-February = relative cold and dusty. From June to October, it's the rainy season (West African monsoon "hivernage"): 28-50% rainfall, milder temperatures 23-32°C, greener landscapes but roads sometimes impassable inland. Highlights: Dakar (capital, already covered by BDW, Atlantic peninsula, cosmopolitan West African urban atmosphere), Gorée Island 20 min by ferry from Dakar (UNESCO 1978, one of the most poignant memorial sites in the world on the slave trade, House of Slaves with "Door of No Return" symbolic, overwhelming atmosphere, African-American and Obamesque pilgrimage), Saint-Louis du Sénégal 4h away (UNESCO 2000, former capital of French West Africa 17th-1902, preserved colonial architecture, unique atmosphere on island connected by Faidherbe Bridge 507m 19th-century Eiffel), Shell Island near Saint-Louis (millions of oyster shells accumulated by 200 years of traditional fishing), Pink Lake (Lake Retba) 1h from Dakar (pink salt lake thanks to halophilic algae Dunaliella salina, historic arrival point of the Paris-Dakar Rally 1979-2008 before moving to South America, hand-harvested salt extraction by women), Lompoul Desert 3h from Dakar (mini Saharan desert 18 km² with 50m dunes, Bedouin-Moroccan camps, "affordable" desert experience), Petite Côte (Saly, Mbour classic tourist beach resorts), Casamance in the south (more humid tropical forest region, Diola people, Cap Skirring beach resorts), Niokolo-Koba National Park in the south-east (UNESCO 1981 but in danger, last Senegalese savannah with lions and hippopotamuses, inadvisable for insecurity), Bandia Reserve 1h30 from Dakar (well-managed private reserve to observe giraffes, zebras, rhinoceros, secure alternative to Niokolo). Try thieboudienne (rice with fish, Senegalese national dish inscribed UNESCO intangible heritage 2021), yassa (chicken with onion lemon), mafé (peanut sauce), bissap (hibiscus juice), fresh ginger, spicy Touba coffee. Dakar Blaise Diagne airport (DSS) opened 2017 50 km from Dakar.
abidjan:en: Abidjan, economic capital of Ivory Coast on the Gulf of Guinea coast: 5 million inhabitants in urban area (the 2nd most populated in francophone West Africa after Lagos). Former chief town of French West Africa (AOF) and historic capital until 1983 when Houphouët-Boigny transferred the political seat to Yamoussoukro his native village (but Abidjan remains the de facto economic capital, with 90% of the country's economic activity, nicknamed "the Pearl of the Lagoons" then "Africa's Manhattan" for its skyscraper skyline rare in sub-Saharan Africa outside Johannesburg). August is my favourite month (7.5/10): 23-27°C, 51% rain, this is the relative dry season. Abidjan never tops 7.5/10 (humid equatorial tropical climate hot year-round). From November to February, it's the dry season: 23-32°C, more bearable climate, harmattan Saharan atmosphere December-January. From May to July, it's the great rainy season: 70-80% rainfall. From October, it's the small rainy season. Abidjan highlights: Plateau (business centre with skyscrapers from the 1970s-2000s, CGRAE Tower, Postel Tower 2001 21 storeys), Saint Paul Cathedral (1985, modern architecture by Aldo Spirito spectacular, figurative stained glass representing African evangelisation, one of the most impressive cathedrals in Africa), Cocody Quarter (smart residential with embassies, presidential palace), Marcory Quarter (historic French residential), Treichville Quarter (popular, lively traditional markets), Félix Houphouët-Boigny University (Cocody campus, one of the largest in francophone Africa), Treichville Market (the most traditional in Abidjan, pagne fabrics, spices, traditional masks), Museum of Civilisations of Ivory Coast (traditional Senufo, Baoulé, Dan mask artefacts), Ébrié Lagoon (urban lagoon of Abidjan 566 km² with traditional pinasse ferries between quarters), Assinie-Mafia Beach 1h30 away (Ivorian beach resort favourite of Abidjanese on weekends, beach hotels). Ivory Coast excursions: Yamoussoukro 3h away (nominal political capital with Basilica of Our Lady of Peace 1989 = the largest Christian church in the world by ground area, copy inspired by St Peter's Vatican but 1.5x larger, 158m high, capacity 18,000 places, integrally financed by Houphouët-Boigny himself 300 million USD, controversy about appropriateness in poor country, little used as functional church), Grand-Bassam 30 min away (UNESCO 2012, former French colonial capital 1893-1900, preserved colonial architecture, beach), Taï National Park 8h west (UNESCO, one of the last primary forests in West Africa, chimpanzees, endemic pygmy hippopotamuses), Korhogo north ("Senufo capital", traditional textile and mask craftsmanship), Man (the Teeth of Man mountains, spectacular Dan stilt mask tradition). Safety: Abidjan globally safe in the Plateau/Cocody/Marcory quarters, caution Treichville/Adjamé/Yopougon. Try attiéké (cassava couscous, Ivorian national dish) with grilled fish, kedjenou (chicken in earthen pot), foutou (pounded plantain), aloko (fried plantain), bangui (palm wine), bissap. Abidjan Félix Houphouët-Boigny airport (ABJ).
kinshasa:en: Kinshasa, capital of the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC, "Congo-Kinshasa") on the Congo River: 17 million inhabitants in urban area = 2nd largest sub-Saharan African megacity after Lagos Nigeria, projection 35 million by 2050 = future largest city in Africa. Chaotic and frenetic capital ("Kin la Belle" marketing nickname vs reality "Kin la Poubelle" resigned local nickname), facing Brazzaville on the other side of the Congo River. Former Belgian colony "Belgian Congo" 1908-1960 (Congolese genocide under Leopold II 1885-1908 = 10-15 million deaths for rubber exploitation, one of the worst colonial crimes of the 19th century), independence 1960, Mobutu dictatorship 1965-1997, Congo wars 1996-2003 = the deadliest war since WWII with 5.4 million deaths from disease + famine + direct violence. June is my favourite month (9.1/10): 18-29°C, 1% rain, this is the ideal dry season. May-August stay excellent (8.0-8.7/10). From May to September, it's the dry season "cacimbo": 18-30°C, dry and cool tropical climate. From October to April, it's the equatorial humid season: 50-65% rainfall. Kinshasa highlights: Boulevard du 30 Juin (main artery of Kinshasa, tribute to the date of independence 1960), Stade des Martyrs (80,000 places, one of the largest stadiums in Africa), Central Market of Kinshasa ("Marché Central", extraordinary-chaotic-extreme atmosphere), Academy of Fine Arts ("Kinshasa school of painting" = Congolese painting school recognised worldwide, artists Chéri Samba + Moke + Bodys Isek Kingelez), National Museum of the DRC (African ethnographic collection, signature ritual masks Lega-Kuba-Songye-Pende-Yaka), Symphonie des Mille Et Une Nuits (amusement park), Quay of the Congo River (river atmosphere facing Brazzaville visible 4 km away), Lola ya Bonobo 30 min away ("Bonobo Paradise", sanctuary of the bonobos = great ape species endemic only to the DRC, more genetically close to humans than chimpanzees but much more peaceful), Kinshasa Cathedral (Notre-Dame du Congo). Essential DRC excursions = this is why the DRC is visited despite the dangers: Virunga National Park (UNESCO 1979 the oldest national park in Africa 1925, houses mountain gorillas Mount Mikeno + the Nyiragongo volcano 3470m with permanent lava lake one of the most active in the world, 5h hike to the summit for bivouac above the crater = signature world volcanic experience, but Rwandan border very unstable zone and current M23 conflict = inadvisable), Garamba National Park (UNESCO 1980 in danger, last northern white rhinoceros before near-total extinction by poaching), Lola ya Bonobo, Brazzaville 15 min by ferry (unique world signature, see Brazzaville). Safety: increased France Diplomacy vigilance for Kinshasa, extreme urban crime (frequent armed robberies), current M23 + ADF + other armed groups wars in Kivu (east of the DRC Rwanda-Uganda border) = the 2nd largest humanitarian crisis in the world after Sudan, very inadvisable travel except professional escort, do not attempt Virunga without experienced organisation. Try moambé (signature Congolese culinary, chicken or fish in red palm nut sauce), saka saka (cassava leaves), pondu, mikate (Congolese beignets), thomson (fish), cassava fufu, Primus + Skol Congolese beers. Kinshasa N'Djili airport (FIH).
douala:en: Douala, economic capital of Cameroon on the Gulf of Guinea at the mouth of the Wouri river: 3 million inhabitants in urban area. Political capital = Yaoundé (250 km inland, higher in altitude). Douala is the 2nd most populated city in Central Africa after Kinshasa and the main port of Central Africa (60% of imports Cameroon + Chad + landlocked Central Africa). Very humid equatorial climate. January-December are my favourite months (6.5-6.7/10): 24-32°C, 56-63% rain, this is the relative "little dry season". Douala never tops 6.7/10 (very humid equatorial climate year-round with brief lull December-January). From December to February, it's the relatively least humid season: 24-32°C. From March to November, it's the near-continuous wet season: 81-97% rainfall in Douala (one of the rainiest cities in Africa with 4000 mm/year, comparable to neighbouring Kribi). Douala highlights: La Nouvelle Liberté (modern statue 12m sculpted by Joseph-Francis Sumegne, contemporary signature of Douala = "The New Liberated Africa", in bronze and steel, roundabouts), Flower Market and Central Market (traditional markets), Saints Peter and Paul Cathedral (1936 French colonial architecture), Mandessi Bell Pagoda (19th-century Duala princely residence), Maritime Museum of Douala (port and maritime history of Cameroon), Bonanjo Quarter (administrative quarter and preserved smart French colonial), Edéa Beaches and Limbé 1-2h away (Limbé volcanic black sand beach, former British colonial Victoria with 19th-century botanical garden). Cameroon excursions: Mount Cameroon 2h via Buéa (4040m, highest summit in West Africa, active volcano, 2-3 day ascent = signature experience, last eruption 2012), Korup National Park 3h away (one of the oldest tropical forests in Africa 60 million years, exceptional biodiversity), Kribi Beach 3h away (already covered by BDW, golden beaches + Lobé waterfalls flowing directly into the ocean = unique world signature), Yaoundé capital 4h away (already covered), Foumban 6h away (royal palace of the Bamoun, the most powerful sultanate of Cameroon 18th-19th century, Bamoun art unique in Africa), Waza National Park in the north 18h by bus (lions and elephants, inadvisable for Boko Haram insecurity Nigerian border), Dja Reserve (UNESCO 1987, primary tropical forest, Baka pygmy peoples). Safety: Douala globally stable but significant urban crime (frequent armed robberies, assaults). Anglophone crisis in the South-West and North-West regions since 2017 (Anglophone "Ambazonian" separatists 20% minority of Cameroon colonised by British vs 80% francophone majority), generalised violence, inadvisable France Diplomacy. Northern border with Nigeria affected by Boko Haram. Try ndolé (bitter leaf vegetable in meat/fish sauce, Cameroonian national dish), poulet DG ("General Director", chicken in tomato plantain sauce), eru (leaf vegetable with waterleaf), Kribi-style grilled fish with miondo (fermented cassava sticks), bobolo, local "33" Export or Castel beers. Douala airport (DLA), main airport of Cameroon, Air France-Cameroon Airlines hub.
libreville:en: Libreville, capital of Gabon on the Atlantic coast of Central Africa: 800,000 inhabitants in urban area = 50% of the total Gabonese population. Former French colony "Gabon" 1885-1960 (the name "Libreville" 1849 refers to the liberation of slaves bought back by the French from the Brazilian slave ship Elizia = abolitionist symbol), country rich in offshore oil (4th African producer), precious wood (okoumé), manganese, iron. Modest population 2.3 million on 267,668 km² = one of the least densely populated countries in Africa (8 inhab/km²) thanks to 88% of preserved primary equatorial forest. July is my favourite month (8.2/10): 21-27°C, 32% rain, this is the ideal dry season. June-August stay excellent (7.7-8.0/10). From June to August, it's the great dry season "little dry season" ironically named as it is the longest: 21-28°C, sea at 25°C, the most pleasant dry and cool tropical climate of the year. From September to May, it's the equatorial humid season: 84-99% rainfall (Gabon is one of the most rained countries in the world with 3000-4000 mm/year). Libreville highlights: Saint Mary Cathedral of Libreville (1958 French colonial heritage), Hassan II Mosque ("Grand Mosque", financed by King Mohammed VI Morocco), Mont-Bouët Market (the largest market in Libreville, West African atmosphere), Libreville Beach / Sablière (urban Atlantic beach), Pointe Denis 30 min by boat (peninsula of preserved beaches with high-end resorts), Presidential Palace (presidential residence visible from outside), National Museum of Arts and Traditions of Gabon (Fang-Punu-Kota signature ritual masks of African art), Petit-Paris quarter, Akanda smart suburb. Essential Gabon excursions = this is why one visits Gabon: Lopé National Park 5h away (UNESCO 2007, mosaic forest savannah unique in Central Africa, lowland gorillas + chimpanzees + mandrills + forest elephants + 19 traditional ethnic communities), Loango National Park 8h away ("Africa's Last Eden" nicknamed by National Geographic 2002, the only place in the world where one can see elephants and hippopotamuses on the Atlantic beach + gorillas + buffaloes + signature unique world leatherback turtles in nesting), Ivindo National Park (UNESCO 2021, spectacular Kongou and Mingouli waterfalls, preserved jungle, baïs = marshy clearings where wildlife gathers), Minkébé National Park (one of the last refuges of forest elephants threatened by poaching), Lambaréné 4h away (Albert Schweitzer Hospital 1913 = medical mission of the 1952 Nobel Peace Prize Albert Schweitzer, become a humanitarian pilgrimage place), Pointe Denis near Libreville. Safety: Libreville globally safe by African standards, moderate urban crime. Try nyembwe (chicken in red palm nut sauce signature Gabonese), saka saka (cassava leaves), grilled captain fish, chicken in moambé, atanga (Gabonese fruit), Beaufort lager + local Régab beer. Libreville Léon-Mba airport (LBV).
luanda:en: Luanda, capital of Angola on the Atlantic coast of southern Africa: 8 million inhabitants in urban area. Former Portuguese colony (1575-1975), independence by colonial war then civil war MPLA-UNITA-FNLA for 27 years (1975-2002, one of the longest and deadliest African conflicts of the 20th century). Today Angola = 2nd oil producer in sub-Saharan Africa (after Nigeria), Luanda is paradoxically one of the most expensive cities in the world for expatriates (top 5 worldwide according to Mercer, due to chronic inflation and rare imported goods). June is my favourite month (9.5/10): 18-24°C, 3% rain, this is the ideal cool dry season. From June to September, it's the dry season: 16-27°C, this is the relative tourist high season (but Angola = still little touristic). From January to May, it's the wet season: 21-30°C with 9-26% rain, hotter and more humid. From October to December, expect 21-30°C: transition to wet season. Paradoxical temperate climate: Luanda is on the geographic equator but the cold Benguela current rising from Antarctica along the West African coast cools the climate = Luanda benefits from a unique freshness in tropical zone, "cold desert" hyper-arid and cool type climate. Highlights: San Miguel Fortress (1576, Portuguese colonial architecture, houses the Armed Forces Museum + panorama over Luanda Bay), Luanda Cathedral (16th-century Portuguese Jesuit), N.S. de Nazaré Church (16th century), National Museum of Anthropology (one of the best in Africa on Bantu cultures), National Slavery Museum (30 km south, in the former Portuguese chapel where slaves were baptised before transatlantic deportation to Brazil = Angola = main source of slaves to Brazil 1500-1850, 4 million Angolans deported), Marginal of Luanda (renovated 2012 seaside promenade, luxurious atmosphere contrasting with neighbouring musseques shantytowns), Ilha do Cabo (urban peninsula with beaches, smart seafood restaurants), Roque Santeiro Market (one of the largest informal markets in Africa, chaotic atmosphere, partially dismantled 2010 but persisting). Excursions: Mussulo (peninsula in the south with preserved beaches), Kissama National Park 70 km away (reserve with elephants, antelopes, restocking after civil war), Quedas de Kalandula 4h away (105m, one of the largest waterfalls in Africa, little known), Lubango in the south (Serra da Leba mountains, temperate climate, Cristo Rei statue copy of Rio in large), Benguela (south coast, beaches, former colonial capital). Safety: high urban crime in Luanda (frequent armed robberies, inadvisable to go out alone at night), northern and eastern provinces residual civil war anti-personnel mines. Try moamba de galinha (chicken in palm oil peanut sauce, national dish), funje (cassava flour mash), calulu (dried fish dish), jindungo (spicy sauce), Angolan coffee (one of the best in Africa but little known, Robusta plantations), cerveja Cuca (national beer). Logistics: Luanda Quatro de Fevereiro airport (LAD), mandatory visa for most nationalities, e-visa since 2018.
maputo:en: Maputo keeps its Portuguese Art Deco character and its tropical Mediterranean side. Humid climate, scorching summer. August-September are my favourite months (9.0/10): 16-27°C, 9-19% rain, constant sunshine. Macaneta beach 30 km away is accessible year-round. From December to February (austral summer), expect 22-30°C with 43-54% rainfall: heavy humidity, cyclone risks (Mozambique in moderate risk zone). Inhambane and the Bazaruto archipelago 6-8h by road are the real cards of Mozambique. The Janeta markets and the Portuguese-Mozambican cuisine (chamuças, peri-peri prawns) remain alive. Safety has improved but remains to be checked at night outside the Polana and Sommerschield quarters. Direct flights from Lisbon facilitate access from Europe.
durban:en: Durban is the other face of South Africa: less photographed than Cape Town but more tropical. Subtropical humid climate, sea always warm (22-26°C). January stays my favourite month (8.6/10) despite 38% rain: these showers are brief, the warm Indian Ocean catches up everything. From May to August (local austral winter), expect 12-24°C with 12-19% rain. Paradoxically, it's the best season for humpback whales offshore and for hiking in the Drakensberg 3h by road. The Golden Mile remains workable year-round (rare in Africa). From November to March, expect 30-45% precipitation in stormy showers: cyclonic season of neighbouring Mozambique, with sometimes repercussions. The strong Indian community gives Durban a separate cuisine (bunny chow, curry rotis), especially in Grey Street.
johannesburg:en: Johannesburg lives at 1750m altitude: temperate subtropical climate, never really hot. September is my favourite month (9.1/10): 10-25°C, 10% rain, clear sky. From June to August, the nights drop to 4-7°C with mild days (17-21°C). Paradox of a clear and cold winter. From November to March (austral summer), expect 13-26°C with 32-55% precipitation: violent storms in late afternoon, sometimes hail. Safety remains a topic: the Sandton and Rosebank quarters concentrate the secured tourist activity, the city centre (downtown) requires accompaniment. The Apartheid Museum is the obligatory historical passage. Soweto is visited on organised tour. Pretoria 1h by Gautrain. Joburg mainly serves as a hub to Kruger National Park (5h by road or domestic flight), Drakensberg, Cape Town. The local fusion cuisine (boerewors, bunny chow imported from Durban) deserves the stop.
harare:en: Harare, this is African high altitude (1500m) that softens the climate: not the Mozambican heatwave, nor the extreme drought of Botswana. September is my favourite month (9.6/10): 14-28°C, 2% rain, just before the wet season. From November to March, it's the subtropical rainy season: 38-71% stormy showers, often in late afternoon. The roads become muddy in some national parks (Mana Pools, Hwange). The Victoria Falls tourist peak, on the other hand, is in April-May (full waters after the rains). May to August are the cool dry season: 9-23°C, clear sky, ideal for safaris (animals concentrated around water points). The Zimbabwean economic context remains complicated: plan USD cash, few cards accepted outside high-end hotels. Lake Chivero 30 km away offers a nature getaway.
guatemala:en: Guatemala, country in Central America between Mexico and Honduras-Salvador: 17.6 million inhabitants. The most populated country in Central America, the cradle of classical Maya civilisation (Tikal 2nd-9th century), 60% Maya indigenous population = the highest Maya concentration in the world. Capital Guatemala City. Country marked by the civil war 1960-1996 (200,000 deaths including Ixil genocide 1982 under dictator Efraín Ríos Montt = convicted for genocide 2013, freed for senile dementia died 2018), high crime but exceptional landscapes. March is my favourite month (9.2/10): 14-27°C, 15% rain, this is the ideal dry season thanks to altitude. February stays excellent (8.9/10). From November to April, it's the dry season "verano": 13-28°C, temperate climate on the high plateaus thanks to altitude (Guatemala City = 1500m, Antigua = 1530m, Lake Atitlán = 1560m), this is the tourist high season. From May to October, it's the rainy season: 60-84% rainfall, but stable temperatures 15-26°C, greener landscapes (the most visually beautiful season). Highlights: Antigua Guatemala (UNESCO 1979, former capital of the Kingdom of Guatemala = Spanish viceroyalty of Central America 1543-1773, one of the best-preserved Spanish colonial cities in the world, ruins of baroque churches and monasteries destroyed by earthquakes but preserved as is, unique atmosphere paved-bougainvillea-volcanoes in the background = Volcán de Agua 3760m + Volcán de Fuego 3763m + Volcán Acatenango 3976m, 2-day Acatenango hike bivouac panoramic on Fuego in permanent eruption = signature experience of Guatemala), Tikal (UNESCO 1979, one of the largest Maya sites in the world, capital of a great classical Maya kingdom 2nd-9th century, 16 km² of pyramids in the middle of the Petén jungle, signature Templo I Gran Jaguar 47m + Templo IV 64m, exceptional experience early access 6am for howler monkeys and birds, mystical atmosphere unique in the world), Lago Atitlán ("the most beautiful lake in the world" according to Aldous Huxley 1934, alpine volcanic lake surrounded by 3 ideal cone volcanoes + 12 indigenous Maya tzutujil-cakchikel villages preserved on its shores accessible only by lancha, Panajachel = main tourist village, San Pedro La Laguna = backpackers, Santa Cruz = more traditional, San Juan La Laguna = Maya tzutujil textile craftsmanship), Chichicastenango (signature Maya market Thursdays-Sundays, one of the most colourful and spiritual in Latin America, unique atmosphere Maya-Catholic syncretism with shaman rituals in front of the church on the steps), Quiriguá (UNESCO 1981, Maya site with sculpted stelae the highest in the Maya world 8m), Semuc Champey (signature natural turquoise waterfalls in the jungle of Alta Verapaz, swimming in superimposed natural pools 6 levels), Pacaya Volcano (active volcano accessible 1h from Guatemala City, 4h round trip hike to approach incandescent lava flows), Livingston (on the Caribbean coast, unique Garifuna Afro-Caribbean community, accessible only by boat). Safety: Guatemala high crime in Guatemala City (frequent armed robberies, gangs), but Antigua and Lake Atitlán are safe for tourists. Try pepián (signature spicy stew), kak'ik (Maya turkey soup), Guatemalan tamales, fiambre (signature cold salad 1 November All Saints), Antigua/Cobán/Huehuetenango coffee (among the best coffees in the world), Zacapa Centenario rum (one of the great world premium rums, awarded). Guatemala City La Aurora airport (GUA), Flores Mundo Maya airport (FRS) for Tikal.
san-salvador:en: San Salvador, capital of El Salvador in Central America: 525,000 inhabitants intra-muros, 2.4 million in urban area = 40% of the total Salvadoran population. Smallest continental country in Central America = 21,041 km², 6.3 million inhabitants, the only country in Central America without a Caribbean coast (only Pacific). Country marked by the bloody civil war 1980-1992 (75,000 deaths, US-Soviet interference during the Cold War). Today under President Nayib Bukele 2019-today = spectacular authoritarian transformation of the country thanks to the war against the MS-13 and Barrio 18 gangs (massive repression campaign since March 2022 = 80,000 imprisonments in 2 years, homicide rate collapsed from 105/100,000 inhab 2015 to 2/100,000 2024 = global security miracle but criticism for human rights, extrajudicial crackdown). El Salvador = 1st country in the world to adopt Bitcoin as official currency September 2021 (international IMF controversy). January-February are my favourite months (8.9-9.1/10): 21-29°C, 7-9% rain, this is the ideal dry season. March-December stay excellent (7.4-8.5/10). From November to April, it's the dry season "verano": 21-30°C, dry and cool tropical climate thanks to altitude (San Salvador at 660m). From May to October, it's the green season "invierno": 51-94% rainfall, but stable temperatures 21-29°C. San Salvador highlights: Historic centre in majestic restoration since 2022 by Bukele, Metropolitan Cathedral (modern architecture 1956-2024, signature colourful frescoes by Salvadoran artist Fernando Llort, houses the tomb of Mgr Óscar Romero archbishop assassinated by right-wing death squads 24 March 1980 while celebrating mass = martyr canonised saint in 2018 by Pope Francis, one of the great witnesses of Latin American liberation theology of the 20th century), Iglesia El Rosario (modern church 1971 brutalist architecture signature with immense rainbow stained glass interior), Plaza Libertad (historic central square), Mercado Central (traditional central market), Museo de Arte de El Salvador (MARTE, Salvadoran contemporary art), Volcán de San Salvador ("El Boquerón", dormant volcano 1893m visible from the city, hike to the crater 1.5 km in diameter, 360° panorama over the country), Lago de Coatepeque 1h away (spectacular turquoise volcanic crater lake, one of the most beautiful lakes in Central America, swimming and lakeside restaurants). El Salvador excursions = this is why one visits El Salvador: Joya de Cerén 30 min away (UNESCO 1993, "Maya Pompeii" = Maya village buried under volcanic eruption 600 AD perfectly preserved including utensils + food + textiles, unique world archaeological signature revealing daily Maya life), Tazumal (other Maya site at Chalchuapa, well-preserved pyramid), Pacific coast with El Tunco + El Sunzal + Las Flores = capital of surfing in Central America, waves among the most regular in the world signature new tourist of El Salvador), Suchitoto 1h away ("Salvadoran Antigua", preserved Spanish colonial old town with Lake Suchitlán + traditional indigo arts), Volcán Santa Ana 2381m (highest point El Salvador, 4h round trip hike for spectacular signature green turquoise crater), Volcán Izalco ("Pacific Lighthouse" historically, one of the most active volcanoes in the world 1770-1958 then dormant), Ruta de las Flores (panoramic road through coffee villages + indigo plantations). Safety: El Salvador went in 2 years from the most dangerous country in the world to the safest country in Latin America thanks to Bukele's war on gangs, San Salvador globally safe since 2023 (controversial for human rights but transformed visitor experience). Try pupusa ("Salvadoran national dish" = corn flatbread stuffed with cheese / beans / chicharrón, declared UNESCO intangible heritage 2024, to eat in a traditional pupusería signature cultural), curtido (fermented cabbage accompaniment), yuca frita (fried cassava), local Pilsener + Suprema beers, local Tic Tack rum, Salvadoran coffee Bourbon + Pacamara (native variety awarded worldwide). San Salvador Monseñor Óscar Arnulfo Romero airport (SAL).
nicaragua:en: Nicaragua, country in Central America between Honduras and Costa Rica: 6.7 million inhabitants. Capital Managua. Country marked by the Sandinista revolution 1979 (fall of the Somoza dictatorship) then civil war Contra 1981-1990 (rebels armed-financed by Reagan CIA), contested re-election of President Daniel Ortega 2007-today become himself authoritarian (massive demonstrations repressed 2018 = 300 deaths, opponents imprisoned-exiled since). Country among the poorest in Latin America. January-March are my favourite months (6.9/10): 22-34°C, 8-10% rain, this is the ideal dry season. Nicaragua never tops 6.9/10 (hot humid tropical climate year-round). From November to April, it's the dry season: 22-34°C, this is the tourist high season. From May to October, it's the rainy season: 57-88% rainfall. Highlights: Granada (see dedicated review = signature colonial city of the country), León ("the other old colonial capital", historic rival of Granada, more university and revolutionary atmosphere = birthplace of poet Rubén Darío + cradle of Sandinism, León Cathedral UNESCO 2011 = the largest cathedral in Central America, panoramic roof ascent signature), Managua capital (little touristic, ravaged by the 1972 earthquake which destroyed the historic centre, chaotic capital rebuilt randomly), Volcán Masaya 30 min from Managua (one of the only active volcanoes in the world where one can look into the crater of the incandescent lava lake = unique signature experience, access by car to the edge of the Santiago crater, to see at dusk for visual effect), Volcán Concepción and Volcán Maderas (on the Ometepe island in the Lake Nicaragua = the largest volcanic island in freshwater lake in the world, 2 volcanoes in "hourglass", demanding Concepción 1610m hike but exceptional panorama over the largest lake in Central America), San Juan del Sur (south Pacific beach resort, popular surf beach for international surfers, nightlife), Caribbean coast ("Mosquito Coast", little accessible and very different from the rest of the country, indigenous Miskito people, distinct Afro-Caribbean atmosphere, Bluefields), Corn Islands (Big Corn / Little Corn, Caribbean archipelago accessible by plane, paradise turquoise beaches, reggae creole atmosphere, traditional alternative to traditional tourist Caribbean), Indio-Maíz Reserve (preserved jungle Costa Rican border), Solentiname (archipelago of islands in Lake Nicaragua, community of naive primitive Nicaraguan painters). Geological particularity: Nicaragua has 19 volcanoes of which 7 active, crossed by the "Pacific Cordillera" volcanic alignment. Safety: fragile political situation since 2018, potential demonstrations, but crime globally lower than in neighbouring Honduras. Try gallo pinto (rice+red beans, national dish shared with Costa Rica), nacatamales (giant Nicaraguan tamales), vigorón, quesillo (tortilla+cheese+onions), local Toña/Victoria beer, Flor de Caña Nicaraguan rum (one of the best in the world, several international awards). Managua Augusto César Sandino airport (MGA).
granada-nicaragua:en: Granada, colonial town of Nicaragua on the shores of Lake Nicaragua = the "colonial pearl" of the country: 130,000 inhabitants. One of the oldest Spanish colonial cities in America founded in 1524 by Francisco Hernández de Córdoba ("Nicaraguan Granada" historic rival of León = liberal-revolutionary vs Granada = conservative-aristocratic, bipolar rivalry that structured all of Nicaragua's political history 19th-20th century). Plundered several times by Caribbean pirates English-French-Dutch 17th century (the pirates went up the Río San Juan from the Caribbean Sea), burned 1856 by the American filibuster William Walker. Today preserved and smart town, unique Hispanic-colonial atmosphere. January-February-March are my favourite months (6.9/10): 22-32°C, 8-10% rain, this is the ideal dry season. Granada never tops 6.9/10 (hot humid tropical climate year-round). From November to April, it's the dry season: 22-34°C, this is the tourist high season. From May to October, it's the rainy season: 57-88% rainfall. Highlights: Granada Cathedral (yellow ochre 1583, one of the oldest cathedrals in Central America, multiple reconstructions after destructions 17th-19th century = signature visual of the central square), Parque Central / Plaza de la Independencia (main square, preserved Hispanic atmosphere), Iglesia La Merced (17th century, ascent of the bell tower panoramic 360° on the ochre roofs signature visual of Granada signature visual = viral Instagram photo), Calle La Calzada (main pedestrian street with restaurants-bars-boutique hotels, lively atmosphere in the evening), Iglesia de Xalteva, Iglesia San Francisco (1529, one of the oldest churches in continental America), Convento San Francisco (museum with pre-Columbian stone stelae from Lake Nicaragua = vestiges of the Chorotega-Nahuatl peoples), Casa de los Tres Mundos (17th-century palace transformed into cultural centre by poet Ernesto Cardenal), Cementerio de Granada (monumental 19th-century cemetery with tombs of Granadan elites), Lake Nicaragua / Cocibolca (the largest lake in Central America 8264 km², one of the only freshwater lakes in the world hosting freshwater bull sharks = endemic species evolutionarily adapted to fresh water = unique biological phenomenon = species that goes up the San Juan river towards the lake via Caribbean), Las Isletas (archipelago of 365 volcanic islets near Granada formed during the ancient eruption of Volcán Mombacho, accessible by boat tour from Granada, some islets are private residences of Nicaraguan fortunes), Volcán Mombacho (1344m, national park with altitude cloud forest, canopy + clouds signature hike). Essential Nicaragua excursions: Ometepe Island 3h by ferry from San Jorge (the largest volcanic island in the world in a freshwater lake, 2 "hourglass" volcanoes Concepción 1610m + Maderas 1394m, demanding Concepción hike but exceptional panorama), Volcán Masaya 30 min away (one of the only active volcanoes in the world where one can look into the incandescent lava lake crater at dusk = unique signature experience = access by car to the edge of the Santiago crater), León 2h30 away (the other rival old colonial capital, already covered by BDW), San Juan del Sur 2h away (south Pacific beach resort, popular surf, nightlife), Indio-Maíz Reserve (preserved jungle Costa Rican border accessible via Río San Juan river), Solentiname archipelago (school of naive primitive Nicaraguan painting founded by poet-priest Ernesto Cardenal). Try gallo pinto (rice red beans signature shared with Costa Rica), nacatamales (giant Nicaraguan tamales signature), vigorón (cabbage + yuca + pork), tres leches (signature dessert "3 milks" cake), local Toña/Victoria beer, Flor de Caña Nicaraguan rum (one of the best rums in the world, several international awards). Logistics: Granada 45 min from Managua airport (MGA) by taxi.
san-jose:en: San José, capital of Costa Rica on the central plateau at 1170m altitude: 340,000 inhabitants intra-muros, 2.5 million in urban area (50% of the total Costa Rican population in the "Gran Área Metropolitana"). Modern and dynamic capital but often seen as the transit stop to Costa Rican natural destinations = "one doesn't come to Costa Rica for San José". Yet offers rich cultural life, gastronomy, peaceful Latin atmosphere. February is my favourite month (9.0/10): 16-26°C, 6% rain, this is the ideal dry season. January-March stay excellent (8.6-8.7/10). From December to April, it's the dry season "verano": 13-26°C. From May to November, it's the green season: 73-89% rainfall, mild temperatures 15-25°C thanks to altitude. Permanent climate: San José never tops 27°C in maximum average and rarely descends below 13°C in minimum average, "Eternal Spring" tropical climate ideal thanks to altitude 1170m, one of the most pleasant climate capitals in the world. Highlights: Teatro Nacional (1897 neo-classical model Paris Opera, renowned café under the interior arcades, one of the most beautiful cultural institutions in Central America, financed by Costa Rican coffee barons 19th century), Plaza de la Cultura (central square with Museo del Oro Precolombino underground, unique pre-Columbian gold art collection), Mercado Central (1880, atmospheric traditional market with local "sodas" restaurant stands, spices, tropical flowers, exotic fruits), Museo Nacional de Costa Rica (in former Bellavista fortress, butterfly garden, pre-Columbian hall, mysterious Diquís stone spheres UNESCO), Museo de Arte Costarricense (in former airport, 20th-century Costa Rican art), Museo de los Niños (former prison reconverted into children's museum), Barrio Amón Quarter (historic quarter preserved 19th-20th century colonial and neo-classical architecture, bohemian atmosphere), Escalante Quarter ("San José foodie", concentration of the best cosmopolitan restaurants in the capital), Metropolitan Cathedral (1871 neo-classical), Iglesia La Merced and Iglesia La Soledad, Museo Calderón Guardia, Parque Metropolitano La Sabana (Costa Rican Central Park, 72 hectares with former airport reconverted). Day excursions from San José: Volcán Poás 1h away (one of the most accessible in Costa Rica, active crater accessible by car, sulphurous green lake, sometimes closed for activity), Volcán Irazú 1h30 away (the highest Costa Rican volcano 3432m, exceptional panorama in clear weather over the 2 oceans Atlantic-Pacific simultaneously), La Paz Waterfall Gardens 1h away (waterfalls + zoo), Cartago 30 min away (former colonial capital, Basilica of Our Lady of the Angels = Costa Rican pilgrimage place 2 August), Orosi Valley 1h30 away (old colonial church 1743, coffee plantations), Sarchí 1h away (craftsmanship of traditional painted carts, signature Costa Rican cultural UNESCO 2014). Try casado (traditional rice beans meat salad platter), gallo pinto (national breakfast), olla de carne, Costa Rican ceviche based on tilapia, local Tarrazú coffee (one of the best in the world), guaro (cane eau-de-vie), batidos (tropical fruit smoothies).
caracas:en: Caracas, capital of Venezuela: 2.5 million inhabitants at 900m altitude in a sunken valley of the Cordillera de la Costa. Venezuela is going through a major political, economic and social crisis since 2013 (hyperinflation, massive exodus of 7 million Venezuelans, contested Maduro government), Caracas is today considered one of the most dangerous cities in the world (homicide rate among the highest). Travel advisers: most Western countries formally advise against travel to Venezuela since 2017. February is my favourite month (8.5/10): 18-26°C, 27% rain, the driest period. From December to April, it's the dry season: 18-27°C, 27-58% rain, pleasant climate. From May to November, it's the rainy season: 49-84% rainfall, but stable temperatures (18-27°C, tropical altitude climate). Highlights (to know theoretically, caution required for any travel): Plaza Bolívar (colonial historic centre), Caracas Cathedral, Casa Natal de Bolívar (birthplace of "Libertador" Simón Bolívar 1783), El Ávila (Cerro El Ávila, national park 2765m, cable car from Caracas, panorama over the Caribbean coast), Sabana Grande Quarter (large commercial axis), Universidad Central de Venezuela (UNESCO, modern architecture Carlos Raúl Villanueva). Main Venezuela excursions: Salto Ángel in Canaima Park (the highest waterfall in the world 979m, accessible by plane + boat, 3-5 day expedition), Margarita Island (Venezuelan Caribbean), Roraima Tepui (the "Lost World" that inspired Conan Doyle), Los Roques (coral archipelago). Try arepa (the Venezuelan version, garnished), pabellón criollo (national dish: shredded meat + rice + black beans + plantain), cachapa, hallaca for Christmas, ron Diplomático (one of the best rums in the world).
asuncion:en: Asunción, capital of Paraguay: 525,000 inhabitants (2 million in urban area) on the east bank of the Río Paraguay facing Argentina. Founded in 1537 by the Spanish, one of the oldest capitals in South America ("Madre de Ciudades" = "Mother of Cities" as other cities like Buenos Aires were founded by expeditions departed from Asunción). Very little touristic capital of one of the least visited countries in South America (but also one of the cheapest, ideal for restricted budget). September is my favourite month (8.9/10): 17-28°C, 25% rain, this is the austral spring. From April to September, it's the austral winter: 14-29°C, 18-38% rain, mild bearable climate. From November to March, it's the hot austral summer: 21-33°C with 36-40% rain: extreme heat (sometimes 42°C+ with crushing humidity typical of the Paraguayan Chaco), reduced hotel prices. Highlights: Palacio de los López (presidential palace on Plaza Constitución, 19th-century European architecture built for President Francisco Solano López who was never able to live there — died in the disastrous Triple Alliance war 1864-1870 that destroyed Paraguay), Catedral Metropolitana (19th century), Panteón Nacional de los Héroes (mausoleum of national heroes including President Solano López and Carlos Antonio López), Casa de la Independencia (where Paraguay's independence was declared on 14 May 1811), Museo del Barro (the best museum of folk and indigenous art in Paraguay, magnificent collection of ñandutí textiles, masks and contemporary Guaraní art), Manzana de la Rivera (renovated quarter around the square with colonial houses), Mercado 4 (gigantic chaotic market, local atmosphere of non-tourist South America), Costanera (promenade along the Río Paraguay, urban river beaches). Excursions: Jesuit Mission of Trinidad and Jesús de Tavarangue 4h away (UNESCO, ruins of 17th-18th century Jesuit reductions, 30 ruins in total in southern Paraguay and northern Argentina The Mission film by Roland Joffé 1986), Paraguayan Pantanal in the north. Try chipa (cassava and cheese bread, the national bread), sopa paraguaya (paradoxically "the only solid soup in the world" as it is actually a salted corn bread), tereré (cold mate instead of hot like in Argentina/Uruguay, ideal for the Paraguayan heat), asado, chipa guazú.
bolivie:en: Bolivia, South American country landlocked between Peru-Brazil-Paraguay-Argentina-Chile: 12 million inhabitants, one of the most diverse countries in South America (Andes 6500m + Amazon + Atacama desert). La Paz is the administrative capital at 3640m (the highest capital in the world) and Sucre the constitutional capital. From August to November are my favourite months (7.5-7.7/10): 1-18°C at altitude, 7-42% rain, this is the ideal dry season (clear sky indispensable for Salar de Uyuni photos). From May to October, it's the tourist high season: -5/17°C at altitude, 7-31% rain, dry and cold climate. From December to March, it's the rainy season: 60-69% rainfall, but the Salar de Uyuni flooded becomes giant mirror (one of the most spectacular landscapes in the world, to see between January and April). Highlights: Salar de Uyuni (10,582 km², the largest salt desert in the world at 3656m altitude, 3-day 4x4 trip from Uyuni with night in salt hotel + Coloured Lagoon + pink flamingos + Green Lagoon + Sol de Mañana geysers 5000m), Lake Titicaca (3812m, shared with Peru, Sun Island legendary Inca origin, Copacabana sanctuary of the Virgin), La Paz (longest urban cable car in the world 30 km, El Alto at 4150m panoramic view of the Andes, Witches Market Mercado de las Brujas), Sucre (UNESCO, constitutional capital, white colonial city), Potosí (UNESCO, former largest city in the world 16th-17th century thanks to the Cerro Rico silver mines, still in operation today in very difficult conditions), Madidi National Park (one of the most biodiverse Amazonian parks in the world). Try salteñas (empanada turnover), api (hot purple corn drink), llajwa (spicy sauce), singani (eau-de-vie).
sucre:en: Sucre, constitutional capital of Bolivia (the administrative capital being La Paz, constitutional dualism unique in the world): 280,000 inhabitants at 2810m altitude. Listed UNESCO 1991 for its immaculate white colonial historic centre ("la Ciudad Blanca", urban regulation that imposes white whitewash on all houses since the 19th century, signature visual). More accessible and lower than La Paz (3650m), often recommended as the first stop in Bolivia to acclimatise to altitude. August is my favourite month (8.4/10): 6-21°C, 12% rain, this is the dry season, intense permanent blue sky. From June to October, it's the dry season: 6-22°C, 7-22% rain, stable temperatures thanks to altitude (summer in the Andes paradoxically = dry season, not hot). From December to March, it's the rainy season: 10-20°C, 49-77% rain, often overcast sky. Constant climate thanks to equatorial altitude: stable temperatures 6-22°C year-round, main variations on precipitation. Highlights: Plaza 25 de Mayo (main square, central pavilion, Government Palace of Chuquisaca), Casa de la Libertad (museum where Bolivian independence was declared on 6 August 1825, signed by Simón Bolívar), Catedral Metropolitana (mix of Renaissance, baroque and neoclassical 16th-19th century), Iglesia y Convento de San Felipe Neri (panoramic view from the roof), Museo de la Recoleta (17th-century Franciscan convent converted into museum, view from Recoleta square), Mercado Central (traditional market, fresh fruit juices, food stalls), Cementerio General (one of the most beautiful cemeteries in Latin America, luxurious mausoleums), Tarabuco 2h away on Sundays (Yampara ethnic market, one of the most colourful in South America). Excursions: Parque Cretácico 5 km away ("Cal Orck'o", the largest concentration of dinosaur tracks in the world, 12,000 tracks of 8 species over 1.5 km of vertical limestone wall revealed by erosion of a cement quarry), Potosí 3h away (UNESCO, former colonial silver centre, Cerro Rico mines poignant visit of miners in operation), Salar de Uyuni 8h away (the largest salt desert in the world, one of the signature Bolivian destinations, classic 3-day Uyuni-San Pedro de Atacama tour). Try salteñas (sweet-spicy meat turnover), api con pastel (hot purple corn drink + turnover), chuño (Andean dehydrated potato), llajwa (traditional spicy sauce).
paramaribo:en: Paramaribo, capital of Suriname (former Dutch Suriname, independent since 1975): 240,000 inhabitants on the Suriname river. UNESCO 1980 for its historic centre in wood with Dutch colonial 17th-19th century houses. Multi-ethnic country unique in the world: 27% Indo-Surinamese, 22% Maroons (descendants of escaped slaves), 18% Creoles, 14% Javanese, 5% Chinese, etc. Official language Dutch (only country on the continent), but Sranantongo Afro-Creole vehicular language. March is my favourite month (8.0/10): 24-28°C, 57% rain, end of the small dry season. From September to November, it's the great dry season: 25-32°C, 43-70% rain, ideal for visiting. From December to February and May-July, these are the rainy seasons: 67-97% rainfall, but stable temperatures (24-30°C). Paramaribo highlights: Historic centre (UNESCO, largest set of wooden colonial architecture in the Caribbean, to discover on foot along Waterkant Avenue), Fort Zeelandia (17th-century Dutch, history museum), Saints Peter and Paul Cathedral (the largest wooden cathedral in Latin America, 1885), Keizerstraat Mosque and Neveh Shalom Synagogue (cohabit side by side, multi-religious symbol of the country unique in the world), Centraal Market (Market Place, 6 distinct ethnic markets: Indian, Creole, Javanese, Chinese, Maroon). Excursions: Brownsberg Nature Park (Amazonian forest 130 km away, hiking, waterfalls), Galibi (sea turtle reserve at the mouth of the Marowijne), Amazon Forest (90% of Surinamese territory is virgin forest, one of the highest forest cover rates in the world, expeditions to the Saramaccan Maroons). Try pom (Creole cassolette), bami goreng (Javanese heritage), roti curry (Indian), pomtaja, Parbo Beer.
georgetown-guyana:en: Georgetown, capital of Guyana (formerly British Guiana, independent since 1966): 200,000 inhabitants on the Atlantic coast between Venezuela and Suriname. Small English-speaking country (1 million inhabitants in total), often confused with neighbouring French Guiana or Guyana = main ethnic origin 40% Indo-Guyanese (descendants of Indian workers who came after the abolition of slavery in 1838) + 30% Afro-Guyanese. March is my favourite month (7.9/10): 25-28°C, 63% rain, the relatively driest period. From September to November, it's the 2nd dry season: 25-30°C, 64-78% rain. From April to July, it's the major rainy season: 71-97% rainfall, possible flooding in Georgetown (the city is below sea level, protected by a Sea Wall dyke since the Dutch era 17th century). From December to February, expect 24-28°C with 73-85% rain: 2nd rainy season (minor). Highlights: Saint George Cathedral (the tallest wooden church in the world 43m, neo-Gothic 1899), Stabroek Market (Stabroek Market, 1881, cast iron architecture with clock, lively central market), Sea Wall (450 km dyke along the coast, necessary as Georgetown is below sea level), Promenade Gardens, Botanical Gardens, National Museum. Major Guyana excursions: Kaieteur Falls (the jewel of Guyana, 226m high waterfall — 5x Niagara — in virgin jungle, accessible by plane 1h from Georgetown or 5-day trek), Iwokrama Forest (Atlantic-Amazon protected forest, 30m canopy), Rupununi Savannah (savannah, ranches, jaguars), Shell Beach (sea turtles lay eggs). Try pepperpot (signature national stew, traditionally based on cassareep concentrated cassava liquid), roti, Guyanese curry (Indian heritage), El Dorado rum (one of the best rums in the world, 12 and 15 years).
guyane:en: French Guiana, French overseas department-region in South America between Brazil and Suriname: 300,000 inhabitants on 84,000 km² (the largest French department in surface area + 1 of the smallest populations). European border with Brazil = France has the longest European border with a South American country via French Guiana. 96% of the territory covered by Amazon jungle = France has the 2nd largest tropical forest in the world after Brazil. Capital Cayenne 60,000 inhabitants. Economy dependent on the Guiana Space Centre at Kourou (CSG, European Ariane launch base since 1968 + Vega + Soyuz, ideal thanks to proximity to the equator 5° latitude vs Cape Canaveral 28° = 17% boost in payload, 50% of European satellites launched from Kourou). August is my favourite month (6.9/10): 23-31°C, 38% rain, this is the ideal "little dry season". French Guiana never tops 6.9/10 (extremely humid equatorial climate year-round). From July to November, it's the relative dry season: 23-32°C, this is the relative tourist high season. From December to January, it's the "little rainy season": 64-67% rain. From March to June, it's the "great rainy season" equatorial monsoon: 75-87% rainfall, the greenest landscapes but roads sometimes flooded. Highlights: Guiana Space Centre (CSG) at Kourou (mandatory free guided tour by reservation, potential observation of an Ariane 5/6 or Vega launch = unique signature experience = a few launches per year, calendar published in advance), Salvation Islands 15 km off Kourou by boat ("Devil's Island" = former French penal colony 1852-1953 where Captain Alfred Dreyfus was deported 1895-1899 + Henri Charrière author of "Papillon" 1969 = major historical signature of French Guiana, overwhelming memorial atmosphere with preserved cell ruins, paradisiacal coconut palms contrasting with historical horror), Cayenne capital ("Place des Palmistes" + Cépérou Fort + traditional Central Market + Creole-Amazonian atmosphere), Saint-Laurent du Maroni (Suriname border, former convict transportation camp 1858-1953, "Camp de la Transportation" preserved as museum), Awala-Yalimapo (Atlantic beach on the Suriname border, leatherback sea turtle nesting = the largest turtle in the world 700 kg, March-August, one of the world sites for leatherback observation), Iracoubo (church painted by a Chinese convict Pierre Huguet 1893 = unique French Guianan artistic signature), Régina and Saül (preserved Amazonian jungle, ecotourism), Kaw Marshes (on the delta of the Approuague river, observation caimans + red ibises = signature bird of French Guiana + rare jaguars), Patawa Camp (Amazonian jungle camp). Activities: jungle expeditions on the Maroni river by traditional Boni dugout canoe (Creole bushinengue people descendants of escaped slaves), bird watching (700 species including 8 toucan species, scarlet macaw), wildlife safari (caimans, sloths, howler monkeys, rare jaguars). Population: unique ethnic mosaic = Creoles 35%, mainland French 12%, Brazilians 10%, Surinamese 6%, Haitians 6%, indigenous Amerindian peoples 5% (Wayampi-Wayana-Teko-Kali'na-Lokono), bushinengue Black Maroons 5%, Hmong refugees Laos 1975 1%, Chinese 1%. Try awara broth (French Guianan national dish based on bitter awara fruit signature), chicken colombo (Antillean Indian heritage), Antillean blood pudding, fish blaff, French Guianan chaudin, ti'punch (rum + sugar + lemon, the obligatory aperitif of the French DOM-TOM), French Guianan coffee. Cayenne-Félix Eboué airport (CAY).
belize:en: Belize (formerly British Honduras), small English-speaking country in Central America between Mexico and Guatemala on the Caribbean coast: 410,000 inhabitants. Only English-speaking country in Central America (British colonial heritage 1862-1981), former pre-Columbian Maya territory. Renowned worldwide for the Mesoamerican Barrier Reef (2nd largest coral reef in the world after the Australian, 300 km long off Belize) and the Great Blue Hole = signature blue hole of world diving. February is my favourite month (8.1/10): 20-28°C, 57% rain, this is the ideal dry season. January stays excellent (8.0/10). From December to April, it's the tourist high season: 20-32°C, sea at 26°C, hotel prices x2-x3. From June to October, it's the active cyclonic season: 79-84% rainfall, real hurricane risk (Belize on the Caribbean "Hurricane Alley", Hurricane Hattie 1961 = 300 deaths devastating). Highlights: Great Blue Hole (circular blue hole 300m diameter 124m deep off Ambergris Caye, karstic submerged formation at the end of the last glaciation, popularised by Jacques-Yves Cousteau who declared it "one of the 10 best diving sites in the world" in 1971, diving reserved for advanced divers due to depth 40m+, lunar décor with underwater stalactites + reef sharks, world signature aerial photo, boat-diving tour 250-350 USD/day from Ambergris Caye), Ambergris Caye (the largest island in Belize, main tourist destination, town San Pedro "La Isla Bonita" inspiration of Madonna's 1986 song, diving and snorkelling on the reef + Hol Chan Marine Reserve), Caye Caulker (the other famous island, calmer and bohemian, local motto "Go Slow", reggae-backpacker atmosphere), Tobacco Caye and South Water Caye (more remote islands), Belize Barrier Reef (UNESCO 1996, in danger 2009-2018 then saved by protection measures), Caracol (largest Maya archaeological site in Belize 9 km², classical 2nd-9th century, Caana pyramid 43m artificial highest point of Belize, difficult access in the jungle), Xunantunich ("the maiden of stone", more accessible Maya site with El Castillo pyramid 40m), Lamanai (Maya site in jungle accessible by boat), Altun Ha (Maya site), Cayo District (interior region mountain pine ridge, jungle, waterfalls, ATM Cave caves famous for Maya skeleton crystals = remains of human sacrifices preserved in the cave covered with crystalline calcite, unique world adventure experience), Hopkins and Placencia (Garifuna coastal villages, unique Afro-Caribbean culture). Garifuna = unique people in Belize-Honduras-Guatemala = descendants of shipwrecked Africans mixed with indigenous Caribs in the 17th century, Garifuna language UNESCO intangible heritage 2008. Diving: Belize is one of the top world destinations with Great Blue Hole + Mesoamerican Reef + Lighthouse Reef + Turneffe Atoll (one of the only coral atolls outside the Indian-Pacific Ocean). Activities: diving, snorkelling, kayaking, jungle trekking, ATM Cave (Actun Tunichil Muknal, one of the most extreme tourist adventures in the world = partially submerged Maya underwater cave to swim through, in the company of sacrificed skeletons), cave tubing. Try rice and beans (signature national dish with coconut milk), conch ceviche, fry jacks, cohune cabbage, hudut (Garifuna plantain mash), Belikin beer (national beer brewed locally), local One Barrel rum. Belize Goldson airport (BZE).
trinite-et-tobago:en: Trinidad and Tobago, micro-state of the Lesser Antilles in the south of the Caribbean 11 km off Venezuela: 1.4 million inhabitants on 2 main islands (Trinidad 4768 km² + Tobago 300 km²). Independent of the United Kingdom 1962, republic since 1976, Commonwealth member, official language English + Trinidadian English creoles + French. Cultural particularities = birthplace of the steel pan (steel drum, metal percussion instrument invented in Trinidad 1930-1940 by metallurgical workers using oil drums = the only musical instrument invented in the 20th century universally recognised) + calypso + soca + limbo = musical cradle of the Caribbean. Capital Port of Spain + Trinidad Carnival = one of the largest carnivals in the world (rivalling Rio + Notting Hill London). January-December are my favourite months (6.9/10): 23-31°C, 31-32% rain, this is the ideal dry season. Trinidad never tops 6.9/10 (hot humid tropical climate year-round). From January to May, it's the dry season: 23-32°C, this is the tourist high season (Carnival February-March + dry season). From June to November, it's the wet season: 50-78% rainfall, outside the main cyclonic zone (Trinidad-Tobago is south of the Hurricane Belt at 10°N latitude = rarely hit by hurricanes unlike northern Caribbean, one of the major advantages of the southern islands). Highlights: Port of Spain capital (Trinidad, urban Anglo-Creole Afro-Caribbean atmosphere, Queen's Park Savannah large central square, National Museum and Art Gallery, Royal Botanic Gardens 1820), Trinidad Carnival (world cultural signature, February-March depending on Easter date, 2 days of Mardi Gras + Ash Wednesday with masked costumed parades "masquerade" + steel pan competitions Panorama + soca music = unique world cultural signature experience, one of the 5 largest carnivals in the world with Rio + Salvador + Notting Hill + Cologne, hotel prices x4-x5 during), Maracas Bay (the most famous beach in Trinidad, golden sand, Atlantic waves, signature bake and shark sandwich to eat), Asa Wright Nature Centre (top world ornithological reserve in the Northern Range jungle, endemic birds jacamar + manakin + macaw), Caroni Bird Sanctuary (sanctuary of the Scarlet Ibis = scarlet ibis the national bird of Trinidad, twilight observation by boat when thousands of red ibises return to sleep at sunset = signature visual experience of the Caribbean), Pitch Lake at La Brea (the largest natural asphalt lake in the world 100 hectares, exploited since the British colonial era to tar roads worldwide, unique geological signature), Tobago neighbouring island (30 min by ferry or 25 min by plane, smaller and more touristic, Pigeon Point Beach ranked among the most beautiful beaches in the Caribbean, Buccoo Reef top regional snorkelling, Argyle Falls waterfall, Tobago Main Ridge Forest Reserve = the oldest protected forest reserve in the world 1776). Try doubles (flatbread with spicy chickpeas, signature national dish), bake and shark (grilled shark sandwich), pelau (Caribbean sauce chicken rice), roti (Indian heritage grand mass), curry chicken, callaloo, rum punch, Carib beer + Stag beer + Angostura rum (the famous Angostura bitters was invented in Trinidad 1875 by the German doctor Johann Siegert to treat Bolivar's soldiers = world signature of cocktails). Port of Spain Piarco airport (POS).
grenadines:en: Grenadines (Saint Vincent and the Grenadines), Caribbean micro-state in the south of the Lesser Antilles: 110,000 inhabitants on 32 islands of which 9 inhabited. The country is made up of the main island Saint Vincent + 32 small Grenadines islets (Bequia, Mustique, Canouan, Mayreau, Union Island, Tobago Cays, Palm Island, Petit Saint Vincent). Independence from the United Kingdom 1979, Commonwealth member. April is my favourite month (9.1/10): 24-29°C, 16% rain, sea at 27°C, ideal temperatures. January-March stay excellent (8.7-8.9/10). From January to May, it's the dry season: 23-30°C, this is the tourist high season (ultra-smart clientele), hotel prices x3-x4. From June to October, expect 24-31°C with 32-56% rain: active cyclonic season. From November to December, transition. Highlights / Main islands: Saint Vincent (main island, capital Kingstown, active volcano La Soufrière 1234m last devastating eruption April 2021, summit hike 6h round trip, Kingstown botanical gardens 1765 = the oldest in the Caribbean), Bequia 1h by ferry from Kingstown ("Pearl of the Grenadines", picturesque port with yachts, Princess Margaret Beach, relaxed atmosphere), Mustique (most exclusive private island in the Grenadines, only 100 villas, very limited access, celebrities Mick Jagger/Princess Margaret/Bryan Adams have houses, Cotton House 5*+ unique hotel, prohibitive prices), Canouan (smart island with marina), Mayreau (very small island, 200 inhabitants, Salt Whistle Bay beach = one of the most beautiful Caribbean beaches), Union Island (diving stopover), Tobago Cays (national maritime park, archipelago of deserted islets in a turquoise lagoon = the signature visual of the Grenadines, cult Caribbean yacht mooring, snorkelling with green turtles, accessible only by boat), Palm Island (private island 18 hectares, 5*+ unique hotel-resort), Petit Saint Vincent ("PSV", another even more exclusive private island, 22 scattered cottages). Activities: sailing = the n°1 attraction of the Grenadines, one of the world sailing paradises (sailboat rental with or without skipper, week 2000-10000€ depending on category, classic route Saint Vincent-Bequia-Mustique-Canouan-Mayreau-Tobago Cays-Union-PSV), diving sites Devil's Table, Anchor Reef, Mayreau Gardens, kitesurfing (Union Island = kite capital of the Grenadines), traditional sailing (Bequia preserves the tradition of sailing whaling according to IWC International Whaling Commission quotas, controversial but legal), Soufrière Volcano hike. Filming locations: Pirates of the Caribbean (Wallilabou Bay on Saint Vincent = village reconstituted as Port Royal for the film). Public: ultra-wealthy on yacht, European-American sailors, few traditional tourists, discreet VIP atmosphere. Try roti (Indian-Trinidadian heritage), callaloo soup, conch fritters, mountain chicken (actually endemic giant frog), local Sunset Captain Bligh rum, breadfruit. Logistics: Argyle International Saint Vincent airport (SVD) opened 2017, direct charter flights Miami, Toronto, London, several inter-island airports (Mustique MQS, Canouan CIW, Union UNI, Bequia BQU).
saint-vincent:en: Saint Vincent, main island of the independent state Saint Vincent and the Grenadines (Commonwealth member): 100,000 inhabitants (110,000 in the entire country including the Grenadines), active volcanic island (La Soufrière 1234m erupted in April 2021 with evacuation of 20,000 people, last major eruption 1979). Capital Kingstown. More traditional and less touristic than neighbouring Saint Lucia or Barbados. April is my favourite month (8.3/10): 25-28°C, 36% rain, end of the dry season. From December to April, it's the tourist high season: 24-28°C, 36-44% rain, sea at 26°C, constant trade winds. From June to November, it's the cyclonic season: 61-72% rainfall, hurricane risk (Saint Vincent is on the classic cyclone trajectory). Saint Vincent highlights: La Soufrière (1234m, 6h round trip hike, still active volcano, last eruption April 2021, sometimes closed access for seismic activity), Dark View Falls and Trinity Falls, Botanical Gardens of Kingstown (the oldest botanical garden in the Americas, founded 1765, the original breadfruit tree brought by Captain Bligh "Mutiny on the Bounty" 1793 is still alive), Fort Charlotte (18th-century British, panorama over Kingstown), Owia Salt Pond (natural marine pool Atlantic side). But mainly the Grenadines = the major attraction: 32 islands and islets in the south, Caribbean paradise for sailing: Bequia (the largest of the Grenadines, 5000 inhabitants, local atmosphere, Princess Margaret Beach), Mustique (private luxury island, Mick Jagger and Bryan Adams have their villas there, legendary Basil's Bar pub), Mayreau (the smallest of the Grenadines, paradisiacal Salt Whistle Bay beach), Tobago Cays (5 uninhabited islets in the heart of a protected coral reef, turquoise water, snorkelling with green turtles, signature postcard Caribbean photo). Pirates of the Caribbean: filming in 2003 and 2006 (Wallilabou Bay = beach from the film). Try arrowroot (Saint Vincent = world leader), callaloo soup, breadfruit, Sunset Very Strong rum (84.5% the strongest in the world, sold only to residents). Logistics: Saint Vincent 1h30 by plane from Barbados (often stopover), Argyle international airport opened 2017.
dominique:en: Dominica is the "nature island" of the Caribbean: 750 km², 70,000 inhabitants, mountainous (Morne Diablotin 1447m), volcanic, 365 rivers, primary tropical forests. Not to be confused with the Dominican Republic. May is my favourite month (8.6/10): 23-28°C, 41% rain, sea at 27°C. From June to November, it's the cyclonic season: 59-74% rainfall, real hurricane risk (Maria 2017 devastated the island at 90%). From January to April, expect 21-27°C: tourist high season. Highlights: Morne Trois Pitons National Park (UNESCO, volcanic landscapes), Boiling Lake (boiling water lake, 2nd largest in the world, 12h round trip hike, demanding), Trafalgar Falls (twin waterfalls), Emerald Pool (emerald basin in the forest), Champagne Reef (volcanic underwater bubbles, snorkelling), Sari Sari Falls, Middleham Falls. Roseau = capital, market. Carib Territory in the east (reserve of the last Kalinago descendants, prehispanic Amerindian people). Whale watching (sperm whales year-round, one of the rare spots in the world with permanent presence). No mass tourism: no large all-inclusive, eco-lodge hotels and guesthouses. Try mountain chicken (actually giant frog, now protected species), callaloo, sancocho, local Macoucherie rum. Flight via Antigua, Barbados or Saint Lucia + small plane 30 min.
bonaire:en: Bonaire is the smallest and calmest of the ABC islands: 290 km², 20,000 inhabitants, declared "Marine Park" in 1979 (one of the first in the world). Outside the cyclonic zone. March is my favourite month (8.6/10): 25-28°C, 39% rain, sea at 26°C. From September to December, expect 26-31°C with 31-72% rainfall. From January to August, stable dry climate. Year-round is workable as outside hurricane zone. Bonaire is above all a diving destination: 86 sites accessible directly from the beach (drive-in diving with roadside markers), full marine reserve, exceptional biodiversity (more than 350 species of fish), among the best spots in the world for "shore diving" (without boat). Highlights: Washington-Slagbaai National Park (40% of the island in the north, desert landscapes, highest point Brandaris 240m, 4×4 necessary), Kralendijk (colourful capital, village size), Lac Bay (protected lagoon, kitesurf and windsurf, constant winds), Pekelmeer Sanctuary (pink flamingos, largest nesting site in the southern Caribbean), Salt pans (coloured salt marshes pink/blue/white), Klein Bonaire (uninhabited islet accessible by water taxi). Try goat stew, fish soup. Dutch + Papiamento + English. Much less touristic than Aruba or Curaçao, dive resort atmosphere. Direct flight from Amsterdam or via Curaçao.
saint-thomas:en: Saint Thomas, main island of the US Virgin Islands (USVI) in the Lesser Antilles: 50,000 inhabitants on 81 km². Unincorporated US territory since 1917 (bought from Denmark for 25 million USD to prevent their seizure by Germany during the First World War), American citizens with US passports but without presidential voting rights. Capital Charlotte Amalie = former Danish capital, Caribbean atmosphere mixed with preserved Danish heritage. Main Caribbean cruise destination (15+ ships/day in high season). March is my favourite month (8.4/10): 24-26°C, 25% rain, this is the ideal dry season. January-April stay excellent (8.2-8.4/10). From December to April, it's the tourist high season: 24-27°C, sea at 26-27°C, hotel prices x2-x3. From June to November, it's the active cyclonic season: 35-56% rainfall, real hurricane risk (Saint Thomas and Saint Croix devastated by Hurricane Irma + Hurricane Maria September 2017 2 weeks apart = historic double catastrophe, multi-year reconstruction).
