Highlights: Magens Bay (regularly ranked among the most beautiful beaches in the world by National Geographic, regular semi-circle bay with powdery white sand and turquoise water, paying 5 USD/person but equipped), Charlotte Amalie (capital, 19th-century preserved Danish old town with Danish colonial architecture unique in the Caribbean, port-marina with cruise ships, luxury duty-free boutiques), Fort Christian (1672, the oldest building in the Virgin Islands, former Danish red-brick fort signature), Mountain Top (722m, highest point, 360° panorama over Saint Thomas + Saint John + British Virgin Islands BVI to the east, signature visual with banana daiquiri "invented here in 1958" according to local tradition), 99 Steps (18th-century Danish historic staircase, actually 103 steps despite the name), Blackbeard's Castle (17th-century Danish tower, legend of the pirate Edward Teach "Blackbeard" who allegedly observed ships from the tower, in reality doubtful but effective marketing), Magens Bay Arboretum, Coki Beach (family snorkelling), St Thomas Skyride (panoramic cable car). Essential excursions: Saint John 30 min by ferry (the other USVI island, 60% of the island classified as Virgin Islands National Park = US federal national park, preserved beaches Trunk Bay top world according to Condé Nast, snorkelling Underwater Trail), Saint Croix 30 min by plane (the largest of the USVI, more traditional and less cruise atmosphere), British Virgin Islands (BVI, Tortola/Virgin Gorda, ferries from Saint Thomas 30 min, more peaceful and less tourist atmosphere, top world for sailing), Anegada BVI (flat coral island with turquoise lagoon, signature lobsters), Jost Van Dyke (bohemian atmosphere, legendary Soggy Dollar Bar with signature painkiller cocktail BVI). Try conch fritters, callaloo, johnnycakes, fungi (Caribbean polenta), salt fish, painkiller cocktail (rum-coconut-orange-nutmeg signature BVI), Cruzan rum (local Saint Croix rum), Pusser's Rum BVI. Logistics: Cyril E. King Saint Thomas airport (STT), direct flights from Miami/New York/Atlanta, accessible to American citizens without passport (US territory), passport required for foreigners.
basseterre:en: Basseterre, capital of Saint Kitts and Nevis in the Lesser Antilles: 13,000 inhabitants. Smallest independent country in the Americas = 261 km², 53,000 inhabitants in total, independent of the United Kingdom 1983, Commonwealth member, parliamentary constitutional monarchy. First English colony in the Caribbean 1623 = "Mother Colony" of the British West Indies. Bilingual country (English creole + standard English official). March-April are my favourite months (8.3/10): 24-27°C, 42-44% rain, this is the ideal dry season. May-June stay excellent (8.2/10). From December to April, it's the tourist high season: 24-29°C, sea at 26-27°C, hotel prices x2-x3. From June to November, it's the active cyclonic season: 48-64% rainfall, moderate hurricane risk. Highlights: Brimstone Hill Fortress (UNESCO 1999, "Gibraltar of the West Indies", British 17th-18th century fortress perched at 240m altitude on Mount Brimstone, one of the best-preserved military forts in the Caribbean, panoramic view over Saint Eustatius + Dutch Saba + Nevis to the south, defended Saint Kitts against the French = the largest English fortification in the 18th-century Caribbean, taken by French Comte de Grasse 1782 then returned to England by Treaty of Paris 1783), Independence Square in Basseterre (main square, former 17th-19th century slave market, renamed 1983 at independence), Cathedral of the Immaculate Conception, National Museum at the former Treasury Building, Romney Manor (17th-century colonial manor transformed into Caribelle Batik batik workshop signature local, preserved plantation atmosphere), Wingfield Estate (17th-century sugar cane plantation ruins, Caribbean slavery memorial atmosphere), Black Rocks (black volcanic formations on the Atlantic side seafront), St Kitts Scenic Railway (former 1912 sugar cane train reconverted into 30 km panoramic tourist train around the island, unique signature Caribbean tourist), Mount Liamuiga (dormant volcano 1156m, highest point, demanding 4-5h round trip hike to the crater). Nevis (neighbouring frigate-half island, accessible 30 min ferry from Saint Kitts, calmer atmosphere with preserved British colonial architecture, Mount Nevis volcano 985m, top regional Pinney's Beach). Activities: diving and snorkelling (sites in the south of Saint Kitts), catamaran sailing between Saint Kitts and Nevis, Brimstone Hill tour, Caribelle Batik workshops, Marriott resort casino. Public: East Coast Americans in the majority (charter flights from New York-Miami), Canadian snowbirds, British. Citizenship by Investment: Saint Kitts has the oldest "Citizenship by Investment" programme in the world (since 1984) = obtaining Saint Kittian passport against 150,000 USD real estate investment or donation to the National Development Fund, attracts emerging fortunes from Russia/China/Middle East seeking 2nd passport, international controversy. Try goat water (signature spicy goat stew, national dish unique in the Caribbean), salt fish + johnnycakes, conch chowder, Caribbean ratatouille, fried plantain, Carib beer, Brinley Gold Shipwreck Rum (local rum). Robert L. Bradshaw Saint Kitts airport (SKB).
riyad:en: Riyadh (Ar-Riyad), capital of Saudi Arabia on the central plateau of Najd at 600m altitude: 7 million inhabitants intra-muros, 9 million in urban area = the largest city in the Arab world by surface area. Historically ultra-conservative capital, massive tourist opening since 2019 (e-visa, lifting of driving ban for women 2018, opening of cinemas-concerts-international events under Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman MBS's Vision 2030). March is my favourite month (8.5/10): 16-28°C, 9% rain, this is the ideal dry season. November stays excellent (8.5/10). From November to March, it's the tourist high season: 9-28°C, dry climate and cool in the evening (sometimes 5°C in January night). From May to September drop to 3.1/10 due to extreme heatwave (26-44°C, sometimes 50°C+, arid desert climate). Highlights: Kingdom Centre (Burj Al Mamlaka, signature 302m skyscraper of Riyadh opened 2002, distinctive architecture with arch at the top, Skybridge accessible to visitors with 360° panorama over Riyadh at sunset = signature experience), Al Faisaliyah Tower (other signature skyscraper 267m with sphere at the top housing restaurant), Diriyah (UNESCO 2010, former historic capital of Saudi Arabia, cradle of the Al Saud dynasty 15th-19th century, At-Turaif District = mud brick quarter preserved in majestic restoration project "Diriyah Gate" 50 billion USD to make the "Arab Versailles"), Masmak Fort (historic fortress 1865 where Abdulaziz reconquered Riyadh 1902 founding the modern Saudi kingdom, museum), Souk Al Zal (traditional central souk with antiques, carpets, oud perfumes), Edge of the World 1h30 from Riyadh ("Jebel Fihrayn", spectacular 300m high cliffs in the desert, panorama over the infinite desert horizon = signature visual of Saudi excursions), National Museum of Saudi Arabia (national museum, Islamic pre-Islamic art-archaeology), Red Sand Dunes (red dunes accessible 1h from Riyadh, 4x4 dune-bashing), Riyadh Season (annual winter festival October-March since 2019, international concerts Justin Bieber/Mariah Carey/Eminem played = major Saudi cultural opening, "sportwashing" and "artwashing" controversy). Saudi Arabia excursions: Al-Ula 1h flight (see dedicated review), Mecca 1h flight (forbidden to non-Muslims), Medina 1h flight (forbidden to non-Muslims). Safety: Riyadh globally safe, near non-existent crime thanks to strict sharia (very severe penalties), but travel subject to conservative dress code, alcohol strictly forbidden (prison sentences + flogging for foreigners), homosexuality forbidden (death penalty in theory). Try kabsa (Saudi spicy rice, national dish, Bedouin signature), mandi (rice with lamb cooked in earth-buried oven), harees, jareesh, mutabbaq, Arabic coffee with Medjool dates, Ajwa dates (the most prestigious date in the Arab world, IGP). King Khalid Riyadh airport (RUH).
djeddah:en: Jeddah, 2nd city of Saudi Arabia on the Red Sea: 4.7 million inhabitants. Gateway to Mecca = main entrance for pilgrims of the annual Muslim Hajj (3 million pilgrims/year, one of the largest religious concentrations in the world). More liberal and cosmopolitan atmosphere than Riyadh thanks to its history of international commercial port for 2000 years + mixing of pilgrims from all nationalities. January is my favourite month (9.0/10): 20-28°C, 9% rain, this is the ideal dry season. February stays excellent (8.8/10). From November to March, it's the tourist high season: 20-33°C, more temperate climate than in Riyadh thanks to the Red Sea breeze. From May to October drop to 3.8-6.8/10 due to the heatwave combined with oppressive coastal humidity (26-38°C, sometimes 45°C+ with 80% humidity). Highlights: Al-Balad (UNESCO 2014, historic old town of Jeddah, one of the last preserved examples of Hijaz architecture = traditional Arab houses in coral-wood with roshan = characteristic openwork wooden balconies allowing women to look outside without being seen, Oriental atmosphere unique in the Arabian peninsula), Al-Balad evening market (spices, incense, oud perfumes, 22-carat gold jewellery, traditional atmosphere), Jeddah Corniche (40 km of seaside Red Sea promenade, one of the longest in the world, signature of the modern city, monumental open-air sculptures including the famous giant "Bicycle"), King Fahd's Fountain (the highest fountain in the world 312m, water jet visible from all of Jeddah, signature visual at night when illuminated), Floating Mosque (Masjid Ar-Rahmah, "Floating Mosque" on stilts directly on the Red Sea, open to non-Muslim visitors), King Abdullah Sports City, Red Sea Mall (one of the largest shopping centres in the Middle East), Naseef House (19th-century historic house transformed into museum, traditional Hijaz atmosphere), Tayebat Museum (private museum of Islamic art). Activities: Red Sea diving = major asset of Jeddah (the Red Sea is one of the top world destinations with crystal waters + coral reefs + exceptional marine biodiversity, PADI training possible, major sites Abu Galawa, Shaab Abu Nuhas), Durrat Al Arus beach (smart beach resort), Salman Bay beach. Excursions: Mecca 80 km away (forbidden to non-Muslims, but Jeddah is the arrival base for pilgrims), Medina 4h by car (forbidden to non-Muslims), Taif 1h30 away (mountain station 1700m, temperate climate, historic royal summer capital, famous for its perfumed Damascena roses). Try kabsa, saleeg (signature Hijaz creamy rice), mutabbaq (turnover stuffed with meat/egg), shawarma, foul medames (Egyptian fava beans popular in the Hijaz), mango juice, Arabic coffee. King Abdulaziz Jeddah airport (JED), 2nd busiest in the Middle East after Dubai thanks to Hajj pilgrims.
teheran:en: Tehran, capital of Iran: 9 million inhabitants (15 million in urban area) at 1200m altitude at the foot of the Alborz Mountains (Damavand summit 5610m, highest volcano in Asia). Capital since 1796 under the Qajar dynasty. Modern megacity with striking contradictions: theocratic conservative regime since 1979 (Islamic Revolution) facing an educated urban youth (60% of the population under 35, regular protests). Limited tourism since international sanctions (accessible visa but Western feeling of going to "forbidden" country). May is my favourite month (9.2/10): 17-30°C, 13% rain, ideal temperatures before the heatwave. From April-May and September-October, these are the best seasons: 12-32°C, 1-22% rain. From June to August, it's the extreme heatwave: 23-38°C (sometimes 42°C), suffocating atmospheric pollution (Tehran is one of the most polluted cities in the world, persistent smog also in winter). From December to February, expect 0-11°C and 18-26% rain: dry cold, snow possible, skiing on the neighbouring heights at Dizin and Shemshak (50 min from Tehran, 3600m altitude, among the best ski resorts in the Middle East). Highlights: Golestan Palace (UNESCO 2013, former 19th-century Qajar residence, 17 monuments in a Persian garden, signature mosaics), Saadabad Palace (in the north on the heights, former summer residence of the shah), Niavaran Palace (last residence of Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi before exile 1979), National Jewels Treasury (under the Central Bank, one of the most impressive royal jewellery collections in the world: peacock throne in gold 26000 precious stones, Darya-ye Noor diamond 182 carats one of the largest pink diamonds in the world), Tehran Bazaar (the largest covered bazaar in the world 10 km of alleys), National Archaeological Museum (ancient Persian and Achaemenid collections), Azadi Tower ("Liberty", 1971 commemorating 2500 years of Persian empire), Milad Tower (435m, 6th tallest tower in the world 2008), Tajrish Quarter in the north (smart and modern, restaurants and cafés). Excursions: Mount Damavand (5610m, 3-day ascent), Caspian 3h away, Qom Shia holy city 2h away, Isfahan 5h by bus (UNESCO, see dedicated review). Try chelo kabab (saffron rice + skewers), ghormeh sabzi, fessenjan (chicken in walnut-pomegranate sauce), sangak (bread), Persian tea tchaï, doogh (salted sparkling yoghurt).
ispahan:en: Isfahan (Esfahan), historic city of Iran: 2.2 million inhabitants at 1590m altitude in the heart of the Iranian plateau. Capital of the Safavid empire under Shah Abbas I (1598-1629), artistic and architectural peak of Persian civilisation. The Persian proverb says "Isfahan half of the world" ("Esfahan, nesf-e jahan") so much was it considered spectacular in the 17th century. May is my favourite month (9.4/10): 15-30°C, 7% rain, ideal temperatures before the heatwave. From April-May and September-October, these are the best seasons: 4-33°C, 0-18% rain, ideal dry climate. From June to August, it's the heatwave: 20-38°C (July 38°C+), avoid July (3.8/10). From December to February, expect -3/14°C and 11-15% rain: mild day, cool night, snow possible. Highlights: Naghsh-e Jahan Square ("Image of the World", UNESCO 1979, one of the largest squares in the world 89,000 m² 510×165m, designed by Shah Abbas I 1598-1629, 4 major monuments on its sides — signature photo of trips to Iran), Shah Mosque / Imam Mosque (south side of the square, 1611-1638, considered masterpiece of world Islamic architecture, turquoise blue dome 54m high, 7-colour mosaics unique in the world, striking echo under the central dome), Sheikh Lotfollah Mosque (east side, 1602-1619, private mosque of the women of the royal harem, no minaret or courtyard, sand-pink dome colour changing at sunset), Ali Qapu Palace (west side, 1597, terrace-balcony where the shah received foreign ambassadors), Qeysarieh Bazaar (north side), Jameh Mosque / Friday Mosque (UNESCO 2012, 2 km away, covers 1300 years of Islamic architectural evolution from 8th to 18th century, one of the oldest mosques in Iran), Khaju Bridge (1650, 24 arches over the Zayandeh river, unique atmosphere at sunset with traditional songs in under-arch), Si-o-se Pol Bridge ("33 arches", 1602, 298m, longest bridge of its kind in the world), Vank Cathedral (in Jolfa Armenian quarter, 1655, Apostolic Armenian cathedral with biblical frescoes unique mixing Persian and European influences). Try beryani esfahani (local dish, shredded lamb and flatbread), faloodeh (sorbet), gaz (Persian nougat, Isfahan signature), sohan (saffron-pistachio caramel), Persian tea tchaï.
damas:en: Damascus (Dimashq), capital of Syria: 1.7 million inhabitants intra-muros, 2.7 million in urban area (before the civil war). One of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in the world = 11,000 years of continuous human occupation, mentioned in Egyptian texts 2500 BC, capital of the Umayyad Caliphate 661-750 = capital of the most extensive Islamic empire in history extending from Portugal to the Indus. May is my favourite month (9.4/10): 13-30°C, 8% rain, this is the ideal spring. April-October stay excellent (8.4-8.7/10). From April to June and September-October, these are the best tourist seasons: 9-31°C, ideal Mediterranean-desert climate. From July to August drop to 3.9/10 due to the heatwave (20-37°C, sometimes 45°C+). From November to March, expect 1-17°C and 9-21% rain: mild but cool nights, rare snow possible on the neighbouring mountains. Highlights: Old town of Damascus (UNESCO 1979, one of the oldest urban cities in the world, preserved millennial Oriental atmosphere, labyrinthine alleys, Ottoman palaces, hammams), Umayyad Mosque (Al-Jami' al-Umawi, 706-715 AD, one of the oldest mosques in the world and one of the holiest after those of Mecca-Medina-Jerusalem, houses the head of Saint John the Baptist according to tradition + Saladin's tomb in the outer garden, architectural signature visual of classical Islam with its preserved Byzantine mosaics), Saladin's Tomb (Salah ad-Din, 1193, Muslim hero who took back Jerusalem from the Crusaders 1187), Citadel of Damascus (medieval 13th-century Ayyubid), Souk Al-Hamidiyah ("Hamidiyah Souk", 19th-century traditional covered market, one of the largest souks in the Arab world, 600m long with metal sheet roofing signature, Ottoman heritage of Sultan Abdülhamid II), Maktab Anbar House (traditional Damascene notable's palace, preserved Oriental atmosphere), Azem Palace (1750, Ottoman governor Asad Pasha al-Azm's palace, become folk arts museum), Straight Street ("Via Recta", street mentioned in the biblical Acts of the Apostles where Saint Paul converted after his vision on the road to Damascus, still present in the old town), Bab Touma Christian Quarter ("Saint Thomas Gate", one of the oldest Christian quarters in the world, preserved picturesque atmosphere). Safety: Syria coming out of civil war 2011-2024 = the fall of the Assad regime in December 2024 by the HTS forces of Ahmed al-Sharaa (Abu Mohammed al-Joulani) ended 13 years of bloody civil war (500,000+ deaths), new Sunni Islamist transitional government in place, political situation still very unstable, inadvisable France Diplomacy for the entirety of the country, tourist infrastructure destroyed or abandoned, very inadvisable travel except rare humanitarian-journalistic missions. Try Syrian mezze (world culinary signature of the Levant), Damascus kebab, fattoush, tabbouleh, kibbeh, Syrian baklava, Arabic coffee with cardamom, mint tea. Damascus International airport (DAM).
bandar-seri-begawan:en: Bandar Seri Begawan ("BSB"), capital of Brunei (Brunei Darussalam) on the island of Borneo: 100,000 inhabitants intra-muros, 280,000 in urban area. Absolute sultanate ruled by Sultan Hassanal Bolkiah since 1967 (one of the longest reigns in the world, 56 years in 2023, 5th world fortune according to Forbes 28 billion USD personal). Ultra-rich country thanks to oil and gas, one of the strictest countries in the world religiously (full sharia since 2014 = stoning for adultery, death penalty for homosexuality since 2019 = international boycott of Sultan hotels including Beverly Hills Hotel + The Dorchester London). February is my favourite month (7.6/10): 24-29°C, 68% rain, this is the relative dry season. BSB never tops 7.6/10 (very humid equatorial climate year-round). From January to March, it's the relative dry season: 24-29°C. From September to November, it's the most humid season: 88-94% rainfall. Extremely stable climate: BSB never tops 30°C in maximum average and rarely descends below 24°C in minimum average, annual range among the smallest in the world. Highlights: Sultan Omar Ali Saifuddin Mosque (1958, 24-carat gold dome signature visual of BSB, one of the most beautiful mosques in South East Asia, open to non-Muslims outside prayers), Jame'Asr Hassanil Bolkiah Mosque (1992, largest mosque in Brunei with 29 domes in tribute to the 29 Sultans of the dynasty, capacity 5000 worshippers), Istana Nurul Iman (Sultan's Palace, the largest residential palace in the world according to Guinness Book = 1788 rooms + 257 bathrooms + 110 garages = for the Sultan's private collection estimated at 7000 cars including 600 Rolls-Royce, open to the public only 3 days/year for Hari Raya end of Ramadan), Royal Regalia Museum (museum of royal regalia, state diplomatic gifts received by the Sultan, golden royal palanquin for his wedding), Kampong Ayer ("Water Village", stilt quarter on the Brunei river housing 13,000 inhabitants for 1300 years, one of the largest aquatic-villages in the world still inhabited, nicknamed "Venice of the Orient" by Magellan in 1521 during his passage, accessible by traditional water taxi), Tasek Lama Recreational Park (forest park in the city centre with proboscis monkeys endemic to Borneo). Essential excursions = this is why one comes to Brunei: Ulu Temburong National Park 1h by boat + 4x4 (Borneo primary jungle park, one of the last intact ecosystems in South East Asia, accessible only by guided expeditions, Canopy Walkway = canopy walkways at 60m high signature, observation orangutans-proboscis monkeys-hornbills-cobras-leeches, exotic signature experience). Safety: Brunei very safe criminally, but very strict Islamic laws applied even to foreigners (private alcohol forbidden, conservative dress code, public shows of affection forbidden). No alcohol in Brunei (total ban, possibility of bringing 2 litres for private consumption on arrival for non-Muslims). Try ambuyat ("national dish" = sago porridge with spicy fermented durian tempoyak sauce + cacapan, signature unique Bruneian culinary experience), nasi katok (rice with curry + fried chicken, popular snack), kueh (Malay cakes), local coffee. Brunei International airport (BWN).
dili:en: Dili, capital of East Timor (Timor-Leste) on the north coast of the island of Timor in South East Asia: 280,000 inhabitants. Youngest country in Asia = independence on 20 May 2002 after 24 years of bloody Indonesian occupation 1975-1999 (200,000 deaths or 1/3 of the Timorese population by massacres-famine-violence) then UN administration 1999-2002. Catholic country at 96% (heritage of Portuguese colonisation 1515-1975) = only Catholic country in Asia with the Philippines, official languages Tetum + Portuguese. August is my favourite month (8.9/10): 21-31°C, 4% rain, this is the ideal dry season. June-July-September stay excellent (8.7-8.9/10). From May to November, it's the dry season "bailoro": 19-32°C, sea at 27°C, dry and cool tropical climate thanks to south-east trade winds, this is the relative tourist high season. From December to April, it's the wet season "roromata": 33-72% rainfall, but stable temperatures 24-32°C. Dili highlights: Cristo Rei de Dili (Christ-King of Dili, 27m high Christ statue on panoramic hill 80m overlooking Dili Bay = signature visual, copy inspired by Christ the Redeemer Rio de Janeiro but offered by Indonesia 1996 during the occupation as a controversial "reconciliation" symbol, accessible by signature 580 steps, exceptional panorama over Dili + north coast), Cathedral of the Immaculate Conception (1989 opened by John Paul II during historic 1989 visit = encouragement to independence in the context of Indonesian occupation), Tais Market ("tais" = traditional Timorese textile signature cultural, hand-woven fabric with geometric patterns unique to each village, UNESCO intangible heritage classification 2021), Resistance Museum (museum of Timorese resistance against Indonesian occupation 1975-1999, overwhelming memorial atmosphere, photos of massacre victims), Santa Cruz Cemetery ("Santa Cruz cemetery" site of the Santa Cruz massacre of 12 November 1991 = 250+ young Timorese massacred by the Indonesian army during the funeral of independence activist, filmed by British journalist Max Stahl = video that alerted the whole world to the Timorese genocide and triggered international solidarity = major memorial place), Statue of Nicolau Lobato (independence hero assassinated 1978), Areia Branca Beach ("white beach" urban beach of Dili with golden sand, peaceful local atmosphere). Timor excursions: Atauro Island 25 min by boat from Dili ("most biodiverse island in the world" according to Conservation International 2016 with 643 fish species discovered at the foot of the beaches = top world for diving and snorkelling, traditional insular atmosphere), Maubisse 3h away in the central mountains 1400m (cool temperate climate, Timor-Leste coffee = one of the most prestigious Arabica coffees in Asia, plantations to visit), Mount Ramelau (highest point 2986m, 4h round trip hike for sunrise at the summit = signature Timorese experience), Baucau 2nd city (preserved Portuguese colonial atmosphere), Jaco Island (extreme east, sacred uninhabited island by Tetum tradition, paradise beach accessible 30 min by boat from Tutuala). Activities: diving and snorkelling (Atauro top world), coffee tour (Timor-Leste = one of the world's coffees sought by chef roasters), Mount Ramelau hiking, humpback whale watching (October-December, migration route). Try ikan saboko (signature grilled fish in banana leaf), batar daan (corn cooked with beans), Timorese feijoada (Portuguese heritage), Timor-Leste coffee, imported Portuguese vinho ("tradition"), local Tiger Beer. Dili Presidente Nicolau Lobato airport (DIL), flights from Bali Denpasar (1h30) + Darwin Australia (1h) + Singapore (3h30).
kaohsiung:en: Kaohsiung, 2nd city of Taiwan on the south-west coast: 2.8 million inhabitants (3rd in urban area after Taipei + New Taipei). Main port of Taiwan = 4th largest container port in the world, economic capital of southern Taiwan. More peaceful and tropical atmosphere than northern Taipei, hot tropical climate (different from the humid subtropical of Taipei). March is my favourite month (8.9/10): 19-26°C, 19% rain, this is the ideal dry season. January-February-November-December stay excellent (8.6-8.8/10). From November to April, it's the tourist high season: 16-29°C, pleasant dry and tempered climate. From May to October, it's the wet + cyclonic season: 41-78% rainfall, real typhoon risk (Taiwan is at the heart of the "Typhoon Alley" north-west Pacific, 4-6 typhoons per season), temperatures 24-31°C. Highlights: Kaohsiung 85 Tower (Tuntex Sky Tower, signature 348m skyscraper of Kaohsiung 1997, in the form of "高" = Chinese character "high" which also forms the 1st character of the name Kaohsiung, panorama observatory 75th floor), Lotus Pond ("lotus pond", lake with 20 colourful Confucian+Buddhist+Taoist temples on its shores + signature Tiger and Dragon Pagodas = entering through the dragon's mouth and exiting through the tiger's brings luck according to tradition, Spring and Autumn Pavilion, Beiji Pavilion), Pier-2 Art Center (former port warehouses reconverted into trendy artistic quarter 2002, street art frescoes, galleries, cosmopolitan restaurants), Liuhe Night Market (the most famous night market in Kaohsiung, signature of Taiwanese night market culture, spicy street food), Cijin Island (island 5 min by ferry from Kaohsiung port, urban beach, seafood, calm insular atmosphere, Cihou Lighthouse 1883), Fo Guang Shan Monastery (most important Taiwanese Buddhist monastery, founded 1967, housing 36m Buddha statue + 8 pagodas), Dome of Light (the largest blown glass public artwork in the world, Formosa Boulevard metro station, signature visual of Kaohsiung), Old British Consulate (19th-century colonial vestige), Sizihwan Bay (panoramic bay at sunset), National Sun Yat-sen University Beach. Excursions: Kenting National Park 2h south ("Taiwanese Hawaii", tropical beaches and corals, the most famous beach national park in Taiwan), Tainan 30 min by train (former capital of Taiwan 1683-1885, historic old town, temples), Sun Moon Lake 3h away (signature alpine lake of Taiwan, already covered), Alishan 4h away (alpine mountains with historic steam train, signature sunrise above the sea of clouds = signature Taiwanese experience), Liuqiu Island (Lambai, coral island 30 min by ferry, diving with green turtles almost guaranteed). Try beef noodle soup (Taiwanese national dish), bubble tea (boba tea, invented in Taiwan 1980 = signature culinary worldwide adopted), oyster omelette, stinky tofu ("fermented tofu" that smells but delicious, signature night market experience), pineapple cake (gourmet souvenir), Taiwan Beer, gao liang sorghum liquor from Kinmen. Kaohsiung International airport (KHH).
quy-nhon:en: Quy Nhon, coastal town of central Vietnam: 290,000 inhabitants in the province of Binh Dinh, 600 km south of Hanoi and 700 km north of Saigon. Long royal capital of the Cham empire (Vijaya 11th-15th century before Vietnamese conquest), remained off the tourist routes until the 2010s. Today in full development, preserved beaches and traditional local life. February is my favourite month (7.9/10): 21-25°C, 40% rain, this is the ideal dry season. From January to April, it's the dry season: 21-29°C. From May to August, expect 26-33°C: intense heat, warm sea (28-29°C), Vietnamese domestic tourist peak (school holidays). From September to December, it's the typhoon season: 53-84% rainfall (the central Vietnam coast is one of the most affected by typhoons in South East Asia). Highlights: Quy Nhon Beach (4 km arc-shaped at the heart of the city, palm trees), Eo Gio ("Headwind", spectacular cliffs at Cape Phuong Mai 25 km away), Ky Co Beach (next to Eo Gio, white sand and turquoise water rivalling the best beaches in Asia), Ham Ho Nature Tourist Area (gorges and rivers in the jungle), Banh It Cham Towers (11th-12th centuries, complex of 4 towers on a hill, one of the best preserved in Vietnam), Duong Long Cham Tower (12th century, 24m the tallest Cham tower), Long Khanh Pagoda (the main Buddhist pagoda of the city), Trung Luong (smart seafront), Cu Lao Xanh ("green island" 24 km offshore, ferry 1h, preserved beaches). Excursions: Tay Son Museum 1h away (memorial museum of Nguyen Hue, peasant rebel leader become emperor Quang Trung who defeated the Chinese 1789, Vietnamese national hero), Hoi An 5h by bus, Nha Trang 4h away, Pleiku Plateau 3h away (Highlands). Try Quy Nhon banh xeo (signature mini Vietnamese shrimp pancake), nem cha (fried rolls), bun ca (fish soup), fresh seafood. Logistics: Phu Cat airport (UIH) 30 min away, direct flights from Hanoi/Saigon in 1h30.
battambang:en: Battambang, 2nd city of Cambodia: 250,000 inhabitants on the Sangker river at the heart of the Cambodian "rice belt". Often neglected by tourists in favour of Siem Reap (Angkor) or Phnom Penh, it is yet the Cambodian city where the French colonial heritage is best preserved (1920s-30s architecture along the river). January-February are my favourite months (6.9/10): 22-34°C, 10-15% rain, this is the ideal dry season. Battambang never tops 6.9/10 (hot tropical climate). From November to March, it's the tourist high season: 22-35°C, dry climate, bearable temperatures in the morning and evening. From March to May, it's the pre-monsoon heatwave: 25-35°C (sometimes 40°C), impossible to visit in mid-day. From May to October, it's the monsoon: 63-89% rainfall, but milder temperatures (24-34°C). Highlights: Bamboo Train ("Norry", bamboo platforms on rusty railway tracks, propelled by an 8 hp engine, speed 30 km/h, 7 km ride through rice paddies and villages, 5$/person, unique world experience), Sampov Lighthouse 12 km away (hill with temple, Killing Caves memorial of the Khmer Rouge massacres 1975-1979), Battambang Bat Cave (near Phnom Sampeau, exit of millions of bats every evening 6pm, 30 min spectacle in continuous black line, free), Wat Banan 25 km away (11th-century Angkorian temple, mini Angkor prefiguration), Wat Kor Temples (on foot in the city). Asia Lighthouse and French colonial architecture of the city centre. Phnom Penh 5h by bus, Siem Reap 4h by bus (Angkor). Try amok (fish curry), beef lok lak, num pang sandwich (French heritage), bia angkor.
papouasie:en: Papua New Guinea (PNG, "Papua New Guinea"), archipelago country of Melanesia north of Australia: 11 million inhabitants on 462,840 km². Country unique in the world for its ethno-cultural diversity = 850 different languages spoken (the most linguistically diverse country in the world, 12% of world languages), 1000+ distinct tribal ethnic groups, Highlanders peoples who had no contact with the outside world before the 1930s-1950s ("first contact" of the Highlands in 1933 by the Leahy brothers = extraordinary documentary expeditions). Country shared between independent 1975 (formerly Australian colony 1949-1975) east of the island of New Guinea + West Papua in the west = territory occupied/annexed by Indonesia 1969 (international controversy, OPM separatist movement repressed). June-July are my favourite months (4.9-5.0/10): 7-14°C, 85-92% rain, cool altitude temperatures for Highlands. PNG never tops 5.0/10 in its Highlands due to humid alpine tropical climate. From June to October, it's the relative dry season "tradewinds" south-east trade winds: 7-15°C Highlands, 24-32°C coasts. From November to May, it's the tropical wet season: 89-96% rainfall, stable temperatures 8-14°C Highlands. Highlights / Regions: Port Moresby capital (on the south coast, modest capital 400,000 inhabitants, high crime, little touristic in itself), Mount Hagen central Highlands ("cultural capital" of the Highlands, access base for signature tribal festivals), Goroka Show (annual cultural festival September at Goroka Highlands, 100+ tribes present their sing-sings = ceremonial tribal dances in spectacular traditional costumes "bilas" feathers-shells-body paintings + ritual masks = signature cultural experience of PNG, one of the largest tribal festivals in the world), Mount Hagen Show (other major festival August), Trobriand Islands (preserved Melanesian archipelago, peoples famous for matrilineal culture and permissive sexual customs documented by anthropologist Bronisław Malinowski 1922 = pioneer modern anthropology), Sepik River (the longest river in PNG 1126 km, cruise between preserved tribal villages with stilt architecture + ritual masks of the spirit house spirit house signature cultural), Kokoda Track (96 km WWII trail through the Highlands, place of the Battle of Kokoda 1942 between Australians and Japanese = major Australian military memory, 8-10 day trek for WWII enthusiasts and experienced adventurers), Rabaul and Tavurvur Volcano on East New Britain Island (active volcano that destroyed Rabaul 1994, post-apocalyptic atmosphere, top world diving on Japanese WWII wrecks in Simpson Harbour). Signature activities: Tufi diving and Madang (top world for fjords + reefs + WWII wrecks), tribal sing-sings (cultural festivals), birds of paradise birds of paradise endemic 39 species unique in the world with spectacular feathers (one of the last great world sites for ornithology), Kokoda Track trekking. Safety: increased France Diplomacy vigilance for PNG = very high urban crime in Port Moresby (rascals = armed gangs), possible tribal tensions in the Highlands, organised travel with experienced local guides mandatory, never travel alone. Try mumu (pork cooked in stone oven-pit signature Melanesian heritage of ceremonial sing-sings), kau kau (sweet potato Highlands food base), saksak (sago palm), grilled fish, betel nut ("buai" traditional chewed stimulant ubiquitous in PNG). Port Moresby Jacksons airport (POM), flights from Brisbane Australia + Cairns + Singapore.
tonga:en: Tonga is the only Polynesian kingdom still monarchical: 100,000 inhabitants, capital Nuku'alofa on Tongatapu. 169 islands of which 36 inhabited. June-July are my favourite months (8.2/10): 21-25°C, 34-36% rain, austral dry season, sea at 25°C. From May to October, it's the dry season: 34-53% rainfall, temperatures 21-27°C. From November to April, it's the wet season: 53-86% rainfall, heavy heat, cyclonic risk (Harold 2020, Gita 2018, Yasa 2020). Tonga is world-known for swimming with humpback whales: July-October, humpback whales migrate from Antarctica to the protected lagoon to give birth and reproduce. It's one of the very rare places in the world where swimming with whales is legal and regulated (group of 4 max + guide, permit required, distance respected). Expect 200-400$/day for this experience (Vava'u, the main archipelago for this activity). Highlights: Tongatapu (capital, Talamahu market, royal palace exterior view), Vava'u (most picturesque archipelago, 50 islands, whales, sailing, diving), Ha'apai (very quiet), 'Eua (hiking). Mapu'a 'a Vaca (spectacular blowholes over 5 km of coast), Ha'amonga 'a Maui Trilithon (Polynesian Stonehenge). Try 'umu (cooking in the earth), lu sipi (mutton in taro leaves). Flight from Auckland (3h) or Fiji.
palau:en: Palau is an archipelago of 340 islands in the north-west Pacific: 18,000 inhabitants, capital Ngerulmud (the least populated in the world). Independent state in free association with the USA since 1994. March is my favourite month (6.4/10): 25-28°C, 68% rain, relative dry season. Palau never tops 6.5/10: constant humid equatorial climate. From February to April, it's the relative dry season: 68-82% rainfall. From May to November, it's the wet season: 86-97% rainfall, heavy heat (25-29°C). No cyclonic season (Palau is outside the zone). Palau is world-renowned for diving: Rock Islands Southern Lagoon (UNESCO, marine landscape of 250 mushroom-shaped limestone islands covered with jungle), Blue Corner (one of the 5 best diving sites in the world, grey sharks in school), German Channel (manta rays), Jellyfish Lake (lake where one can swim among millions of non-stinging jellyfish). Activities: diving (700-1500$ for 4-7 days), kayaking in Rock Islands (day excursion 100-200$). Historic sites: WWII wrecks (Palau was a major battleground, Battle of Peleliu in 1944). Try coconut crab, fish stew, taro. Mandatory flight via Manila, Taipei or Tokyo. High total cost: 4000-6000€ for 8 days all-inclusive diving.
honiara:en: Honiara, capital of the Solomon Islands on the island of Guadalcanal in the south-west Pacific: 85,000 inhabitants. Melanesian archipelagic country of 992 islands, 700,000 inhabitants in total, independent of the United Kingdom since 1978, Commonwealth member. Site of the bloody Battle of Guadalcanal August 1942-February 1943 = first major US ground offensive against the Japanese in the Pacific = turning point of the Pacific War with 7000 US deaths + 31,000 Japanese in 6 months of jungle-sea fighting, US code name "Operation Watchtower". July-August are my favourite months (7.8/10): 24-29°C, 54-59% rain, this is the ideal dry season. May-October stay excellent (7.6-7.8/10). From May to October, it's the relative dry season "Tradewinds" south-east trade winds: 24-29°C, sea at 28°C, this is the tourist high season. From November to April, it's the wet season: 80-88% rainfall, sea sometimes rough. Highlights: Battle of Guadalcanal Memorials (American and Japanese memorials, Mt Austen, Henderson Field = current airport built by Japanese and captured by marines in August 1942 = central stake of the battle, Bloody Ridge = hill where one of the decisive battles of September 1942 took place), Vilu War Museum 30 min from Honiara (private open-air museum with Japanese and US plane wrecks, rusty cannons, overwhelming memorial atmosphere), Iron Bottom Sound ("iron bottom" = sea arm between Guadalcanal and Florida Islands where 50+ American and Japanese ships were sunk in 1942-43 = top world for WWII wreck diving, frequent reef sharks, technical diving), Honiara Central Market (traditional Melanesian atmosphere, fruits-vegetables-fish, blond-redhead naturally curly-haired people endemic to the Solomon Islands = unique world genetic phenomenon of the south-west Pacific Melanesians), National Museum of Solomon Islands, Centrale Hill (panorama over Honiara). Excursions: Florida Islands 1h by boat (Iron Bottom Sound diving), Marovo Lagoon in Western Province (UNESCO Tentative, the largest insular lagoon in the world 700 km² protected by double coral reef, kayaking between islets, preserved traditional Melanesian communities), Rennell Island (UNESCO 1998 = the largest raised coral island in the world, unique endemic biodiversity, Lake Tegano = the largest insular lake in the south Pacific), Tetepare Island (the largest uninhabited island in the south Pacific, community ecotourism), Tikopia and Anuta (very traditional Polynesian outlier islands at the extreme east of the country, populations preserving ancestral Polynesian culture). Safety: Honiara has experienced ethnic riots in 2006 (Chinese quarter looted) and November 2021 (Chinese quarter looted again, anti-Chinese and anti-Prime Minister Sogavare), political tensions with pro-China diplomatic rapprochement 2019 = Western (USA-Australia) concerns about Chinese militarisation of the south-west Pacific. Try kokoda (raw fish in coconut milk signature Melanesian), poi (fermented taro), grilled fish, ceremonial Melanesian kava, local SolBrew beer. Logistics: Honiara airport (HIR), flights from Brisbane Australia 3h, Port Moresby Papua, Nadi Fiji, basic tourist infrastructure.
funafuti:en: Funafuti, capital of Tuvalu in the central Pacific: 6300 inhabitants on the main atoll of Tuvalu (main island + 5 motus of 26 km² total). Tuvalu = 4th smallest country in the world by surface area after Vatican-Monaco-Nauru, 11,000 inhabitants in total on 9 atolls, independence from the United Kingdom 1978, Commonwealth member. Country at the highest world risk of disappearance by sea level rise: average altitude 2m, highest point 4.6m, IPCC forecast = uninhabitable by 2050-2100, active international negotiations to relocate the population to Australia/New Zealand (first agreement with Australia signed 2023 for 280 climate visas per year for Tuvaluans). Tourism near non-existent (1500 tourists/year, among the least visited countries in the world). September is my favourite month (7.7/10): 26-28°C, 73% rain, this is the relative dry season. October-July stay good (6.8-7.6/10). From April to October, it's the dry season "trade wind" trade winds: 27-29°C, sea at 28°C, relatively little rain. From November to March, it's the cyclonic season "cyclone season": 78-84% rainfall, low but existing tropical cyclone risk (cyclone Pam 2015 devastated several motus). Highlights: Atoll of Funafuti (central lagoon of 24 km × 18 km bordered by 33 motus including the main island, lagoon among the most beautiful in the Pacific with shallow turquoise waters), Funafuti Beach (powdery white sand opening directly onto the lagoon or the ocean depending on side), Funafuti Conservation Area (33 km² marine reserve protected since 1996, 6 uninhabited motus, diving and snorkelling with exceptional living reefs), Government Building (modest government palace), Cathedral of Our Lady of the Islands, Vaitupu Marae (traditional Polynesian ceremonial place), Tuvalu National Library and Archives, WWII Memorial (Tuvalu = WWII theatre, American forces occupied Funafuti 1942-1945 as an air base to push back the Japanese in the Pacific). Activities: diving and snorkelling in the lagoon (exceptional living reefs, manta rays, reef sharks), traditional fishing with Tuvaluan fishermen, atoll tour by boat to visit the uninhabited motus (paradise white sand), cycling all around Funafuti island (12 km, single road), marine bird watching (frigates, terns). Cultural particularities: Tuvalu .tv domain (the country earns 5 million USD/year by leasing its ".tv" internet domain name highly demanded by television channels = strategic source of income for the micro-state), Te Ika Tuvalu ("the Tuvaluan fishermen", decorative Tuvaluan currency not used daily, the Australian dollar is the official currency), Polynesian (Tuvaluan language related to Samoan-Tongan), Fete Te Maeva ("independence day" 1 October, signature traditional Tuvaluan dances = "fatele" = polyrhythmic chants with percussion on wooden box). Try palu sami (raw fish in coconut milk), pulaka (local starch close to taro), umu (cooking in underground stone oven Polynesian), grilled fish, fresh coconut milk. Logistics: Funafuti International airport (FUN), unique Fiji Airways flight 3x weekly from Suva Fiji 2h30, one of the most difficult airports in the world to access, runway used as public sports field when no plane (rugby, football, children's games — withdrawn when the plane arrives). Cost: Tuvalu extremely expensive (200-300 USD/day minimum), few accommodations (Vaiaku Lagi Hotel the only international hotel, 2 guesthouses).
